Some of your improvements come from dramatic leaps caused by a new insight or mode of thinking, but in truth, most of our improvements are small, incremental improvements that accrue over time to produce dramatic results out in the future rather then here and now. Look for every little mechanical and tactical improvement you can. One small improvement may not make much difference, but a dozen accumulated over a year can make a very substantial difference to your performance with a favorite move.

What pace should you spar at? When you look at the great champions of Jiu jitsu you will see that some moves at a very fast pace, whilst others moved in a very pedestrian fashion. Many people think pace is the secret to exhausting opponents - so the faster the better. A moments thought however, reveals that very different types of pace are equally effective. Dorian Olivarez regularly exhausts even much bigger opponents with his speed, but Gordon Ryan also exhausted his opponents while barely moving. What really exhausts opponents is making them work in an inefficient manner for extended periods of time. When they are forced to carry bodyweight from mechanically broken stances, or forced to follow fast movement from positions where they can’t catch up - that’s what exhausts people. Different opponents move and hold stance in different ways. Some will be easier to exhaust via speed, some via weight, some via tension. We all favor one method. If you’re light and fast, speed makes a lot more sense as a method, if you’re slow yourself, weight placement makes more sense. Play around with each. Make sure you adapt their use to the opponent, and soon you’ll be able to use pace, or lack of, as a weapon

We don’t get many certainties in grappling but one certainty is that nearly every grappling exchange you will ever enter will begin with establishing a grip on your opponent. The better that grip, the better the opening of the exchange will go for you, and, like most things in life, a better start makes it easy to get to a satisfactory finish. Don’t go into your opening grips thoughtlessly. Have a clear sense of what you want to do and what grip will be required to get it done. Learn to fake an entry into one grip in order to attain the grip you really wanted. Learn how to neutralize an opponents grip. In general it’s a little safer to nullify an opponents grip first, before asserting your own. Good grip strength is desirable but not essential. Much more important is the endurance and placement of your grips. Grip training is often neglected because most people learn Jiu jitsu in terms of set moves and a grip in merely a set up to a move, so they tend to hurried over or even ignored, but this is a terrible mistake, as the success or failure of the move often comes down to your ability to impose a dominant grip prior to its execution

Don’t despair if techniques you’re working on don’t feel perfect. Keep two things in mind - first, almost everyone looks and feels like a klutz when they start on a new skill. How you start isn’t important - it’s how good you are when you finish that you’ll be remembered for. As long as you’re making progress everything is ok. Give yourself time and that progress will accrue into a really powerful skill. Second, your moves don’t have to be perfect to succeed. They only have to be good enough to exceed the defensive capabilities of your opponent at the time of application. Very often a far from perfect execution will be enough to do the job. So don’t be intimidated by a rocky start to skill acquisition. Time and progressive effort will work its magic and soon enough that seemingly hopeless beginning may well furnish one of your strongest techniques

When you’re hunting for submissions it’s never just about the pin - it’s about the pin AND getting your opponents limbs extended out and away from the torso. Pins are for points - but if it’s control to submission you seek - go the extra distance and get your opponents extended. That’s when pins start to convert into submission holds.

Jiu jitsu has a way of rewarding effort and showing indifference to sloth. The more you bring into a training session, the more you leave with. Enter the gym with an agenda of what you want to accomplish and you’ll leave with invaluable data that will either confirm progress towards your goals, or make you revise your beliefs in ways that enable you to adapt and change course towards your goals. In both cases you’ll be better for it. Show up with nothing and you’ll leave with a nothing more than a sweat. The greatest power you have is your self determination to work steadfastly and intelligently towards whatever goals you’ve deemed important - the more you make use of that, the faster you’ll progress.

Whenever you have a clear sense of what you want to achieve, the answers to questions come easier. In a confusing and uncertain environment, clarity of purpose is your biggest asset. After that it’s just a matter of time spent in experimentation and fitting solutions to puzzles.

There is no substitute for taking a keen interest in whatever field you wish to excel in. If you aren’t fascinated by what you’re doing there is little chance of you doing anything remarkable in that field. There are a thousand ways to get good at what you’re doing, but if you aren’t deeply interested in the endeavor you’ll never persist long enough with any of them to attain a high level. The more you study, question and experiment - the more interesting everything becomes - especially when done around like minded people.

They say the first step to recovery from a problem is to recognize that there is a problem - Jiu jitsu is no exception. The first step to deciding where to allocate your training resources next is a thorough and accurate inventory of your current skill set. Analyzing where you win and where you lose, what comes easily and what feels difficult. Where there are problems - that’s where you start doing the bulk of your work. It’s easy to maintain your other established skills with a reduced workload while focusing on the areas that need more attention. Make sure that thorough going self analysis is a fairly regular part of your training. The better you know yourself, the better you can allocate time and effort where it’s needed most

Knowing what you want to accomplish in a workout is one of the great determinants of how beneficial that workout will be. Those who come in with nothing typically leave with nothing. Those who come in with a goal and plan to enact it come out with either success or a better idea of how to accomplish it tomorrow

I’m sure you’ve all had the experience of trying a new move and thinking to yourself “there’s absolutely no way that move will ever work effectively for me!” Have you ever had the opposite experience - where the first time you tried a move it seemed strangely natural and easy, at least relative to other moves? Your first impressions are usually quite accurate. We have a natural affinity towards certain moves. Recognizing and taking advantage of that affinity is an important part of constructing your game over time. When ever you feel that natural affinity make sure you invest some time in that move - given more time and experimentation, that promising introduction may lead to a signature move in your arsenal

Precision is a force multiplier in Jiu jitsu. In a game where a small adjustment can have dramatic effects upon performance, it pays to learn details that make a difference. There will be a trade off between your desire to implement the details that make a difference to performance and your need to initiate the move in a timely manner. There comes a point where if you spend too much time fixating on details you’ll be too late in the application of the move, but if you rush before the details are satisfactorily employed you’ll get suboptimal results. You must learn to find a balance between these two demands.

Know what you’re dealing with: When most people engage in Jiu jitsu they have a pretty clear plan of action as to what they want to do to their opponent. However, very few pay any real attention to the person to whom that want to enact that plan of action. It’s usually a good idea to do some basic reading of your opponent early in the match to try to get a sense of what they are looking to do and their capability to enact it. Rather than just try to impose your preconceived idea of what you wanted to do, read your opponents abilities and then make an assessment as to what would work best in such a person. Jiu jitsu usually works better when you take the other guy into account rather than just go with what you wanted.

Your vision is set up by nature to have an area of focus and this is surrounded by peripheral vision, which lacks focus but gains in breadth of vision. Your peripheral vision is not designed to give precise scrutiny, but rather to see just enough to convey important information such as a threat or opportunity out of your primary focus. Your vision should work the same way in Jiu jitsu as it does in nature. You’ll be focused upon your current move, looking for the information required to make it work, but keep your peripheral vision active as well, you’ll see looking threats and fleeting opportunities outside of the primary move you’re engaged in - and this information may prove vital. Don’t put one hundred percent of your attention into your current focus - leave enough to see emerging threats and opportunities and your game will improve immediately

Learning a move well will definitely help your game, but learning move linked to other moves in sequences that follow the lines of resistance you can expect from a skilled opponent will do a great deal more. It’s not moves that win matches at the highest levels - it’s embedded moves that work in conjunction with others to break through stout resistance.

The game of Jiu jitsu is the game of control that leads to submission. The single biggest means of undermining that control is movement. When locking an opponent up for a submission, focussed first on slowing or even better, stopping his movement. It’s difficult to finish most submission holds, particularly joint locks in the midst of movement. Take the time to at least slow movement down before you lock up to finish.

I am very happy to announce the release of my latest instructional video MASTER THE MOVE: THE BUTTERFLY GUARD SWEEP SUMI GAESHI. When began Jiu jitsu one of my main mentors, Rodrigo Gracie, had a great butterfly sweep. I was greatly impressed by its effectiveness. At that early time in the US BJJ scene, guard play was mostly closed guard. Seeing someone initiate attacks very effectively from a seated open guard rather than passively waiting for opportunities from closed guard made a big impression on me. Moreover, sumi gaeshi is based upon inside position as such it combine extremely well with many aspects of the leg lock game. Every defensive reaction to it involves extending and posting limbs - which leads directly to submission attacks. As such I made it a centerpiece of my coaching. It creates a powerful dilemma between positional threats via sweeps and submission threats when they defend those sweeps. You will often see my students start with sumi gaeshi and then build their attacks from there as their opponents are forced into predictable defensive reaction to that strong initial threat. It is equally effective on the ground and standing, gi and no gi. All of my students had great success with it, as did many other outstanding athletes over the history of Jiu Jitsu. If you want a powerful attack from guard position that takes the attacking momentum in your favor every time - you can’t do better than learning sumi gaeshi the butterfly sweep in depth!! Link in my Instagram story

Whenever you add something major to your game there has to be a period where you forsake your previous skills in order to focus on the new skill. Given our limited attention spans, training time, energy levels etc this inevitably means that the more we focus on the acquisition of a new skill, the less we focus on expanding or even maintaining existing skills. It’s natural to worry that your tried and true skills will suffer or even be lost in the quest for new skills. There is some truth to this. Skills are perishable. If you don’t work on them they diminish. So by switching your attention to new skills, yes, this will come at the price of some diminishing of your existing skills. Don’t be concerned however. It is much easier to re-learn skills than to learn them from scratch. So if there is some diminishing of your previous skills set as you pursue new skills, ready assured that some periodization of focus in the future will quickly bring them back - and you’ll have the new skills as well! That’s the normal pattern of progress

Slowing things down - speeding things up: Learning to control pace is a big part of success in sparring. As a general rule, when you are in control, slow things down. If you’re winning, staying winning longer by slowing the action down so that your opponent must bear the brunt of the work. When neither of you is in control it’s often a good idea to speed things up as increased pace can get you past many defenses. Bear in mind bower that increases in pace will cost you in terms of energy expenditure, so be judicious in your use of fast pacing. If you haven’t got a positive result from faster pace within a suitable time frame, slow down and save energy. Play with pace as you spar. You’ll soon find that variations in pace can bring dramatic effects to outcomes.

Focus: The ability to concentrate your powers upon a task is one of the great determinants of your progress. There are a thousand things inside and outside the gym pulling your attention in every direction. It’s the people that can keep focused on goals and tasks that prevail. There’s a lot of things in our lives we have little to no control over - but your focus is not one of them - it’s one of the few things that’s totally under your control - use it.

When you enact a move on a forceful and decisive fashion, it immediately throws your opponent into defensive reactions. Most of these reactions are quite predictable, since only a few options make mechanical sense as a reaction to the initial move. This creates a simple means of closing down your opponent’s options at any time in the match in a way that enables you reliably predict the next move. If you continue this cycle the options for the opponent get fewer still - and that is when victory is at hand. Never just perform a move. Perform a move with your focus on the next in response to anticipated reactions. That’s the real skill of the game

Learn to see past the current move: Jiu jitsu is a very engaging sport. There is so much resistance to whatever move you’re currently attempting that it’s easy to get so caught up in the execution of that move that you can think of nothing else. You must teach yourself to see past that move into the next two or three moves. In a world where the majority of moves fail against resistance, victory usually goes to the person who sees the next moves even as they are executing the first, so that if the resistance proves too strong, they immediately go into the next move. Myopia is a fault to be corrected in life and also in Jiu jitsu - see past this move and have a plan for the next and you’ll bypass resistance more often than not.

Once you’ve decided that the application of a given move has an acceptably high probability of success - that’s the time to go in with full commitment. Most of the time it’s good to play with a relaxed body, but when it’s time to score or submit and you believe you have a real chance of doing so - this is not the time for half heartedness - go in with real commitment. Even the most well applied move has a minimum horsepower rating in order to be effective against a tough resisting opponent - make sure you exceed that level.

Keep developing your conceptual understanding of a skill and the context around it. There’s always some nuance that were it revealed to you whether under the pressure of live training, experimentation or advice from a peer or coach, could provide immediate performance benefits. When depth of understanding meets realistic live training they form a synergy that creates growth that will carry you to your goals

Developing finesse: When it’s time to learn a move, your first goal is basic function - can you apply the move successfully against resistance where success is usually measured with a score, preventing a score, escaping a bad position or submission or some other form of success? Once you’re functionally capable, the goal is to go further and sharpen the move to a level you you can do more than just be functionally effective with it - the goal is to express it with finesse. This is important because finesse almost always demonstrates more than style - it demonstrates efficiency. When you can perform a move with ease and grace it’s clear that you are not using strength get the move to work. Remember the goal is not just to make the move work, the goal is to make it work with maximum efficiency. This preserves strength and helps you last longer in a tough match so that you can exhaust opponents without exhausting yourself first. Don’t be satisfied with function alone. See function as the big first step in learning, but always go further and look to express that functionality with finesse

Breathing is among the most basic operations of the human body. We do it all day every day and it is so automatic we barely think about for years at a time - yet the second our breathing becomes labored or difficult it’s the immediately the ONLY thing we think about. Any time you are involved in stressful physical activity, breathing becomes even more important than usual. Your ability to complete an arduous task is heavily bound up with your ability to regulate your breathing throughout that task until completion. Typically when we are sparring in Jiu jitsu, our mind is focused upon the performance of technical skills - but never forget that breathing is itself a skill. In ordinary circumstances it’s a skill so basic we largely ignore it - but under heavy physical pressure it’s a skill so important that if you lose control over it, no other skill will make up for it. Sometimes when you’re sparring, particularly against a very evenly matched partner where you know it’s gong to be a hard physical battle - set some time aside to practice breathing as a skill. Learn to slow action and use breathing to stave off any encroaching oxygen debt. Experiment with different depths and cadences of breathing and learn how to recharge your batteries in the midst of action. It may not be the usual type of skill you work on, but it’s a truly important skill nonetheless

Strength is only as useful as your capacity to organize it effectively so that it supports effective action. Organized lesser strength will always defeat disorganized greater raw strength. Getting your movement organized into coherent action is central motivation for training. Regardless of your mode of training, engage with your skill development with this mindset and you’ll go far

Learning new moves vs refining existing moves: We all want to make progress as quickly as possible. Part of that progress is learning new moves. Over time this becomes essential. Be aware however, that if you have only a short amount of time in which to make progress, it’s often wise to focus on refining moves you already employ and use well, rather than try to learn an entirely new move or set of moves. Starting anything from the ground up is always innately more difficult and time consuming than modifying or adding to something already established - Jiu jitsu is no exception. You’d be amazed how much you can add to one of your favorite moves just by adding a new set up, or combining it with another move in a new combination. Simple additions like these can often get nearly as much performance improvement for your overall game as entirely new moves at a fraction of the cost of time and effort. If you’re looking for maximum “bang for the buck” in upgrading your game, start by refining or adding to what you already have.

Keep making projects for yourself: Everything you learn in Jiu jitsu takes time. Typically you start every skill you learn in a state of incompetence and work towards competence and then onward on the long path to mastery. Look at each one of them as a project. The more effort and thought you out into each project, the better the results. You can postpone a project any time to switch to something else, don’t worry, you can always return to it later. At any given time you should have a small set of projects that you’re working on - too many and nothing will be done on any of them. You should know exactly what are and how far you aim to take them. The worst times for training progress in my career were always times when I had no projects I was working on due to distractions etc. Take time to continuously find and start projects. Just a few at a time. If they prove to be a dead end street - abandon them. If they show promise, keep going

When did you feel like you started to develop that was distinctively yours? Self expression through technique is a big part of your development over the years. We each carry a unique body and personality when we enter the game - that’s certainly a big factor in the development of a personal game, but there are other important factors too. Was it a mentor or role model that influenced you a certain way? Perhaps a formative experience that helped shape your game? The emergence of a trend across the sport that you followed? Did you realize yourself that your game was crystallizing in a certain way? Or did your colleagues tell you that you were getting good in certain areas and this made you introspect and agree with them? I think all of those elements appear and play a role in our development over the course of years. Typically the first stages appear organically, but once you recognize them, you tend to reinforce them deliberately.

It’s not the act of getting to a position of advantage that’s so tough, it’s being able to maintain and then use it profitably that’s the hard part. The highest rewards come from what you can do with the advantages you’ve created. Getting a good position is really just the start of the story. Make sure you put plenty of focus on how you use it as well.

It’s normal to worry that other people around you are making faster progress than you are. Some people appear so talented and always seem to learn faster than you. You feel the effect of their improvement every time you train but you rarely feel the effect of your own improvements. Don’t worry. The rate of your progress is almost never as decisive in determining your final level in Jiu jitsu as the training time you accumulate while still making acceptable progress. Imagine you had a truly exceptional team mate who made incredible learning progress more than twice as fast as you, but who burned out after two years of training, while you stayed the course and made average progress for six years. You will find yourself at a higher level than he ever achieved. Rate of progress is important, but even more so is the time you spend in a positive state of progress. Patience has won many races that seemed lost. The only thing that is unacceptable is zero progress - but that is rare. As long as you’re making satisfactory progress, the amount of time you spend in the activity will usually prove more important than how fast you’re progressing over a shorter period. So don’t worry too much about the people around you, focussed more on a sustainable program that will keep you engaged at a satisfactory rate for the long term

You’re in a sport where a single pertinent additional detail can make a real difference to performance of a skill almost immediately. Most often you need to experience a problem as a motivation to hunt for such details as a solution to that problem - sometimes you see that detail being taught and it just makes intuitive sense, either way, it can be a shortcut to better performance. So stay inquisitive and open minded, and of course, ready to test any new ideas under realistic sparring conditions - this will help ensure you keep progressing towards your grappling goals.

Grip: Every grappling engagement begins with grip. How you grip your opponent will affect the rest of that engagement. When you grip with power and authority it will immediately throw your opponent into a cautious and defensive demeanor. When you grip naively and weakly you’re setting yourself up to be immediately countered. Your grip must be mated to your purpose. Have an idea of what you want to accomplish and grip accordingly. Just coming and putting hands on opponents with no sense of purpose will be disastrous against a good opponent. Know what you want to do and know how best to grip to achieve that aim - follow this approach and the opening of every engagement will generally go your way

Come into a workout with a goal and a plan and you’ll leave with a result. It doesn’t matter whether it’s a good or bad result, either way it’s feedback for tomorrow’s session. Come into a workout with no idea what you’re doing and the session will however good as fate determines and it will have no relevance to tomorrow. One approach leads to certain progress over time, the other to frivolous, happy go lucky outcomes that are forgotten the moment you walk out the door

Every good business devotes time to periodically assess its inventory. You have to do the same yourself, only you’ll be doing an assessment of your skill inventory. Athletes who have a strong sense of what they’re good at, what they’re not good at and what they need to work on, typically do better over time than athletes who just randomly pile up skills over time. The ancient Greeks said it best - KNOW THYSELF. That’s the first step to continual progress.

When it’s time to solve problems - staying calm is half the battle - Jiu jitsu is no exception. No one ever became a better problem solver by working more frantically or emotionally. Stay focused on the task, but not so focused that you overlook potential solutions by being too narrow in your survey of possibilities. Make sure the task takes precedence over any else you might be feeling at the time. The more task focused you become, the less room you give for destructive emotions and feelings to take your attention away from what needs to be done

What’s next? Jiu jitsu is a game with very clearly specified goals - to pass an opponents guard, to get to a scoring position, to take an opponent down etc etc which we generally try to achieve via moves, yet the moves are subsidiary to the goals, but we are more likely to achieve those goals when we execute those moves well. This tricky relationship between goals and moves is always a difficult path to navigate in training. The only certainty is that you’ll face resistance every time you go to apply a move or fight towards a goal. As such, most of your attempts will fail in the face of that resistance. It’s vital then, that you always have a clear sense of what you will do next. If most attempts are bound to fail, have a strong next option, whether it be an adaptation or a completely different move, is essential to bypass resistance. Train your mind to think ahead, but not so far that you don’t apply the first moves forcefully, but enough that the first line of resistance doesn’t stop you in your tracks.

A study of Jiu jitsu is essentially a study of the strengths and weaknesses of the human body. The opponents body is the target of all our attacks and our own body is the means by which we make those attacks. We want to use the strengths of our own body to attack the weaknesses of our opponents body in order to maximize our chances. On a superficial level there are obvious and important facts about the body that initially guide us - the human body is well adapted to deal with threats from the front, but not the back. The upper body is much weaker than the lower body. One side is always (almost always) stronger and better coordinated than the other. As your study deepens you will uncover myriad other strengths and weaknesses more subtle than these. All of them are valuable to your study of Jiu jitsu.

You can always extracting more from a training session by having a clear sense of what you want to accomplish and a plan for how you will create the conditions necessary to make those accomplishments happen. If you fail to accomplish those goals, or do so in a way that is unsatisfactory, then you will need a post workout analysis that makes recommendations for the subsequent training session. If you show up to a workout with nothing, it’s very likely you’ll leave with nothing. But if you show up with a goal and a plan, you’ll either succeed or, should you fail, you have data that will help you succeed tomorrow - either way it’s a much better result that arriving and leaving empty handed

What was the first submission hold you ever finished when you began jiu jitsu? Why do you think it was that particular submission hold? Was it heavily emphasized in your coach’s program? Was it random? Was it just something that was available at that moment? Perhaps due to an oversight by your opponent? Was it a formative and memorable experience? I can clearly remember my first real submission success - an arm bar from guard. I remember it mostly because it was surprising. I was an atrocious student when I began, with no aptitude for submissions, so I just got on top of people and squeezed them 😂😂 It wasn’t until I got reprimanded by my long suffering teacher, Renzo Gracie, who admonished me for never playing bottom position and attempting real moves, that finally I started trying genuine submissions. When I got one to work it was truly a formative moment. In a second, I learned the move worked and that I could do it if I tried hard over time. Getting some early success is a big part of what makes us focus on a particular move or area of Jiu jitsu.

The most fundamental and obvious form of control in Jiu jitsu is control over movement. When you first begin you have to learn to control the movement of your own body, but soon your focus will switch to the real challenge - controlling the movement of a skilled opponents body. The better your grips and the better your positioning the easier this will be. When you get to a good position take some time to practice the skill of restricting movement. When you feel you are becoming good at this, work to the next level, which is to DIRECT an opponent’s movement by allowing a pathway to open - a pathway that you control. This is the kind of control you want to build towards

Your skills are highly perishable. All the hard work you’ve done to accrue them can be undone over time with neglect. The good news is that if you’ve embodied them well they can be regained much more quickly than you originally learned them. More importantly they can be maintained much more easily than learned - so if you’re pressed for training - don’t despair - you can maintain your level even with a greatly reduced training schedule. There is a big difference between less training and no training.

How well you react in a bad situation will be a direct reflection of the amount of time you’ve spent there previously and how much knowledge and skill that time allowed you to accrue. Training offense is fun, but training defense is a necessity, make sure your training program reflects this.

The game of Jiu jitsu swings constantly between offense and defense. A great sign of developing maturity and sophistication in your game is the narrowing of the two so that defensive responsibility is built into all your offense and offensive possibilities are built into all of your defense. The more you can merge the two, the better your game will become. Typically you practice them as separate facets of the game, but in actuality you will need to draw them close together

Many athletes are able to give one hundred percent of their effort in hard physical training and go to the point of exhaustion; but far fewer are willing to do the same with mental effort. As the saying goes, the hardest work is hard thinking. As necessary and beneficial as hard physical work is, its effect is greatly curtailed if you’re applying it in the wrong directions. Put time into your thought processes before and after training to check to see if that hard physical work is being applied in an efficient manner. Ask questions about the direction and efficacy of your work. Hard work is impressive but it’s well directed hard work that makes champions and that means taking some time to periodically think hard about the directions that work is taking you

How fast should I be going in training? That’s a great question. Obviously you want to move quickly enough that you can keep abreast of your opponents attempts at offense. You can’t allow a situation where you are moving so slowly that an opponent has an automatic speed advantage that you are always playing catch up. More importantly however, you want to move at a speed that doesn’t exceed your ability to furnish sound answers to the problems you’re trying to overcome as you spar your opponent. Sometimes this will be faster, sometimes slower, it will depend how fast the other guy is moving and how quickly you can perceive and act upon information around you. Learning to find this pace is a big part of your development in sparring.

The big Fall sale at BJJ Fanatics has started! All my instructional videos 49% off for next few days!

Frustration and longevity: A vital key to Jiu jitsu progress is a long term mindset. We all have expectations about where we want to be and often these clash with what actually transpires in training. Jiu jitsu generally rewards the patient and punishes the overly hasty. This doesn’t mean slacking off in training - it’s means tempering your expectations as to what you can expect out of the input you’ve put into training. In many cases you can definitely reduce the time required to get to a given level with good training protocols, but there is a certain amount of time and effort required regardless of how good your training methods may be or how passionately you engage with the sport. Keeping your expectations realistic a big part of your longevity because when there is misalignment between expectations and reality - this is the most common source of frustration and frustration is among the most common causes of quitting the game. Keep your expectations high - that’s crucial in the quest for excellence, but balance them with realism as to how quickly these will be attained and use them to elevate performance rather than plague you with feelings of frustration. As long as you’re participating you’ve got the chance to reach your potential, so don’t create conditions that might end your participation completely. At the end of the day you’ll be remembered much more for the height you reached rather than how long it took to get there.

Jiu jitsu is largely a game of problem solving under pressure. I can’t think of a single instance where our ability to solve problems is enhanced by getting more emotional or agitated. As such, it’s important to be able to keep your mental faculties in check as you train. Keeping yourself in an analytical mindset is a big help in avoiding the traps of emotional responses to problems as you’re sparring or competing. When the intensity of a match increases and things are heating up quickly, keep your mind cold and analytical - you’ll need it to help solve the second by second problems you’ll need to solve to determine winner and loser

In a game where the difference between success and failure can often be measured in inches and seconds, attention to detail will always be important. However, make sure you import and learn those details at a rate that you can manifest successfully in live sparring. Remember that time is a factor in application. If you’re delaying action in order to accommodate some aspect of mechanical detail - that delay might confound you as surely as inattention to relevant details. Finding a satisfactory balance between the need for precision and the need for timely execution is a huge part of your developing effectiveness on the mat

Give yourself some time: This is a game where the acquisition of skills is the single biggest key to your success or failure. Some skills come easier, some are more challenging - but in either case there will some period of time and some degree of effort required before the skill becomes robust enough to be a reliable means of scoring for you. So often people begin work on a skill and move on after some initial failures or difficulties. Give yourself some time. Instead of seeing failure as a reason to shift to another skill, use failure as feedback for another try tomorrow. I’ve seen athletes repeatedly fail with a technique but persevere and a year later be recognized as an expert with that move when they win a major tournament with it. Early failures should be expected. It’s not what happens when you first start attempting a skill that you’ll be remembered for - but rather what you do with that skill after you’ve spent months and years refining it

Possessing a strong defensive game is good. Possessing a strong offensive game is good. But being able to merge offense and defense seamlessly together is best of all. The better your opponents the more you’ll need to integrate offense and defense rather than see them as separate parts of your game. Strive always to manifest defense into all your offense and offense into all your defense and you’ll do very well in this game

The first line of defense is always awareness. Once a move has been applied upon you, you’ll need precise physical methods of defending it, but before it’s applied, awareness of the danger and subsequent positioning to avoid or negate that danger is 90% of the game. As they say in medicine, announce if prevention is worth a pound of cure - so too in defense. An ounce of awareness of potential danger is worth a pound of late escapes.

Experience teaches us that the majority of moves we will attempt against an opponent of similar size and skill level will fail. This realization can make you hang back from attacking or de-emphasize your first move in the expectation that it will probably fail, so focus on the second move. You can’t think this way. Even when you’re pessimistic about the chances of your first move succeeding, you have to go in with serious intent. Half hearted attempts at a move get strongly countered by a good opponent. You have to go in with sufficient commitment to elicit a strong defensive reaction and prevent giving up an easy counter. In addition that second attack of yours is only likely to succeed if there is a reaction to the first. So even in a tight match where most of your attacks are running into a brick wall - go in with commitment to the first attack to make that second one succeed

This is a game where every good move you perform is proportionately bad for your opponent, and every bad move proportionately good for your opponent. Therefore as your understanding of an offensive move grows, by extension you’ll understand more about its defense, since doing the opposite of what your opponent wants to do will generally result in the shutting down of the move. Deepening your understanding of one aspect of the game has positive ripple effects on other aspects of the game - particularly in the opposite direction of whatever you are learning about.

One of the great features of Jiu jitsu is the many choices it offers in the performance of a given task. What works well for one person may not be so suitable for another. That’s fine - the plethora of choices means that there’s a method for everyone. In time you’ll find the ones that work best for you. Bear in mind however, that a plethora of choices can lead to indecision and confusion - so make a few solid choices for each of the major areas of the game and work on them diligently, rather than making a new choice every month so that you never spend the time required to attain a high skill level. This will give you a unique game well suited to your body type and personality and also let you spend long enough with your choices to develop real expertise with them

As they say - organisms that learn by trial and error usually lose to organisms that learn by other peoples trial and error.

Garry Tonon ties the knot with his lovely wife Amanda! Part of the old NYC crew got together at Mr Tonons wedding here in Austin. Two of my favorite friends from the basement - Brian Glick and Jamie Crowder came down from the Apple to see the big occasion! Congratulations Garry and Amanda!

Every match starts with you in front of your opponent, but real advantage comes from being behind your opponent. Make sure always that the skill of getting behind your opponent’s elbows from any neutral position - top, bottom, left, right, standing or ground is a very high priority in your training regimen.

Don’t be despondent when a skill you’re working on doesn’t come easy. If skills were easily learned they’d have no value. What makes them precious is the fact that most people give up on learning early out of frustration. Be happy that it’s a hard path to skill acquisition. That means when you finally get there, you’ll be the only one in the room with the abilities that skill makes possible.

Tension and relaxation: Jiu Jitsu is a game that often requires maximal isometric tension to get a task completed. Submission holds are an obvious example. Lack of sufficient tightness in the lock is a common source of failure. However, maximal isometric tension comes at a heavy price in terms of fatigue. Some tasks are best performed in a state of relative relaxation. This allows for greater fluidity and endurance. Learning to correlate your tension level to the task at hand is crucial not only for success in the performance of whatever move you’ve allotted to the task, also to the match overall, because once you’ve pushed too hard on the gas for too long, it’s usually very difficult to recover inside the timeframe of a match. When you’re sparring make sure you’ve got your attention focused on more than your opponent. Keep an awareness of your own energy consumption relative to your opponent overall and also in the performance of the immediate task at hand - this is one of the most important determinants of outcome.

Ultimately your success and failure as both an athlete or a coach will be determined not by what you know, but by how you think. The methodology behind your thinking will overcome any current shortcomings in knowledge, but no amount of knowledge can make up for failures in your methodology of thinking.

Adding an entirely new technique to your repertoire is time consuming and difficult work. Refining or adding to an already existing technique in your repertoire is relatively easy. When you’re looking to add some new elements to your game in a hurry - refining and adding to what you already do well is the way to go. There will be times you need to make totally new additions, but if you’re looking for quick additions - refinement beats novelty

Victory in Las Vegas! Teenage phenom Helena Crevar @helenajiujitsugirl won gold tonight here in Las Vegas with a very hard fought submission victory against her extremely talented rival, Sarah Galvao - daughter of the great Andre Galvao. Both athletes came out in excellent form, Ms Galvao had won brilliantly against the current ADCC Champion the night before and Ms Crevar had won a razor close decision against the great ADCC double gold medalist Adele Fornarino. It’s quite remarkable to think that these two teenage phenoms were battling for the biggest prize money in female Jiu jitsu history - a very bright moment for the future of the game. After a very intense first two rounds, Helena was able to lock in one of her devastating trademark leg locks and take a brilliant victory. In the men’s division our team fought on the tough side of the brackets against the great ATOS team, probably the most favored team in the event, packed with ADCC champions and medalists. We prevailed after an outstanding team performance and went into the finals. There we won 3 out of five matches, but in a rather perplexing final decision the teams were judged as a draw because one of the B team’s victories was judged a 10-8 round, apparently disregarding one of our athletes victories which was almost identically dominant but judged only 10-9. This meant a technicality was introduced whereby the team who won the last match was adjudicated the overall winner - so despite winning more matches we lost. We talked briefly with the judges over the apparent inconsistencies in scoring but it did not shed any light on the situation. Congratulations to all the teams who competed. There were many outstanding individual performances and the teams all showcased excellent use of technique and strategy. I am very proud of what our team did and would like to thank @giancarlobodoni @luke_griffithjj and @teamolivarez who had a particularly brilliant tournament and really showcased his potential. In addition I must say it was truly a great honor to work alongside @micagalvaojj @melquigalvaojj and @vagnerrochabjj and train with @diogoreisbjj Thank you all!

I was extremely impressed by these young Brazilian super stars and feel very confident they will greatly elevate this sport out into the future. See you all soon!

When you HAVE control - USE control: If you manage to work your way into a controlling position, this is not the time to rush things. The great advantage of controlling position is that it affords you TIME - time to work and further solidify the advantage you’ve gained. If you suddenly gained $100 would just immediately frivolously gamble it away? Of course not - you consolidate it into your savings and use it advantageously in the future. So too with positional control. You worked hard to get - be judicious in how you use it.

When you know what you’re trying to achieve the adaptation of technique to circumstance takes care of itself. The Ancient Greeks implored us “know thyself.” In Jiu jitsu “know what you’re trying to accomplish.”

Lever and fulcrum: Insofar as Jiu jitsu is based upon the laws of physics and biomechanics, it has an objective side to it where answers to problems can be clearly correct or incorrect. Insofar as Jiu jitsu is played by human agents with great variation of body type and build and under chaotic conditions, the actual use of those objective mechanical laws will be subject to a great amount of subjective adaptation, adjustment and choice. Physics gives Jiu jitsu its structure, choice and interpretation give it its soul.

How good you are at a given move in real sparring is not so much a reflection of how good you are at that particular move, but rather how good you are at all the contextual moves that out you into a position to perform that particular move. Excellence in a single move would be meaningless in a real situation, not is it really possible in actuality because you need all the contextual moves to reliably get you into situations where you can actually perform the move in sufficient numbers to achieve excellence in the first place. Don’t just work the move - work the context as well.

One of the greatest attributes you can ever develop to further your progress is a strong sense of curiosity. This will do more to keep you on a path of continuous learning than anything else. Taking a genuine interest in the workings of the game;the what’s, why’s, when and how’s will keep you researching for a lifetime. Over time it will create a level of understanding that greatly improves your performance when allied with realistic training.

Opportunity for attack is always directly in front of you - it’s up to you to be able to identify them and act on them. Good athletes “see” more when looking at the same thing - great athletes do likewise but pair it with the confidence to pull the trigger on demand.

Any time you focus on new skills it must be at the expense of older skills. Skills do diminish without use so it’s rather sad to think that as we try to expand our skills set in new directions, our older game must suffer to some degree. Don’t worry, though it’s true that skills diminish without regular use, it’s equally true to that it’s far easier to re-learn a skill than to learn it ground up. So don’t despair about your established skills diminishing slightly as you pursue new ones. If you ever need to sharpen them it’s usually a fairly quick and easy process. Knowing This gives you the confidence to spend time pursuing new skills

One of the greatest weapons of the various grappling arts is smothering pressure. This lets you use time as a weapon. If you’re thinking in the middle of a match “when will this be over?” Typically the outcome will not be good for you. If you can make the other fellow have the same thought, typically the outcome will not be good for him. When you have an opportunity to apply heavy pressure - take it. It won’t have immediate effects on a tough opponent but it certainly will take its effect further down the line

When there is an opening - take it: They say good things come to those who wait - that may be true sometimes - but a lot of times in combat sports advantage goes to the one who pulls the trigger immediately upon opportunity. In your sparring train yourself to go as soon as you identify a gap to enter. Opportunity rarely gets better with more time and usually degenerates. So when it’s time to go - GO!

Many is the time you’ll be caught in a well applied move. Jiu jitsu doesn’t just reward you for how well you apply moves - it also rewards you for how quickly you recover when you’re on the receiving end of a well applied move. Even as it is taking effect you should be starting your counter. The earlier you start your counter the less work you’ll have to do and the less risks you’ll have to take. Awareness is your first line of defense, but once that’s been bypassed you’ll need to start an effective physical reaction. While it’s typically more fun to practice your offensive moves, it’s typically more necessary to practice your defensive moves whenever you’re coming up against really talented opponents.

Jiu jitsu is a sport heavily based on mechanics. The laws of mechanics are object and hold equally for all. Nonetheless, there are sufficient differences between our bodies and so many contingencies we have to cover when applying them that any working application of them will have to be quite adaptive so there will be a lot of variation in the actual use of those objective mechanical laws. You will always have to hold true to the unchanging laws of lever and fulcrum - but how you express them in game situations will vary considerably depending on the context. So think in terms of mechanics, but be ready to adapt how you actually apply them in the chaos of sparring

Have a plan but make sure it is an adaptable one that can cover all the main expected divergences that can occur from your starting position. There aren’t many certainties in Jiu jitsu, but one is that your opponent will do everything they can to resist, counter and subvert everything you try. So make sure any plans you make reflect this. In extreme cases you may even have to throw out your initial plans entirely. That doesn’t mean planning is worthless, it just demands that it be adaptable

A big part of good technique is having a clear idea of what you’re trying to accomplish with a given move. Most people only think in terms of the end result - “I want to sweep him.” That’s not very helpful. You’ll need to ask what each aspect of the move is trying to accomplish - that’s when everything starts making sense. That’s when means and ends start unifying and understanding emerges. Each aspect of a move has a purpose that contributes towards its final end. Once you think in these terms you’ll be able to work with greater clarity of purpose in the execution of the move overall.

Luke Griffith @luke_griffithjj getting ready for @cjiofficial Training hard as always!

Efficiency: The better you get at the game the more you must adjust the metrics by which you judge your performances. When you first begin the study of Jiu jitsu the basic metric you use to assess your application of a given move is - did it work or not? That’s an essential metric to use, after all the most important thing is that you be effective in live sparring situations. However, as you go further into the sport you must go further than this. You must start assessing how much energy you expended to get the move to work. As a general rule you want to get maximum effect with minimal cost. This is important because everything we do in this game is done with limited resources and the most valuable resource you have is energy. If you’re expending too much energy to get effect, this will cost you as you go against better opponents who will drag matches on until you are depleted and then your technique becomes ineffective. So start to monitor not just how effective you are but also how efficient you are.

The work starts early: Very often athletes approach an opponent lackadaisically and then start there serious work for control and advantage once they’ve gotten into close contact. This is a mistake. Even before you begin contact you should be reading your opponent. You should be positioning yourself for advantage before you’ve even put a hand on him, and the second you establish contact you should be jockeying for advantage in grip. The earlier you start working, the less work you’ll have to do later. Make sure you begin your quest for advantage as early as you can. Your advantages can accrue and that’s how you go from advantage to domination.

Timing: Even an apparently perfect technique will be ineffective if applied at the wrong time. In fact timing is an essential part of what means to say a technique has been well applied and that a perfect technique with bad timing is a contradiction. Arguably the single greatest essential feature of live training as a superior training modality over drilling is its requirement of elements such as timing that are required for any live application but unnecessary when doing most conventional drills.

I am very happy to announce the release of my latest instructional video MASTER THE MOVE: THE SHOULDER CRUNCH SERIES. Many years ago I began experimenting with no gi grips to make for more effective work from guard position. I was always a fan of underhooks as a means of control, but found that when using underhooks from guard position opponents could pommel themselves free before I could engage a technique. By locking my hands I could attain greater initial control. From this grip a large number of traditional Jiu jitsu guard attacks were possible - in particular sumi gaeshi variations - the butterfly sweep. As opponents learned to resist the sweep, many other excellent offensive moves became possible, leading to a complete series of attacks off this powerful grip. For many years my students have gotten tremendous results with this practical and effective series - I am confident you can too! If you’re interested check out the link in my Instagram story!

Garry Tonon lost a very closely contested fight against the very talented Russian MMA fighter Shamil Gasanov here in Bangkok this morning. Mr Gasonov fought an intelligent counter striking strategy quite different from his usual grappling based game to try to stay away from Mr Tonons dangerous submissions which had proven decisive in their first match. The first and second round were very close, both men had their moments. In the third round Mr Tonon was able to get takedowns and won decisively with an excellent round of grappling spending much of the round in rear mount fighting for a strangle. In the end the judges went with Mr Gasanov and he took the victory. Though I was rather surprised at the decision, especially given OneChampionships purported judging criteria of judging whole matches rather than rounds, it may possibly set up an exciting rubber match in the future as they now have one win each over each other. Thank you all as always for your support and interest in our athletes. As always it was a pleasure to visit Thailand and meet so many talented martial artists here. Now it’s back to Austin to get ready for the next set of challenges!

@garrytonon getting ready to make weight and compete at @onechampionship this weekend here in Bangkok Thailand against the very formidable Russian Dagestai wrestler/Sambo fighter @shamilcobra who has been on a devastating four fight win streak since they last met and who has never lost to anyone except Garry Tonon. This will be a very tough match at Lumpinee stadium

Work on your weak points, but never lose track of the idea that it’s your strong points that will bring you most of your victories. Make sure you constantly strive to first find your strong points and then work tirelessly to improve them and build new attacks off them. This will create the character of your game

What is your mindset when you spar? Do you see it as a match that you must win? An experiment for your beliefs about technique and tactics? Do you react to what your opponent does? Or play with an objective that you try to push towards? Your answers to questions like these will determine how you spar and how you spar has a huge impact on your development. Many people watch competitions - but often you as much or even more by watching great athletes TRAIN as that will carry over more to your experience of Jiu jitsu.

Jiu jitsu is a highly tactile game. When you practice a position it’s crucial you actively seek to develop a FEEL for the move. Advice articulated through language gets you in the ball park, but it’s the experience of feeling the move in operation that adapts it to your individual body.

There is no one thing that guarantees you will succeed in reaching your goals in Jiu jitsu; but there are MANY things that guarantee you WON’T reach your goals. Sometimes it’s easier to focus on avoiding the things that are guaranteed to stop you doing something and making sure you don’t succumb to them, rather than try to find some special element that will get you to your dreams. The things that will stop you are usually much more obvious and easy to see than the things that will elevate you - so focus on them. You know that skipping training, avoiding tough training partners, poor nutrition and rest, being satisfied with the same old routine long after it has ceased to be effective - all these things (and many others that just as obvious) will absolutely impair your ability to reach your goals. Just avoid them and you’ll typically do better than people who endlessly seek some element that is supposed to elevate them to the level they desire.

Position before submission? Sure. Even better - CONTROL before submission. Control has many facets, position is just one of them. It is a deeper, richer notion than position. There are many examples of control that fall outside the traditional understanding of position but are very useful for enabling submission. Control is the game

The single greatest vulnerability of the human body in combat sports is the back. Our bodies are well adapted to deal with threats from the front, but poorly adapted to do defend threats from the back. Make this simple insight one of the core elements of your jiu jitsu and you can’t go wrong. Whenever you don’t know what to do or which direction to go - navigate the shortest route to the back and your direction will be a good one. Even if you can’t get there, just the threat will open up doors previously closed to you. When in doubt - aim for the back!

The never ending journey: Every Jiu jitsu student has an idea of attuning mastery of a set of techniques. Yet mastery proves as elusive as the end of a rainbow. We certainly make progress and feel happiness in the good effects of that progress, but there is always the sense that there is something more that we don’t yet understand and ought to know. Even if you could somehow attain mastery, still you’d have to continue learning because as you age your body will change and you will need to learn to adapt your technique to the changes in your body. Forget mastery then. Focus instead on continual improvement and adaptation. Your techniques don’t need perfection, however that is to be understood, in order to be effective. They only need to be better than your opponents defenses. Study with the goal of improvement and adaptation and you’ll make the gains you seek without the frustration of a goal that nobody could achieve even in theory

At the opening of a match there are infinite possibilities for action and decision - but as you attain increasing control over an opponent his action and decision choices become more and more limited until they come down to the simple binary choice of submit or face the consequences. Train always with this sense of increasing control and limiting choice - it is the hallmark of Jiu jitsu

Even when you’re one hundred percent locked in to a finishing hold - keep a percentage of your attention on a possible next move should that submission fail. We are always told not to put all our eggs in one basket. That’s wise advice in life and it’s also wise advice in Jiu jitsu. Don’t stake everything you’ve got on that one submission. Be ready to move into a follow up if it becomes necessary. Never forget however, that you must genuinely try to finish with that initial submission. Don’t have a mindset where you’re so focused on the next move that you simply bypass the first - you’ll never finish opponents with that attitude. Put genuine effort into the first, but leave enough to have a follow up if it’s needed.

Keep them under pressure: pressure is the factor most likely to lead to decreasing performance levels in an opponent. Everyone looks good at the start of a match when they’re fresh - very few look good after long minutes of unremitting pressure. If you feel you’ve done a good job of applying pressure for time - that’s exactly when you don’t want to step off the gas pedal - keep it up.

Moving fast and slow: The majority of Jiu jitsu is fought at a relatively slow pace on the ground where isometric tension and control is the main feature. Sometimes however, especially in standing position and transitions from ground to standing, some speed and explosion is required. Make sure that your mind and body switches immediately to the demands of the different task. Don’t have the demeanor of slow isometric tension when it’s time to chase an opponent down. Just because most of the match is slow and controlled doesn’t mean there won’t be some very important instances where you need to explode to the next position. Be aware of the distinction so that you carry the right mindset and physical reaction for each.

Every time you get a defensive over reaction to a submission attempt you’ll get an opportunity for a positional move. There is a fascinating interplay between position and submission in Jiu jitsu. Keep alert within your mind to the possibility of using one to set up the other in BOTH directions. Submission specialists tend to follow a failed submission with another submission attempt. Positional specialists tend to follow a failed positional move with another - but the best route is usually to go from submission to positional attack and use the fundamental dichotomy between submission and position in Jiu jitsu to your advantage when attacking in sequences

Impatiently patient: A good learner must reconcile two competing demands. The first is to voraciously seek knowledge and develop skills. The second is to give the development of your skills sufficient time to mature before getting distracted by the next. It’s great to be a seeker of knowledge and skill. In fact that is in my opinion the single greatest attribute a student can bring to the table, but don’t let it turn you into a dabbler who tastes skills without devouring and fully digesting them. Finding a compromise between these competing demands to seek and to complete is very important for your development. Too much emphasis on one can leave you either with too few skills or too shallow skills

Sometimes when battling hard in a neutral position it’s natural to want to hang back to save energy. It’s ok to think this way, but be mindful that if the opponent is getting tired like you and do manage to break through into a dominant position you will have now put yourself into one of the best scenarios in Jiu jitsu - an exhausted opponent underneath your pin - where you can rest but he is still carrying your weight. This usually has a very damaging effect on your opponents morale and his fatigue management. When you’re sparring try to get an accurate read on the likelihood of you getting the positional breakthrough. If you think there is a decent chance - keep pushing hard even if you’re getting tired yourself - the reward will be worth it

If you can get your opponents center of gravity outside his base of support - he will fall every time. This is the sense in which Jiu jitsu has an objective “true for everyone” flavor. Every sweep then, is a simultaneous attack on your opponents base of support and the location of his center or gravity. If you have this simple directive in your head as you enter the move you’ll be able to adjust easily to the many second by second contingencies and you’ll encounter when doing it for real.

Balancing the demands for tension and relaxation: The game of Jiu jitsu is a lot like life - long periods of relative mild activity and then sporadic bursts of maximum effort. It’s important to match your degree of muscular exertion with that necessary degree of effort. People go wrong in two ways. Either they are tense through the entirety of the match and quickly get exhausted, or they are loose throughout, which gives excellent endurance but means when it’s time to complete a move you lack the necessary horsepower to get the job done and have to continue on. Learning to match tension to immediate task at hand is a big part of your development. Learning to match it in the gym first. Remember there is much more to Jiu jitsu than the techniques. Learning the background demands such as calibrating muscular tension with task is a crucial prerequisite to the application of technique. It is a skill that can be practiced like any other skill, but because it’s not a discrete skill that catches immediate attention it’s often overlooked.

Every time you step on the mat to train there should be a skill that you have in mind to train. Passively stepping on the mat to see what happens will rarely create the focus needed to gain skill. Go in with a plan and you’ll come out with the experience of repeated trials of a skill and the feedback you need to do better with it tomorrow.

Jiu jitsu is a game split between offense and defense. Over emphasis on one is always to the detriment of the other. As you increase in skill level you’ll need to bring the two together. All good offense has built in defense and all good defense has built in offense. A rookie separates them, an expert co-joins them.

A word of thanks: Four years ago we arrived in Austin after a team break up that left us with just a few remaining athletes and no gym to train in. A chance connection led us to Roka @roka just north of downtown Austin. The manager of the company, Rob showed incredible kindness and generosity to let us train in the gym that he’d built as part of his eyewear company. We trained there twice a day seven days a week. It must have been a crazy experience for the corporate workers to have a bunch of crazy looking grapplers shuffling in and out of the building every day as they did their work, but they showed truly admirable kindness in putting up with us for so long. Rob never asked us for anything. It’s exceedingly rare to meet someone who exudes such a generous spirit. We will always be thankful for the kindness that he and his company showed. From that rough start and by his kindness we are able to get a whole new team together in record time and have our best ever performance at the ADCC World Championships and beyond. Finally we have our own place now but I wanted to give my heartfelt thanks to Rob and his team for the great kindness he showed to ours.

We all go into action with a general idea of what we want to do, but usually we end up having to modify our plans along the way in response to unforeseen circumstances. It’s important to commit yourself to the outcomes you seek - but not the means of getting to them. Adaptability is a virtue in this game. Let your sense of direction be outcome centered rather than means centered that way you’ll have a goal to navigate to by have enough flexibility to cope with the rapidly changing circumstances you find yourself in.

You don’t need a vast array of different submission holds to be effective in the submission game. The two main considerations are these. First, one well applied submission will end a match, a dozen poorly applied submissions won’t even score you a single point, forget about winning a match. Therefore it’s better to have a few submissions you do very well than many you do poorly. Secondly, it better to have a few submissions that cover the full extent of the body than many submissions that cover only half of the body or less. Coverage and quality are the features you want in your submission arsenal - make sure you train with this in mind.

Keep them off your head: I’m sure you’ve heard the old cliche “he who controls the head controls the whole body.” Like most cliches there is a lot truth to it. Make sure you are disciplined about never letting an opponent get excessive control of your head for extended periods of time. Put up frames that make it difficult for him to get to your head in the first place and failing that, clear him off your head as soon as possible.

When you first begin Jiu Jitsu the big challenge is just to execute the moves satisfactorily. As you get better at this the challenge shifts because your opponents have the skills to recognize and resist even a well executed move. The new challenge becomes how to create openings for those moves such that the opponent will be too late or too out of position to defend even if he does know what to do. As the resistance level increases the primary concern shifts from basic execution to nullifying skilled resistance. This is when the game starts to get really interesting as elements of deception, subterfuge and anticipation start to become crucial.

Basement days: People often comment on the number of great students who came out of there. All of them were amazing people. They paid their price in sweat. Hard work isn’t a sufficient condition for excellence, but it certainly is a necessary one.

One of the best things about jiu jitsu is that our mistakes are usually immediately obvious. When a hold is poorly applied you can literally feel its feebleness and then unsurprisingly the opponent easily brushes it off. When a skilled opponent applies a hold even a beginner can immediately feel that they’re in danger. In this sense the game is self correcting. Errors are obvious and changes and adjustments to those errors have tangible effects for better or worse depending on whether the adjustments were in the correct direction or incorrect. As you gain in experience you won’t even have to feel the application of the hold - you can tell by looking whether it will be effective or not. Learning find that unmistakable feeling of effective application is one of the keys to progress. For most people not starts in drills and has to be transferred to more realistic sparring scenarios, but it can be found first in sparring and built on from there. However you find it, learn to recognize it and replicate it across all your skills. It is one of the great keys to your future success

The worst feeling in Jiu Jitsu is finding yourself in a situation where you have no idea what to do to extricate yourself. The second worst feeling is knowing what to do but being unable to actually do it. The first is a lack of knowledge. The second is a lack of embodied skill. Both are fatal to your prowess in a given situation. The first results is situational paralysis, the second in failed action. These horrible feelings of hopelessness point us in the directions we need to make progress - understanding of the situation and the embodied skills to actually make use of that understanding - one without the other rarely gets good results

Opening: When two athletes approach rather for the first time in a big match this is often the time of maximal tension and energy expenditure - DESPITE THE FACT THAT NEITHER EVEN HAS A HAND ON THE OTHER! Isn’t that odd. Normally we associate maximum energy expenditure with maximum force production - heavy hard muscular work. Yet it’s at the opening when you see sweat, muscular tension, early fatigue, labored breathing etc all before any physical has even started. This shows the incredible effect of nervous energy expenditure. This is very different from the muscular tension/fatigue you feel every day in the gym. I usually eschew all talk of mental energy and vague notions of the mind/body connection in combat sports, but it’s undeniable that excess mental tension manifests itself as excessive physical tension that easily result in fatigue and poor technique execution in matches. It can turn great players into average players very quickly. I wish I had some great advice to give you on how to control it - I don’t - nor has anyone else I’ve encountered despite legions of experts claiming to be able to do so. I think it’s one of the most deeply personal quests of an athletes career. Everyone has to find their own way around the problem. What works well for one may be useless to another. The only thing I can do to help is point to the wisdom of the ancient Greeks - know thyself

Getting ready: Matches are usually won by the mundane things. It’s those who do all the little things better than the other guy on time and under pressure who usually get their hands raised. Every time you practice you get a chance to build those habits that bring victory. Don’t rush them - one good performance of a move garners better results than a dozen mediocre ones. Get a good tactile feel for the move - what it feels like to lock it into place - that’s the most reliable path to victory

If you can significantly impair an opponents ability to move whilst keeping your own ability to move - you score and win far more often than not. Remember, it doesn’t do much good shutting down an opponents ability to move if you simultaneously sacrifice your own ability to move. It the asymmetry in movement capability that creates victory

Weight application is the fundamental notion from top position, but from bottom position it’s kuzushi or off balancing. This is how we use weight from underneath people. We use an opponents own weight against them by pushing or pulling their weight outside of their base of support, forcing them into a recovery that opens them to attack. When working from bottom keep a constant eye on their weight distribution in comparison to their base of support - then push or pull to stumble them - this is the basis of success from bottom position.

Whenever you’re in bottom position - elbow knee connection is the basis of your structure. The further they are apart the more open you are to an opponents attacks and the weaker your structural framing. Just as a good boxer keeps a tight guard when he’s under attack to protect himself, so too a good jiu jitsu player is always vigilant of their elbow knee connection.

Jiu jitsu has two major categories of finishing weapons - joint locks and strangles. Of the two, strangles are the more certain option as they will end a match regardless of whether your opponent submits or not. Moreover, they will work just as well late in a match as early, unlike joint locks whose efficacy suffers more as sweat, slipperiness and fatigue increase over the course of a match. Get good at both, but given a choice, generally go with strangles.

Remember always that you are in a sport where one move performed well can win a match, but a dozen moves performed in a mediocre fashion won’t score a dime. Learn to execute well. Train to work with precision so that you can employ the details that make execution more efficient when it counts.

Most athletes greatly overestimate how much physical tension in their muscular system is required to accomplish the task they’re currently engaged with. Don’t get me wrong - if you’re trying to complete a strangle on a tough opponent in a hard fought match you’ll need plenty of muscular exertion and tension (though I’d still argue it’s less than most people use). However, in many cases you’ll need minimal exertion and tension - so take advantage of that and relax in those moments. Extended tension is exhausting and leads to subpar execution. We’re at our best when the degree of tension matches the task at hand. Neither more nor less.

Most frantic or rushed action in Jiu jitsu can be traced back to simply NOT KNOWING WHAT TO DO IN THAT SITUATION OR SCENARIO. Understanding what we need to do has a wonderful effect on our actions. It immediately gives us a calm sense of direction and purpose - even when it’s not easy to physically get it done. Do everything you can to develop a sound conceptual understanding of the scenarios you play in and interestingly many of of the physical issues you experience in Jiu Jitsu smooth themselves out.

The best athletes I trained were also the best learners. There is no greater skill in Jiu jitsu nor in life itself than the skill of learning. How far you progress in any aspect of Jiu jitsu is directly proportional to your capacity to learn well. Carry yourself as a learner first and everything else second and you’ll far both in Jiu jitsu and in everything else.

Do you remember your first Jiu jitsu class? Mine was thirty years ago and I can remember it well. I think that everyone’s first class follows the same themes - initial confusion as to the various positions; skepticism as to whether the moves shown would really work, and then an absolute ass whipping that revealed they definitely DO work and all those confusing positions work like magic but you have no idea how. This leaves us with the all important time AFTER class - where two options swirl inside your head. The first is to say this was terrible and I’ll never do it again. The second is to say this was terrible and I’ll do it as much as I can until it becomes tolerably less terrible or I can make it just as terrible to other people! I guess we all chose the second option and here we are! 😂😂

Is Jiu jitsu an art or a science? It seems clear that is has both elements within it. Insofar as Jiu jitsu makes clear and frequent use of mechanical principles that are objective in nature - it is a science. For any given scenario there will be an optimal position for lever and fulcrum, it will vary from one scenario to another, but for that scenario there will be features that either align with the laws of mechanics or they don’t and hence are objectively true or false.
