Insofar as we accept this, there is an objective, scientific element to Jiu jitsu. There are also a great many subjective elements to Jiu jitsu. Elements of choice and self expression which vary widely from one person to another. Insofar as this is true, Jiu jitsu has an artistic element. The question itself is interesting. It frames Jiu jitsu as either an art or science, but ignores the possibility that it might be neither, but instead some other type of activity, which is my belief. Either way it’s clear it involves both objective and subjective elements and that a solid understanding of Jiu jitsu must involve both

Once you create pressure - keep pressure: Pressure is a key element in Jiu jitsu. Its effect is directly related to time. Ten seconds of pressure will have no effect on the outcome of a match. Ten minutes of pressure will crush the soul of even the toughest opponents. As much as we all appreciate a smooth and stylish game, at the highest levels it’s usually the question of who can apply pressure the longest that decides winner and loser.

When we talk about control over an opponent in Jiu jitsu there are two main elements involved. The first and most fundamental is control over his MOVEMENT. The second is the resultant control you gain over his DECISION MAKING. When you dictate how he can move, you also gain control over his decisions. Once you’re in control of those two elements, everything else falls into place. The control over the body and movement is obvious to the eye of an observer - the control over his decision space much less so - but it is this form of control that enables you to accurately predict and even determine his next movement - and that’s REAL control.

If you can form angles, you can create attacks. It’s tough to attack straight through the front door. You can do it but you’ll need either some kind of initial advantage or some serious horsepower. Much better is take advantage of the fact that the human body doesn’t cope well with threats from an angle. Change your orientation and you’ll positively change your success rate!

Every target you’ve identified on an opponents body will be defended prior to your attack by a well trained opponent. Therefore the first stage of any attack will generally need to be aimed at the obstruction and THEN at the actual target. Learn to read your opponent. How they stand and hold themselves will tall you a lot about their sense of defensive responsibility. Soon you’ll see the defensive roadblocks they’ve set in front of your main targets and remove them first.

When it comes to building your offensive skills, focus upon a smaller number of connected moves rather than a larger number of disconnected moves. The wider the scope of your moves, the less time you can devote to each to gain mastery. As you gain in number of moves, you lose in time devoted to practice and study of each. Don’t spread your offensive resources too thinly. Offense rewards depth over breadth of skill.

All the most successful people I met and trained in Jiu jitsu had an insatiable desire for knowledge and a tremendous willingness to do the physical work to transform that knowledge into skill. There were great differences among them all, but those two commonalities stood out as clearly as the sun and moon.

Give the class a direction at the onset and the work that follows has a sense of purpose and focus that makes skill development easier. Start with development understanding of the skill area you’re investigating. What are the respective goals of the two athletes? Why are they important? How can they best achieve them? What problems will they face as they attempt to do so? Then it’s time for action. You are measured not by how well you drill, but by how well you perform in sparring, so make sure your primary skill work is done in the mutual attempt to impose your will against resistance.

The start of every engagement is the time of maximum uncertainty. Both you and your opponent have every possible option available to you. With every additional element of control, starting with grip and building up all the way to full positional control, the number of options is stripped away until they are but a handful. At that point the number of options is so small that the next step is entirely predictable. That is when you not only control your opponents body, but even their decision space - and that’s real control - the great goal of Jiu jitsu

Balance and base: one of your greatest concerns in top position is keeping your base. In bottom position it’s upsetting your opponents base (Kuzushi). Almost everything you do from bottom position is made dramatically easier by first disrupting your opponents base of support. Position before submission? Everyone knows that, but just as important is kuzushi before attack. It’s easy to say the sentence, but hard to establish it as a habitual action, yet that is where success from position is almost always found.

From the earliest days I’ve always preferred to see a sense of calm professionalism before a match among my athletes. You don’t freak out and get emotional every day in the gym when doing Jiu jitsu - so why do it before a match? I respect that everyone has their own way of getting ready - the most important is to use what works for you. However, if in doubt, try to capture the same sense of relaxed calm you exhibit every day in the gym. Think in terms of make it more like every day actions rather than someone totally exceptional and different. Give it a try next time you’re getting ready

Helena Crevar @helenajiujitsugirl wins gold at brown belt at the IBJJF World Jiu Jitsu Championships! Teenage phenom Helena Crevar showed her gi/no gi versatility at the @ibjjf world championships today, winning a gold medal in her weight division with another brilliant performance. She won half her matches via submission and then had a thrilling comeback from behind match in the finals that she won in the last twenty seconds. Congratulations Helena!!

Defensive responsibility: The game of Jiu jitsu is equally divided between offense and defense. When you first begin training you have no idea of the threats all around you and are easily caught unawares. However, as you improve you start anticipating and negating threats earlier and earlier. The more you do this the more successful you’ll be. As they in medicine - an ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure - so too in jiu jitsu - the earlier you address potential threats the easier to deal with them. That’s means habitually doing the right things defensively without the need for prompts and reminders. In this game offense and defense can interchange very quickly indeed - make sure you’re defensively responsible throughout and you’ll be able to survive those fast interchanges much better and get just as quickly back into offense

Much of the game of Jiu jitsu is creating mechanical advantage, but almost as important is advantage based on the very nature of the human body. There is no doubt that among we humans the strength of our legs is far greater than the strength of our arms. You want this basic idea to be reflected in your Jiu Jitsu as much as possible. Whenever you can, seek to control your opponents head and shoulders with your legs. This immediately creates a situation where the stronger elements of your buddy as fighting one of the weaker elements of your opponent’s buddy. This disparity allows smaller, weaker people to defeat even significantly stronger people.

I am very happy to announce the release of my latest instructional video MASTER THE MOVE: THE BACK CRUCIFIX. My students have always been well known for their expertise in back attacks. Usually in competition we focus primarily upon rear mount with arm traps because of the points structure that generally favors conventional back control, but in training or in situations where there are no points and the focus is on submission, we often make us of back crucifix attacks. Interestingly most Jiu jitsu athletes are very adept at defending more conventional forms of back control just because they have a lot of practical experience dealing with the position, but because they have much less experience dealing with back crucifixes they often struggle against this great attacking position. This video looks at a systematic approach to control, understanding the main lines of resistance you’ll need to overcome and navigating through the most important positional shifts you’ll need to make to hold down and finish your toughest opponents. Add some venom to your back attacks! Check out the back crucifix! Link in my Instagram story

A terrible mistake in Jiu jitsu is to think that fundamentals is a STAGE THAT YOU HAVE TO GO THROUGH before you emerge in the other side into advanced techniques. You never go through and past the fundamentals - they are a lifetime of study and ever increasing study and sophistication. Rather than pass through, you simply add depth of understanding to them and refine your performance of them. The very same things you worked in your first three months of Jiu jitsu you’ll be doing until your last day on the mats - just with greater precision, against better opponents and with a clearer sense of what you’re doing and why you’re doing it the way you are.

The goal or purpose of Jiu jitsu is submission, but the methodology of Jiu jitsu is CONTROL. Control comes in many forms, position, grip, balance/stance breaking etc. The type of control you seek above all others is CONTROL OF YOUR OPPONENTS MOVEMENT. When you can stifle and control an opponents ability to move, submission will start to become a formality. Control first - submission second.

Hard work is a necessary but not a sufficient condition of excellence in combat sports. The world is full of athletes who worked very hard but have nothing to show for it. It’s always a question of how well directed that hard work is that determines how far you progress. Make sure you’re constantly asking yourself if what you’re currently on and the manner in which you’re working on it is really the optimal choice. Use success and failure as feedback to see if you need to change direction. Many athletes are happy to work very hard with their bodies in physical labor but surprisingly reticent to work mentally on analysis of their trajectory of learning and development. Yet it’s the latter that gives direction to the former and only the combination of the two takes you towards your potential.

The more that a move relies upon SPEED for entry, the more important DECEPTION becomes for successful entry and application. You can fake an opponent out and strike successfully before his reactions can catch up. The more a move relies upon CONTROL for execution, the less important deception becomes and the more important IMMOBILIZATION becomes. Movement is the bane of control, when you can control an opponent’s movement you needn’t be deceptive about your intentions. He can know everything you want to do, but if he can’t move, he won’t be able to stop it.

One of the most important things you can ever teach is where to put your attention. Everyone looks but not everyone knows what to look for, yet knowing what to look for and perceiving it as the trigger to action is among the most important determinants of success or failure in the game.

One of the surest signs of increased proficiency in Jiu jitsu is the speed and efficiency with which an athlete can transition from offense to defense and vice versa. This is a game of defensive skills and offensive skills. The two are usually taught and practiced separately, but in a match you’ll be expected to work seamlessly from one to another. The quicker and more efficiently you can make that switch the better will be your game

At the onset of a match there are infinite possible choices for the next move. Your job as you grapple is to reduce the number of possible choices by imposing control over your opponents movement. As their choices get more and more restricted, they become more and more predictable. The ultimate goal is to funnel their choices down to two - submit or face the consequences. This funneling of the decision space down to the last binary choice is the goal of all good jiu jitsu. Physical control leads to the most important control of all - your opponents decision making.

We are all given attributes in different sector and to different degrees. Some are fast, some strong, some tall, some short. We can have a certain amount of control over them in some cases, others, no control at all. But one thing that we have absolute control over and which has a far greater impact on the level we finally reach, is consistency. If you train consistently in a well thought out fashion it will be very hard indeed for even your most talented peers to keep up with you as time marches on. The short term always favors the naturally gifted, but the long term always belongs to the consistent. As long as your walking in roughly the right directions, how long you walk will always outweigh how fast you walk

As a general rule the more clearly you can articulate and identify the problems you face in any given enterprise, and in particular Jiu jitsu, the more clarity and effect you experience with your solutions. Take the time to study the problems in front of you and be as far reaching and experimental with your questions as you can. When the questions are good, the answers will be even better.

Break an opponents stance and you break most of the resistance to the move you plan to use. Attack the stance first, then the move. Make this your habit and moves that once seemed impossible become significantly easier.

Maximum tension has to be used sparingly in combat sports as it has many bad effects when overused - but when it’s time to use it - use it!! If your skill level get the effect you sought - excellent. If not, learn to release it quickly and move in. It’s not the tension that does the damage - it’s the time under tension. Use it like the gas pedal in your car. You wouldn’t press it to the floor the whole journey - same in Jiu jitsu. Most if the time you’ll be in a comfortable cruise, but sometimes you’ll need to make that engine roar

The basis of all your effectiveness in jitsu is your stance. How effective you are in any given scenario is a direct reflection of the integrity of your stance. When it’s time to attack an opponent, understand that it will be difficult as long as his stance is intact. So then, make his stance the first subject of your attack and whatever subsequent attacks you choose will be rendered easier.

One of the hardest things to learn is PATIENCE WHEN YOU’RE WINNING. Typically we feel a need to speed things up when we’re on a path to victory. It’s natural to feel that way. We think that the longer the time period between where we are now and execution of a winning move, the more opportunity for an opponent to escape. In fact you’re generally better off (time permitting) slowing things down a little getting the pre-requisites in order first. This games generally rewards preliminary control over speed of execution. So next time you’re getting into a potential finishing position, slow things down a little rather than speed them up and take note of your finishing percentages

“If you can keep your head when all about you are losing theirs…”
In a sport where the single most important skill is that of making sound decisions in minimum time under maximum pressure - the ability to maintain composure is truly key. Like everything else it starts in the gym. See every situation that induces fear, frustration and exuberance as an opportunity to practice holding your equanimity and it’ll be easier when you need it most.

When most people learn a technique, they engage in mimicry. They mimic what they’ve seen performed. The idea is not to COPY technique, but to UNDERSTAND it. Only then can the kind of second by second adaptation needed for successful implementation under match conditions occur reliably.

There is much about jiu jitsu that is deeply subjective and individual. Your style, your preferences, your favorite moves, your learning of implicit skills is- but there is one thing that is straightforwardly objective and per case you’re either correct or incorrect - mechanical efficiency. You can be an individualist about many things, but not mechanics. The laws of physics apply the same way to all of us. How efficient your game is has many elements, mechanics is one of the most important. Any deviation from mechanical efficiency will cost you energy and effectiveness.

Give yourself four things - a clear GOAL, TIMELY FEEDBACK to know whether you’re getting closer or further from that goal, and the TIME and RESOURCES to bring the first two closer to alignment, and you can build a skill into yourself or others to a level that can shock people.

Good teaching is just not about telling athletes what to do, in fact I’d go further and say that that is substantially less important than teaching what to care about. When a student knows what is important in a given scenario it’s easy for them to not just implement your advice but to adjust it, adapt it and ultimately improve it.

When it’s over - it ain’t over: One of the best times to impart information is after class. The sparring component of a class always raises problems and alerts us to weaknesses in our game. Nothing motivates us to listen more than an irksome problem, so this is often the best time to reach a student and create a memorable addition to their understanding of a move, a position or a scenario. It’s difficult to impart information to someone who has no perception of a problem, but easy to someone in a state of mental unrest in reaction to a problem that is thwarting their goals. Take some time after class to problem solve and you’ll often add far more effect in less time.

Hard work is a necessary, but not a sufficient condition of progress in Jiu jitsu. At the upper echelons everybody works hard, everyone is highly motivated, everyone is prepared to do the extra work. If you wish to distinguish yourself you’ll need to do something they aren’t. If it isn’t hard work, what is it? It is almost always either the development of a higher understanding of the game, a form of new or rediscovered technique neglected by most, or a better training method that embodies skill better than your rivals for the same amount of effort. Point your sights in those directions and you’ll soon stand out from your peers.

Today I went into surgery for my second hip replacement. I’ve had severe physical issues my whole career due to a crippled leg suffered from an early age. Typically when one part of a system is broken, it puts undue compensatory stress on other parts of that system and so I’ve juggled problems over the years. I’ve been holding off on this second surgery for around four years. I was going to do after we left Puerto Rico but when the team broke up I had to delay it in order to be able to build a new team. Now it’s time as I’m struggling to even walk and perform basic functions, let alone Jiu jitsu. I will be out of action for a while but I believe I can still be a help conceptually until I can get active again 🤗🤗 Thank you to Dr Davidovitch for his work @roydavidovitchmd

Typically it’s more fun to work on your strengths, but more beneficial to work on your weaknesses. When it’s time to make progress, you have to sacrifice a little fun in favor of benefit. Don’t worry, soon enough those skills that were difficult and not much fun to work on will improve so much that they’ll become new strengths - and hence fun to work on! What seems a struggle today will be within your comfort zone in some future tomorrow

One of the surest ways to increase learning rate in Jiu jitsu, and indeed, any activity where physical skill is the main driver of success, is to have a clear idea of what you want to achieve and accomplish in your training sessions. You want a proactive approach to training sessions rather than a spontaneous/reactive/laissez faire approach. The more focused your goal at the onset, the further you’ll progress towards that goal. Absence of a goal in a training session is like absence of a compass on a ship on the ocean - you’ll drift whichever way the winds and currents are running, rather than in the direction you want.

There are many skills to learn in Jiu jitsu, probably too many for several lifetimes in you wanted to study all the techniques in the game; but there is a skill that in importance for how far you’ll progress stands far above any Jiu jitsu technique you’ll ever learn - the skill of learning. THE SKILLFUL LEARNER INVARIABLY BECOMES THE SKILLFUL STUDENT. This is also the the skill that has the greatest potential to make your study of Jiu Jitsu benefit your life overall, as the skill of learning pertains to every aspect of your existence. The study of Jiu jitsu can be a case study of learning in general since its practice, even in quite naive forms, has many features built into it that embody good learning principles.

In Jiu jitsu the object is control. Fundamentally it is control over your opponents body, but the deepest form of control is over your opponent’s decision space. You are forcing him to make decisions with ever decreasing options and less desirable options. As the options for the body decrease, so to do the options for the next decision decrease. There comes a point where the number of reasonable decisions is so low that you can start making reliable projections as to what his next move will be - and that’s real control

There are an infinite number of ways to win. You don’t need to learn them all to get the victories you seek, just enough to get the job done against a wide array of opponents. Don’t spread your training resources too thinly. Better to spend a lot of time on a few techniques than a little time on many techniques. Every choice you DO make in training is made at the expense of the choices you DON’T make - choose wisely.

There are many factors involved in the application of a tight submission hold - don’t worry - it’s almost never the case that even the best athletes apply all of them in a single submission hold. Remember, the application doesn’t have to be perfect - only good enough to overcome the defensive abilities of an opponent in the time available. So instead of trying to remember everything at once, focus on what you believe are the most important details for that application rather than every detail you’ve ever been shown. As the old saying goes, better to go with good enough now than wait for perfection and apply a “perfect” after the opportunity has passed.

If you can guide people’s attention to a crucial detail in a way that they will recall under stress, and that detail is actionable within their current skill set - then you can trigger a significant performance in a very short time frame - more so than perhaps any other means.

Your structure is your strength: Show me a strong man in a weak structure and I’ll show you a weakling on the mat. Show me a weak athlete in a strong structure and I’ll show you someone who’ll shock you with their strength on the mat. Learning the architecture of strong structure is easy - learning the discipline of maintaining a strong structure under pressure for the entirety of a match is difficult. Do it so often that it becomes a habit and you’ll find yourself able to maintain and express whatever amount of strength much longer and much more effectively

The best teachers I had, whether it was in Jiu jitsu, philosophy, mathematics or physics or indeed any subject; were those who sought to build understanding of the subject in a way that was not needlessly complicated, nor overly simplistic. Then they let the students build from there and work independently so as to configure their approach to the subject in their own terms and according to their own understanding under the proviso that they could demonstrate its effectiveness. Keep true to this and you’ll run a good program.

Just as water will always reveal a hole in a cup, so too, pressure will reveal the holes in your game. The best place to first feel that pressure is in the gym rather than in a match. You have to put yourself against very good opponents or put yourself in very bad positions - both will create the pressure that reveals the areas you need to work on. Once you’ve identified those areas then it’s time to formulate a plan to create improvement, but it all starts with identification. You can only fix a problem when you’ve identified a problem.

Reflections on the WNO event last night: In recent years there has a general trend towards shorter Jiu jitsu matches. These are often seen as more exciting due to the higher pace and the need for both athletes to press the action in the time available. One of the few organizations still using long matches is @flograppling where main card matches are fifteen minutes long. As a general rule in Jiu Jitsu, the longer the match and the less intervention from the officials - the more the result will reflect the true relative skill level of the two athletes. In a very interesting twist from last night - both the co-main event between Luke Griffith and Victor Hugo and the main event event between Nick Rodriguez and Kaynan Duarte were matches were if they were in the shorter time format favored by many grappling organizations, the outcomes would have been completely different. Let’s say the two matches were scheduled to be eight minutes each. If that were the case, both Mr Griffith and Mr Rodriguez would have been decisively beaten as both of them were in real trouble early in their matches. However, the extra length of the matches allowed both athletes to turn the matches around and come out with decisive victories. Match length really does matter. For grappling purists - the longer the better - as it gives the most accurate assessment of overall Jiu jitsu skill. Of course for logistical and entertainment purposes I understand the need for shorter matches at many events, but there is a lot to be said for longer matches when it’s the best of the best and we have to decide who is the best grappler in the clearest possible way.

Victory in Austin TX! It was a great night for New Wave young guns @helenajiujitsugirl and @luke_griffithjj who both scored dramatic victories against champion opponents in the @flograppling WNO show here in Round Rock Austin TX. Ms Crevar took on the WNO champion Elisabeth Clay and won via strangle after taking her opponents back during a leg lock exchange. She is now the new WNO 145 pound Champion! Mr Griffith took on the great Brazilian champion Victor Hugo, one of the biggest and most skilled heavyweights in the world. Initially it was very tightly contested, but then Mr Hugo was able to get behind and perform a big lift to take Mr Griffiths back. Mr Griffith showed great composure and defensive acumen to survive a very challenging position. Mr Griffith picked his moment and created a nice reversal to a guard pass into mount and then to the back where he very nearly finished as time expired to take a fine victory against a great opponent who outweighed him by 50 pounds. In the main event Kaynan Duarte started very strong but Nicky Rod weathered the storm and off a takedown attempt secured the mounted position which we able to maintain through to the end for a fine victory against one of the best in the world. Now I’ve got to drive home - hope you all enjoyed the show!!

Going back in time: A young Gordon Ryan and Garry Tonon getting ready for another match early in their careers (I’m not certain but I think this training session was in the days leading up to Gordons match against Ralek Gracie). You see a lot of the themes that a developing Jiu jitsu player must contend with in this photo. An empty local gym, no one but your friends and training partners see what you’re going through. You’ve got to find your own distinctive way to get to the top, at this stage in his career it was leg locks and back attacks for Gordon, though that would evolve over time as his career progressed. Perhaps most important, live training as the basis of skill development. You’ll forget most of what you’re told, but the ingrained reactions and habits forged in years of live training will be burnt into your nervous system in a way that will stay with you forever.

We all want fast progress, but never forget that new moves and tactics and even training methodologies take time to develop. It’s easy to be impatient and dismiss things that later turn out to be valuable. Rome, as they say, wasn’t built in a day, and neither is your technique. Understand that initial even the best moves will feel weak and lacking in potential. The initial deficiency is almost always in our execution rather than the move itself, particularly if it is a well known move that has a strong track record of success for other people at high level. Be impatient in your ambitions but patient in your schedule for them to be realized.

If you can keep moving even when you’re tired you’ll be ahead of most of your training partners. Most people stop when they feel encroaching fatigue. If you’re both tired and he stops and you keep moving, you’ll soon be in control of the action. The decision to stop is always just that - a decision. When you’re training train yourself to keep moving when you feel tired. Your mental resolve can be built and developed in a similar way to your other attributes. Start in the gym and work from there!

Keep an eye on the action: A workout is only as good as the feedback and lessons it furnishes for the next. Someone’s got to keep an eye on what you’re doing, sometimes it’s a coach, sometimes it’s a training partner, but most of the time it will have to be you. It is crucial that you learn to reflect on what happened during each workout so that lessons can be derived for tomorrow. Only when there is progression from one workout to the next is there improvement over time. Without that it’s just an eternal slogging match. So often I see athletes who are fully prepared to give maximum effort during a workout but settle for minimal effort on the post workout reflections which are key for progress.

When you look at some sports you see they favor a very particular body type. It would be difficult to imagine a talk and lanky gymnastics winning at high levels, or a short and heavyset sprinter, or a very short basketball player. A remarkable thing about jiu jitsu is the great variety of body types you see among the great champions, even within a weight class. This is most likely due to the almost infinite variety of techniques and tactics that can be employed by the athletes which can be modified to nearly any body type. It seems that Jiu jitsus heavy emphasis on techniques that can nullify size and strength discrepancies helps in this regard. A sport like basketball has a constraint based on the height of the hoop that largely precludes very short people - Jiu jitsu does not appear to have such an obvious limiting constraint. Jiu jitsu is an adaptive game that allows athletes to create niche areas of specialization that enable athletes of all types avenues to victory regardless of an opponent having what appear to physical advantages. So don’t think that your body is somehow less suited to Jiu jitsu than the people around you. Look through a line up of past champions and there is a very high chance you see someone who looks a lot like you!

Make the first threat a strong one so that your opponent must react. It’s the reaction that opens the door to a second and third threat; and with each successive threat defense becomes more difficult as he is always starting second to your initiation. That’s the pattern of good attacking Jiu jitsu!

Keep working diligently on your skills. There aren’t many things you can rely on in this world, but the ability to get difficult work done by applying skills that few people possess in a field that many consider important, is a notion that will never go out of fashion. People will come in and out of your life, circumstances will change, trends will emerge and then disappear - but your skill set will always be there for you when you need to get difficult work done. Never forget that skills are perishable. Once attained, they’ll need to be maintained. Set time every day to grow and maintain them and you’ll always have a trusted means of getting to your goals.

Action and reaction: The old master of Jiu jitsu and Judo strongly emphasized the idea of push and pull as the basis of grappling. “When pulled, push; and when pushed, pull,” is the classic statement of their approach. There is much wisdom in this. It’s easy to get so caught up in the application of technique that you can lose sight of the big picture. Every time you attempt a technique there will be resistance. By starting with pushing and pulling you get a resistance response - that’s the que you look for to enter into your move. That’s exactly when an opponent over commits to the response and can be scored on. Start everything with push and pull prior to your technique and everything seems to work more easily

Every move in Jiu jitsu has a counter. On the face it this might make you a skeptic about ever using a move successfully. If every move has a counter and your opponent knows roughly as much as you do, how will you ever succeed? Well, there’s a lot more to the game than who knows how much about whatever. Many other factors are involved, such as time, quality of execution, relative fatigue levels etc etc and it’s the sum of all these factors that determines the outcome. So when you go to apply a move, do so with commitment. Don’t hold back wondering if the counter will stop you. That kind of thinking never wins. If you do get countered it’s not a disaster, it’s just a temporary change of direction in the match. So go in with commitment and get on that winning path.

Most of your attempts a given move against someone your own size and skill level will fail. The question becomes - what should we do in the case of failure. Some will abandon that particular move and try some alternatives. Others will persist with the original move in the belief that the next time may be different and that previous failures are not indicative of failure on the next attempt. There is wisdom in both responses - there is no right or wrong answer here. I’ve seen both responses win matches and I’ve seen both responses lose matches. It will come down to your judgement based upon a number of relevant factors in each particular case. I will say however, that many people give up too quickly when they experience failure with a move - remember, it only has to work once to have a big impact on the match, so don’t stop trying it without a strong reason, especially if it’s one of your best moves.

Anyone can look good in a dominant position. It’s how well you deal with being trapped in bad position that will separate you from everyone else. There’s lots of strong hammers out there, but the real test is when you’re the nail. Make sure you work both sides of the positional game!

Take a little break in the action: Jiu jitsu can be a very exhausting game. In daily life what do you do when you’re exhausted? You take a break. Jiu jitsu is the same. When you feel the exertion level has been too high for too long - take a little break in the action. Obviously you can’t just get totally relaxed or get quickly crushed, but Jiu jitsu affords many opportunities to lower the intensity to a degree where you can recover enough to get back to a high exertion level that you can sustain until the end of the match. Most of these opportunities for partial relaxation occur after a positional change. After a hard fought takedown where you land in an opponents guard is a good example. A short break after the energy expenditure of the takedown allows you to go forward with sufficient energy reserves for the positional ground battle. Gaining endurance in Jiu jitsu is largely about this skill of learning where and when to take a little break in the action

If you can create a THREAT, then you can create a REACTION, and if you can ANTICIPATE that reaction you can always beat your opponent to the next move - and THAT’S how you take charge of a match.

Mentors: I was very fortunate when I began Jiu Jitsu that I had an excellent teacher, Renzo Gracie, and many excellent mentors, in particular Matt Serra, Ricardo Almeida and Rodrigo Gracie. As I progressed, those original mentors moved on to teach at their own schools, as I’m sure many of the mentors in your life did, but that doesn’t mean that new mentors can’t come in and have an important effect in your development. I had many mentors who were just visitors who stayed for a week or two, such as Ryan Gracie, Nino Schembri, Dean Lister, Samuel Braga and many others. If someone can give you an important idea that you can then research yourself, it doesn’t matter how long THEY stay, it only matters how long the IDEA stays. One of the greatest resources you will ever have in Jiu jitsu is the people around you, even those who are there only for a short while - make sure you use that resource to the greatest degree you can.

When we think of a path to victory we typically think in terms of an attacking move that slices through an opponent’s defense and gives us the win. That’s certainly a great way to think about it, but it will be difficult to get this approach to work every time against good opponents. Very often a different path to victory is required when an opponent has rock solid defense. Focus your training on becoming as difficult to score on as possible. If an opponent cannot score on you he will become frustrated and try all the harder. This almost always results in fatigue and frustration. Fatigue will always reduce an opponent’s ability to stop an attack. Get him tired enough and moves that weren’t working at all eight minutes ago suddenly start working very well. Frustration will get an opponent over extending into offense and forgetful of his defensive responsibilities. An opponent like this is much easier to attack than one who is in a more defensive mode when you’re initiating all the attacks. So instead of thinking purely about direct offense, spare a thought for making yourself as difficult to score on as possible, this will indirectly lead you to more submissions in the manner described

I am very happy to announce the release of my latest instructional video MASTERING THE MOVE: THE SIDE CRUCIFIX. The side crucifix is one of the premier finishing positions of Jiu jitsu. Jiu jitsu has always been conceived as a game of position and submission. Sometimes this has the unfortunate result of creating a gulf between position and submission. Positional pins control the torso, but submission occurs at the limbs - the control of each is very different and so as a result, many students struggle to go from position to submission. The side crucifix bridges that gap. It is a pin that directly controls the limbs as well at the torso and as such, enables you to smoothly transition from positional control into submissions. I have my own approach to the position which has proven very successful for my students - I’m confident it can for you also! If this is something you’re interested in, check it out! Links in my Instagram story

In jiujitsu, as in most things in life, if you can get off to a good start, you’ll generally find it easier to get to your final destination than if you get off to a bad start. Getting a good start in Jiu jitsu means approaching in a good stance and getting a good working grip that enables you to dictate the opening direction of the exchange. If you can consistently get a grip that enables you to transition into attacks better than your opponent, then your chances of getting into the advantageous positions that bring victory go up considerably.

Taking notes: How do you feel about taking notes after learning? Is it something you regularly do after class? If so, does it actually help you retain information? When I look at myself and my students there is a clear split on this subject. I took notes at every opportunity throughout my time in Jiu jitsu. Eddie Cummings was a prolific note taker, as was Brian Glick, but Gordon, Garry, Nicky, Nicholas, Giancarlo and most of the others never took a note in their lives and yet had no problem retaining information. There is some evidence in the literature to show that writing, particularly hand writing, does aid retention to a degree, but I’ve never seen any strong evidence of it in Jiu jitsu learning. In the case of myself, Eddie and Brian, we all came from an academic background and so I think we just naturally took notes at every taking from that and applied it to a different field of study. The other students did not have an academic past and so found other ways to order their information - both groups were successful in that regard so I don’t think there is a big advantage in taking notes. I think most people have a preferred way of retaining and ordering information and it’s best to use what you’ve had success with previously rather than forcing yourself to take notes just because you’ve seen other people do it.

Progress: In many sports it’s quite easy to discern how much progress you’re making as it can objectively measured by a clock, or the amount of weight you lift, or how far you throw an object. In combat sports like Jiu jitsu progress is not so clear. Sometimes when you’re training in a tough room it can feel like you make no progress for months on end. This is because everyone around you is going through the same program as you and making similar progress. As a result the students are like boats in a fog shrouded river where the various boats can see each other but they can’t see the river banks. The current carries them all at the same speed - they’re all moving but from the perspective of anyone on one of the boats they all appear stationary as they move at the same speed as each other and can’t see the stationary objects on the river banks that would show them that they’re actually moving. So too in Jiu jitsu. As the people around improve at the same rate as you it all seems like an endless slogging match with no forward progress. Rest assured that in the vast majority of cases you are moving forward, it’s just that it’s usually at the same pace as your comrades. Every so often you’ll get insights into your progress when you spar with someone who you used to spar with regularly in the past but who has been training elsewhere for an extended period of time. If you now do significantly better in sparring than you did in the past, then that’s a great sign that you’ve made real progress despite appearances

So much of this game is based on knowing the next move your opponent must make. When you know what the opponent has to do next, that’s when the game becomes easy. If you know your opponents has to get to his side to prevent your passing move, and you know also that the only way he can get to his side is to retract his elbow under his ribs, then you know that the smart move is to control his elbow even as you are in the act of passing. If you can kill the opponents next move before it can even get started your game takes on an effortless look and an opponent feels like he’s getting beaten before he even starts. This game of anticipation is not magic - it’s just recognizing the patterns of human movement as expressed in grappling. If you know the patterns - you’ll have an accurate idea of what’s going to happen next

When you first go in to establish contact with an opponent it’s good to have a general sense of what you want to do - this will give your attacks a sense of direction and purpose. However, it’s also good to have a realistic understanding that very often what we want to do and what ends up happening are often two very different things, so be sure to have an adaptable mindset which is not afraid to switch directions as much as necessary if you run into unanticipated barriers and obstacles to your initial plan. Have a sense of direction, but be ready to switch routes if the situation demands it

Try to stay relaxed when you’re sparring in the gym. It’s human nature to get tense in stressful situations but tension and tightness never helped anyone think more clearly or react more smoothly and efficiently. Staying relaxed as possible under pressure is a skill like any other - practice it more often and you get better at it. Next time you’re sparring be mindful of your levels of tension - self awareness is key to keeping it under control.

Just because you choose to START in bottom position in Jiu Jitsu doesn’t mean you have to STAY in bottom position. Threatening to get up from bottom is one of the best ways to enlarge the scope of your bottom game and prevent the match degenerating into stalling and passivity. It adds a whole new dimension to bottom game and greatly increases the threat level you present to an opponent

How long should you persist with a move when it feels like it’s been stopped? Every move has a counter. When you try a given move on an opponent and it gets stopped, there’s a brief time where you have to decide between persisting and hoping extra effort or a slight change in angle or direction might get you past that resistance; or switching off to a completely different move, or “bailing out” of the move altogether. It’s never easy to make that choice, especially given that it’s almost always made under great physical pressure. This is why it’s so important to practice your moves in complimentary combinations. Remember that every move has a line of resistance used by a given counter, if you have a secondary move that counters that line of resistance, the decision you have to make is much easier - stay on offense and switch off to the secondary move. If you think only in terms of single moves you’ll be bailing out much more often than you need to

If you can break an opponents stance, moves become easy - if you can’t - every move is difficult. They say position before submission. Every bit as important is the notion of stance breaking before moves. A move is only as effective as an opponents (lack of) ability to defend it - the basis of his defense is his stance. Break the stance first and the move becomes doable on even a very tough and experienced opponent.

Performance was never helped by being frantic. You can be fast without being frantic, but it’s generally easily to avoid degenerating into frantic action by slowing things down a little. This is true in both offense and defense. Human nature tends to make us all a little frantic when we feel we are on the verge of victory or defeat. This is never a help, only a hinderance in these moments. Take the intensity down a little without becoming docile and you’ll find that performance improves as you can make more precise movements and you are much less susceptible to tunnel vision.

Working out on a crowded mat: One of the most common sources of injury and frustration/annoyance in Jiu jitsu is working out on a crowded mat. Sometimes one pair will fall or roll into another. I’ve been knocked out cold three times in my career by people explosively falling/rolling into me from behind while training. I’ve seen people get broken noses, chipped teeth, even a fractured orbital bone from accidental impact. The problem is particularly bad when you have standing grappling going on right next to ground grappling so that the weight of two athletes can crash down on on the others - usually from an unseen angle so that you cannot brace yourself for the impact. Even if there is no injury, you can’t grapple if two people move right into your space. What’s need is first, a culture of situational awareness. Teach people about the potential dangers and build a culture that has people looking around themselves as they grapple rather than get tunnel vision and see only the opponent in front of them. Build a culture where they value team safety over scoring a point on a training partner. We all like to score a good move, but not at the expense of safety. Make sure you have some kind of established order in place so that students know when they infringe on another pairs space what to do. A simple approach I’ve always used in every room I coached is that if two grapplers in the course of grappling find themselves entering into the space of another pair, the entering pair stop and relocate, the other two remain in place. If it’s unclear who entered because both pairs were moving, both pairs stop and relocate. If you prefer a more traditional approach you can say that when it’s unclear who infringed on the other pairs space, the higher belts remain in place and lower belts relocate. This way you can have even a very crowded room full of athletes working hard and in constant motion - back in the basement it was common for us to have over one hundred athletes working out in less than 3500sqf of space, but this simple advice of building a culture of situational awareness and a simple criteria maintaining a safe amount of space between pairs of athletes worked well

Most people have a sense of when it’s likely they’ll be scored on and accept the score without further resistance. There is some good logic to this. If you fight too long against a score you can create a situation where your opponents score is amplified - usually by exposing your back to your opponent. So the conservative option is to take the loss and move on to the next battle. There is a middle ground between fighting the move you’re being attacked by to the bitter end and giving up too early. You want to make the opponents attempt to score as costly in terms of energy expended as possible and draw the action out as long as possible to give yourself more time to slow an opponent down and then counterattack. Time in these situations is the best friend of the defender and the kryptonite of the attacker.

There’s not a lot an opponent can do when take both his feet off the ground and apply a force to him. The question in your mind now should not be the move you’ve performed - it’s done its work already - now it’s time to consider what will happen in the aftermath of a successful move. With this much elevation the landing will be chaotic - so you know there’ll be a scramble the second he lands. Even as you’re completing one move you must be plotting the next in the context you’ve just created. Don’t perform a move and then try to figure out what to do next - figure it out BETWEEN the moves so that you’ll always be a step ahead and build momentum from one move to the next.

When you begin a match there are an infinite number of possibilities for both you and your opponent to choose. Every time you begin to grip and control each other, the number of possible actions drops. As control increases, the number of possible actions decreases. Your aim is to gain so much control that the number of possible actions for an opponent becomes first manageable, then so small that you can begin to predict his next action from the small number of alternatives left. Best of all is to have control combined with the threat of submission. This means your opponent will only be able to move in certain predictable ways to avoid that submission. In this way you can make his next move predictable. This is the highest goal. At this stage you are like a puppeteer controlling an opponent’s movement and knowing where he will be next one or two moves from now. The idea is to take your opponent’s possible options into a funnel. At the opening of the funnel many options can fit, but as you progress further into that funnel you can control and direct the water as it merges out the narrow end. When he is reduced to two or three known options at the end you can essentially read his future

When you go to apply isometric strength in Jiu jitsu, don’t apply your pressure instantly, you’ll burn out quickly that way. Get everything in position and exert your tension over time rising to a peak. Make very sure you keep breathing as you do so because you’ll need to apply that tension for a sufficient time to get the job done. This way you can apply strong tension without exhausting yourself. This game is all about the application of isometric tension from mechanically advantageous situations. Make sure that tension works against your opponent and not against yourself!

When you can absorb an opponents attack without being unduly off balanced, you will be in the best attacking position of them all - you still in solid stance and your opponent out of stance and over extended due to over committing on a failed attack. Just as an army that loses grasp of its strategy and tactics and pushes too far forward away from supplies and reinforcements and is now in danger of encirclement and destruction by a counterattacking enemy, so too, an athlete who keeps charging forward into an attack that has lost its way will find himself overextended and vulnerable to counters. Position may be the most well known precursor to submission, but counters from a strong defense against an overcommitted but failed attack from an over zealous opponent may be even better. Practice absorbing an attack and staying cognizant of opportunity in the immediate aftermath and you’ll find a wealth of new opportunities

A large part of what we call control in Jiu jitsu is the action of degrading your opponents athletic potential. The human body is capable of remarkable movement under ideal circumstances - but if you change those circumstances by say, trapping a leg off the mat, locking two legs together, pinning the head and shoulders to the floor or any other hold that compromises the body’s ability to move freely, then physical control over an adversary becomes much easier. When you go to restrain opponents don’t just put your weight in them. Ask yourself what is the first physical movement they need to make in order to begin escape - and take away their ability to make that first move. This is what makes control appear effortless when done well.

Some sports divide offense and defense very strongly. Some so strongly that have athletes responsible for defense and others for offense. In Jiu jitsu and most combat sports, you and you alone are responsible for both. When you begin typically there is a bias towards defensive skills first, and then as your game matures things swing the other way and your focus shifts most towards offense. At the highest levels however, the two become inseparable. ALL GOOD DEFENSE HAS BUILT IN OFFENSE AND ALL GOOD OFFENSE HAS DEFENSIVE RESPONSIBILITY BUILT INTO IT. The more talented your opponents the better their ability to counter you in the blink of an eye, thus the need for defensive responsibility when attacking; and also the better their ability to maintain an attacking momentum once they start, hence the need to break that momentum with your own counter offense when under attack. Don’t see offense and defense as opposite ends of a spectrum, but rather as the dual demands of good technique

When you can create dual threats at the same time you will greatly increase your success. A naive opponent can be defeated by any move. A good opponent can be defeated by a well executed move. But a great opponent will be defeated only by multiple moves working together in opposing directions so that defense of one will entrap him in the other. Learn to pair moves and you’ll double your effectiveness in a very short time

It’s much easier and faster to refine an existing technique than to learn an entirely new one. If you’re looking for fast progress, look for new applications of old techniques in your repertoire. Any given technique can be given a whole new life by the simple addition of a new set up or a new pairing with another already existing technique in your repertoire. It’s easy to dismiss your old techniques as passé but changing the packaging around them can quickly make them exciting and effective again

The narrower the focus of your attacks, the easier for an opponent to station all of his defenses in that area to stop you. As your attacks gain in breadth around the body, the more an opponent must stretch his defenses to cover all the potential danger areas. Make you can attack top half, bottom half and both left and right and it’ll never be easy for an opponent to cover all the bases.

A question I’m often asked is - how much physical intensity should I be applying to my moves when I’m sparring? I’ve seen some great champions train with great intensity. I’ve seen others train in a very relaxed fashion. I believe that in general excessive physical tension is detrimental to athletic performance. My general advice is to train at something close to the minimum physical tension/intensity to perform the task you’re working on - no more, no less. Now this means that sometimes you’ll be using a LOT of physical intensity because sometimes the task at hand is a big one. The problem is that most people have a hard time dialing back the intensity once they’ve accessed it. As a result they tend to keep the intensity higher than it needs to be and quickly become fatigued. So at all times in match, keep an eye on your intensity level and ask yourself if your current level of intensity is well matched to the task at hand. Most times for most people it’s usually higher than necessary. Learn to monitor it well and you’ll be much more efficient in your expression of Jiu jitsu

Many people have a tendency to push opponents away with their legs when in finishing positions. In the vast majority of cases you want to do the opposite - pull your opponents IN with your legs. For most leg locks you want to be tightly bound hip to hip, for most arm locks pull yourself hip to shoulder. Pull in with your legs like a hamstring curl - this will create a fit into your opponents body that will make escape more difficult and submission more likely

Great night of grappling in Las Vegas!! @ufcfightpass put on a great show tonight in Las Vegas with many very dramatic matches and submissions! @helenajiujitsugirl Helena Crevar continued her devastating run with a fine leg lock submission victory and won submission of the night! The teenage phenom stunned the crowd with a very fine submission sequence to showcase her ever increasing arsenal. @tazagarami had an action packed battle with the very talented Renato Canuto in a dramatic match that won the best match of the night award! Mr Canuto won on a score late in the match but both athletes shone! Congratulations to UFC Fight Pass for another great show! I hope you all enjoyed the action!

Workout in Las Vegas completed! @tazagarami and @helenajiujitsugirl completed their first workout in Las Vegas in preparation for UFC @ufcfightpass along with our good friend David Avellan @davidavellan and @sniper.bjj I always love time in Las Vegas - so many good memories of past MMA and grappling events! This upcoming UFC Fightpass grappling show has many great matches - keep your eyes open for this one!

The old cliche in combat sports is “he who controls the head controls the whole body.” Like most cliches there is a lot of truth in it. Nowhere is this more evident than in pins and passes from chest to chest positioning. If you can lock down an opponent’s head and shoulders whilst still being to move freely into the next position yourself, then you’re on track to win. Take every opportunity to wedge your opponents head in place with your head and underhook and you’ll be a menace from top position

It’s rare that the application of a single move is successful against an opponent your own size and skill level in a competitive match. Typically what brings victory is strings or series of moves applied in the context of physically wearing an opponent down or distracting him with some form of set up. Your training should reflect this fundamental truth. Learn your moves in sequences so that when things get competitive you’ll apply them in sequences

When you can pin with authority time becomes a weapon in your game. The longer you spend in a given pin the weaker an opponent becomes. As fatigue sets in, skill level drops. Thus can good pinning skills be the means to tip the balance of an even match as the game goes further on the clock

Whenever you attack with multiple moves in a combination or sequence, it is critical that the preceding move be done with real intention, even though you are not expecting it to succeed and to use it as a stepping stone to the next move,
Which you are counting on to be the actual winning move. It’s easy to merely go through the motions in a complacent or lackadaisical fashion with the first move, since your mental focus is on the next move This will almost always end in failure. The only way the second move will take effect is if the opponent reacts strongly to the threat of the initial move - THAT’S what opens the door to the second winning move. Without that perceived threat you won’t get the defensive reaction that will make the second move successful. So next time you’re working in combinations, don’t short change the first move, even when you know that’s not the one that will get you the breakthrough. Make the threat look and feel very real. That’s the key to getting a big defensive reaction that will make the second move work

Just as a musician seeks to constantly expand and refine his performance of his repertoire, so to a jiu jitsu athlete must constantly seek to expand and refine his repertoire of submissions. Each addition will add something to your game that will make the task of defense more difficult for your opponents. Keep studying, keep adding and as time passes your versatility and depth will increase in ways that improve your performance

Placido Santos @placisantos the world’s most famous uke got a well deserved brown belt after his double gold performance at The Austin Open. Placido is a fine example of balancing family life with jiu jitsu. Most of my well known professional athletes train full time with very few distractions. However, most people live their Jiu Jitsu lives under very different circumstances, seeking to train while prioritizing family and work, training when they can and doing their best to juggle their other responsibilities with their training time. I’m always greatly impressed by people who do this. Over the years I’ve had many students who reached a very impressive level while holding down very difficult jobs and raising children. Placido is a great example of this. Many of the professional athletes will tell you that some of their best and toughest training partners when getting ready for international competition are people who come in before or after working a full day at work and who will be going home to raising their family. That’s an achievement every bit as impressive as a full time athlete getting to the top of the competitive field. Well done Placido! Keep up the good work!

One of the best parts of training is having immensely talented visitors stop by and work alongside us. This week the great American wrestler Helen Maroulis @helen_maroulis came in for training. She is one of the greatest freestyle wrestlers of all time Olympic champion (the first American woman to win this title), three time world champion and a stunning ten time medalist in Olympic and world championships competition. She now trains Jiu jitsu in addition to her wrestling. It was truly fascinating to watch her game blend wrestling and her fast developing Jiu jitsu skills. Her wrestling style is very unique - lots of upper body controls and takedowns with footsweeps and truly superb transitions to the ground. It is very well suited to crossover with jiu jitsu. She taught one of her signature takedown variations to the squad and showed her teaching skills are as good as her competitive skills! It was a great lesson to the team to see someone who is already so accomplished still learning and moving in new directions. She is a true treasure for the grappling world!

Make an opponent work hard all the way through even when you know he is likely to score on you. It’s ok if an opponent scores on you. If he’s good that’s very likely to happen. The only thing that is unacceptable is that he scores easily on you and that you don’t contest it. If an opponent does score on you it should be a nightmare for him that has him seriously questioning whether it was worth the energy he had to expend getting the score. You’d be surprised how long you drag things out before you concede a score. When an opponent has to work like a dog to get a score on you and now feels like he’s gone much further into the red than he believed he would have to go - it’s going to be a daunting prospect for him to try any more offense. So if you feel you have to concede points make sure they come at a very heavy price in terms of energy expenditure to your opponent. In that way even a loss can come out as a partial win that can help steer you towards victory further down the line.

If a jiu jitsu student just focused on what he learned in his first year of training it would not be out of the question that he would be able to get to the top of the sport with these techniques alone. Such is the power of the fundamentals of the game. The fundamentals furnish the clearest lessons of the principles of the sport from which all other, more advanced and esoteric elements spring, and in addition, are the clearest expressions of those principles in action. It’s easy to overlook those early lessons as you go further into the sport, but as you go further still, you’ll look back on them in wonder and admiration

Some improvement through working hard. Others by working smart. But the best get there by doing both. We do the hard physical work in the gym, but that’s never the end of it. Then it’s time for the mental work outside the gym. That’s when study, evaluation and assessment is made. This creates the direction and focus of the next physical session, which turn provides new feedback into the cycle of skill development. That’s the path to your goals

Most people measure themselves by how well they perform in winning situations, but when you are up against your toughest opponents you’ll be measured by how well you handle losing situations and fight your way back into winning situations. Make sure your training reflects this truth.

It’s harder for an opponent to attack you when he has to continuously react to the threats you create for him. You can never let an opponent attack you uncontested for any length of time. Any attacks he launches should have to be done in the midst of defending himself from your attacks. This minimizes his set ups and the overall quality of his attacks. Seizing control of the proactive vs reactive narrative of a match is key to winning against opponents of similar skill level. When the attacking momentum is in your favor, your own attacks will come easier and his will be easier to squash

Do you bring a special gift to the game? I’ve always believed that everyone has some special attribute that they possess which, if used properly, will govern them a measure of advantage against their opponents. This gift varies wildly from person to person, but each of us must identify it and try to use it to maximum effect. For some people it’s an obvious physical feature, strength, reach, speed. For others it’s a mental attribute, toughness, determination, a calm demeanor in chaos. Sometimes it’s less obvious - an attribute such as patience isn’t always easy to see but can be of tremendous value in the long haul. Do you have something that you’ve always felt you did particularly well? Does it currently play a role in your game? If not, could it? When you look within yourself it may not be immediately obvious. For example I’ve always considered one of Gordon Ryan’s best attributes that contributes significantly to his success is memory. He has an astounding memory for Jiu Jitsu information and his ability to make Jiu jitsu an information battle makes this a serious weapon in his arsenal. We don’t ordinarily think of memory as a relevant attribute in Jiu jitsu but he made it one of his best. Tr hard to know yourself. The better you know yourself the better you can build a game around who you are and the better the armor of your jiu jitsu will fit your body.

A guiding principle of high percentage Jiu jitsu is to nullify first - attack second. You’re engaged in a game where the other fellow is always trying to attack you at the same time you’re trying to attack him. You don’t want to get into shootouts where it can go either way like a coin toss. Prudent athletes usually first negate an opponents ability to attack first and only then launch their own attacks. Identify the danger and remove it early - for example in the case of guard position, the feet are the essential connectors that enable an opponent to attack - so negate them first. Then you can begin your attacks without concern about what he might do as you attack. This will enable you to focus on what you want to do without distraction, making your attacks much more likely to succeed!

Keep searching for knowledge. There aren’t many certainties, but there are many refutations - so keep an open mind and seek experimentation at every opportunity. When you can transform that knowledge into a skill, that skill can transform your game and generate new questions and insights and create a virtuous circle between skill and knowledge. Keep everything honest with trial and error and you can move forward over a lifetime

Momentum: If you can string some attacks together in a sequence you’ll develop attacking momentum. This is an extremely important element of success at high levels where immediate breakthroughs and success are rare. It doesn’t matter if three moves in a row fail to go all the way to completion - just so long as they force extended defensive reactions. Once you build attacking momentum - see how long you can keep it. Don’t stop on your own accord. Stop only if you get stopped. That momentum can steer the whole match in your favor for a sizable period of time. It creates a reactive mindset in your opponent where he is no longer thinking about what he will do to you, but only what you’re doing to him. Switch rapidly between positional and submission attacks to carry the momentum more easily and in different directions to make it even harder to stop. Get a few sequences in your favor like this and you’ll set yourself up for victory later!

You have to work with what you’ve got: In my Jiu jitsu journey one of the biggest issues was always my own body. I began the sport late with a crippled leg. I always tried to live as active a life as my body would permit. Unfortunately when one part of the body is compromised, other parts have to take an added strain, which in the long term will always cause problems. Eight years ago I got a hip replacement and was able to resume coaching at a professional level. About four years ago my other hip started to deteriorate in a similar way. I was going to get it replaced, but when the squad broke up I had to start a new team from scratch and so I had to delay it. Recently my hip went into final decline and now doctors are urging me to get it replaced and also the crippled knee that was the root cause of the problems. This year I will have to do four surgeries to try and get a satisfactory body back. I started today with the easiest surgery - a bilateral inguinal hernia repair that I’ve been putting off too long. Then I will have to schedule the much more serious hip and knee replacements (after that I have to get lens implanted my eyes to get my deteriorating vision back in order). My last hip replacement took about six months to a year to fully recover from. The issue will be the more complex and generally less robust knee replacement, which in my case will be attached to a femur that already has a hip replacement on the other side of it. The doctors say that generally this is the end of any kind of Jiu jitsu type movement at high level. As such I will most likely have to make a reappraisal of my role in Jiu jitsu coaching. I hope that given enough time I can return to high level coaching but it may be that I have to switch focus to more recreational Jiu jitsu coaching. Despite the uncertainty, there is something of which I am very sure. If I do have to step away from coaching professional athletes, my students are without exception, truly outstanding teachers so I am extremely confident that they can represent what we espoused far better than I ever did and contribute greatly to the sports continued growth. Today, one operation down, three more to go!

You don’t have to be perfect in your execution of technique in Jiu jitsu - you only have to be good enough in your execution to be able to surpass the resistance of the opponent in front of you. So don’t hold back thinking that if you aren’t perfect you aren’t good enough to try. You aren’t perfect - but nether is your opponent. Don’t let your admirable desire to seek perfection in technique become a hinderance. Keep a healthy sense of not giving a damn and go for it!

Jiu jitsu is a game of progression towards two (mostly) correlated goals - position and submission. The highest goal is submission. Most of the time, the safest path to submission is along the road of position, since the better your position the more likely it is you’ll be able to submit your opponent and the less likely he’ll be able to do the same to you. Build a mindset of working forwards toward your positional goals no matter how many roadblocks and obstacles your opponent puts in front of you and you’ll be a difficult opponent for anyone. Don’t just work hard - work with a sense of direction. What’s your next position? How will you get there? What can your opponent do to stop you and how can you overcome this? As long as you’re thinking in these terms you’ll always be on the right track no matter how difficult things may seem second by second.

Taking a break from the action: It can be very tiring when working against a skilled opponent. If you keep pressing the gas pedal when you’re already tired it’s quite likely you’ll get to a level of fatigue where if you can’t finish your opponent you’ll lose on sheer exhaustion. Learn to take breaks when appropriate. When you’ve gotten to a position of advantage there is usually time to slow things down and recover your breath and dissipate some lactic acid. If you’re in decent shape even a short break can lead to significant recovery. Since you’re in a pin your opponent won’t find recovery as easy as he is carrying your weight. Once you feel sufficiently recovered , then it’s time to get back to work, but in a condition where renewed work won’t push you over an edge from which you can’t recover. If you had. A big project to complete at work over a month, you wouldn’t just keep working on it with no sleep, no food, no rest - you’d be completely frazzled long before it was complete. Instead, you’d take regular breaks for food, rest, sleep, even weekend does off to re-energize and that way you’d have the staying power to get the project completed. Your jiu jitsu should be the same.

The skills of Jiu jitsu are highly perishable. The fact that you’ve ATTAINED them does not guarantee that you’ll RETAIN them. Stop training and you’ll find that those precious skills can lose a step quicker than you’d like. The good news is that once skills are well learned, you can generally MAINTAIN them with considerably less effort than the initial learning process and in addition, should you ever lose them, you can generally RE-learn them much more quickly than the initial learning period. The lesson we learn from this? Don’t neglect the skills you’ve worked hard to gain. If you do, inevitably they’ll deteriorate to some degree. Even a basic maintenance program can keep them in good shape. If circumstances do create a time where skills deteriorate, don’t despair, you can recover them much more easily than you learned them

Whenever you get stonewalled in front of a tough opponent - change the angle and get underneath them and suddenly a whole new set of opportunities present themselves. Your opponents defensive stance always assumes your current position - change your position, angle and/or orientation and now it’s not so defensively sound anymore. You aren’t married to your current position/angle or orientation, so if it’s not providing you opportunity - change it!

The effectiveness of your submissions is directly linked to their mechanical tightness. Space creates a loose fit and a loose fit is fatal to a submission hold on an opponent who has any defensive skills. When you drill, aim to create the sense of a very tight fit around your opponents limb in order to feel what a good mechanical fit feels like and then seek to reproduce that as best you can in sparring - that’s where the true test of your tightness will be.

They say that tired men make mistakes - our experience in Jiu jitsu suggests there is a lot of truth to this. Add to it the notion that pressure also makes us prone to mistakes and you’ve a formula for overcoming even your most technically adept opponents. They might be very sharp in technique, but if you can get them sufficiently fatigued, frustrated and subject to severe pressure, that sharp technique will be rendered blunt in time. When you get a position that offers a chance to wear an opponent down - take your time there. Moves aren’t the whole story of victory and defeat - the physical and mental state of the opponent plays an important role in the outcome also - so if you have a chance to influence it - take it!

How fast you can execute a move isn’t that important in Jiu jitsu. Much more important is fast you are at going from one move to another. This is not determined so much by your physical speed as the speed of your decision making. You can make yourself appear faster by linking your favorite moves into series, or even complete systems. That way the decisions are made and ingrained long before you act and you’ll spring into action at the first sign of opportunity!

You are in a game that is built around lever and fulcrum. Even a slightly inefficient placement of the fulcrum or a suboptimal grip on the lever can mean a serious loss in force generation. This is a time when taking an extra second to make adjustments will pay you big dividends. Study and understand leverage and you can make your perceived strength double overnight, not because you got twice and strong, but because you got twice as efficient.

If you can make an opponent carry your weight on his head and shoulders for periods of time, you can make him fatigue faster than you. Remember, it’s not how tired you get in a match that counts - it’s how tired you get relative to your opponent that determines the outcome. It’s ok to be 80% exhausted so long as the other guy is 90% exhausted. It’s the disparity in fatigue that creates winner and loser rather than the fatigue itself. See the head and shoulders as one of the best areas to apply your weight and you’ll create a situation where he works harder than you over time - and that’s a true path to victory

All knowledge begins with curiosity: To achieve high standards in Jiu jitsu or whatever other domains you will need knowledge. The precursor of knowledge is curiosity. Building a deep sense of curiosity is one of the greatest catalysts to knowledge acquisition you can ever acquire. The day your quest becomes boring or tiresome is the day it dies.
