You can’t force curiosity, but you can inspire or arouse it. The best way is always to ask questions of yourself and others and experiment with answers. Students with a questioning disposition and a yearning for answers are always the ones who make the best long term progress. Cultivate within yourself a questioning mind and a rational means of searching for answers and you’ll never stop making progress.

Executing a move well is very important, but even more important is the ability to combine moves together well. Perfect execution can be stopped by perfect defense but it’s very hard to stop a series of moves where the defender is playing catch up after investing too much in defending the previous move when you’ve already moved on to the next. That’s how you beat opponents with very stout defenses. Don’t just learn moves - learn to string moves together and you’ll soon soba jump in your success rates.

Opening move: When you come to grips with your opponent at the beginning of an exchange there’s always a moment where both of you are searching for an opening move. Make sure your mind is thinking of more than an opening move - you’ll need to think in terms of precursors to that opening move because the move itself will be difficult to apply on a good opponent if you just go straight in with that move. Always think in terms of gaining some form of preliminary advantage prior to attempting the move. So for example, get an advantageous angle or grip, get him out of balance, distract him with feints or some other action that breaks his stance and focus - THEN it’s time to go in and hit that opening move!

Be a maximizer: When most people get out of a bad situation in Jiu jitsu they are happy to get back a neutral position and aim their sights accordingly; just as most people working their way out of debt are happy just to pay off the debt and stop there. The better goal is to escape a bad position and immediately get into counter offense. In the majority of cases it is no more difficult to escape into an attacking position or scramble than it is to get back to a neutral position. Sometimes it’s made easy by an opponent who over commits to the hold he has as you’re escaping (nobody wants to give up a good position that they’ve worked hard for and so they often hold on too long and get over extended and thus easy to counter attack). It all starts with mindset. If you only think in terms of getting back to neutral, that’s where you’ll stop. If you think in terms of escape directly into counter attack you’ll shock yourself how often you’re able to do exactly that with no extra effort or risk.

I am very happy to announce the release today of my latest instructional video MASTER THE MOVE: THE ANACONDA STRANGLE. The anaconda is one of the efficient strangles in the game. It can be used from set positions or in the midst of a wild scramble. It combines extremely well with other submissions from front headlock situations and once you establish a threat with it, will make positional attacks much easier as well. Due to the fact that front headlock is one of the most accessible holds in the game, opportunities for its use are everywhere. Combine this with fact that it’s relatively easy to learn and requires no special physical skills and you’ve got a move that you can use very often and learn relatively quickly. As such it can make a real impact on your game in minimum time. If you’re interested - check out the link in Instagram story!

Only when you see failure as a teacher rather than a defeat will you be ready to build towards great success. In Jiu jitsu you will fail a dozen times for every success you have. You can choose to experience this as frustration that will make you quit today, or as a lesson from which you can build towards a better tomorrow. The choice is yours.

It ain’t over till it’s over: Very often when an opponent does a good job executing a move against us we tend to accept that he got us this time and accept the consequences and start again from the new position we find ourselves in. Good players don’t think this way. They keep countering deep into the move, fighting and countering every step of the way and only concede when the risks of further opposition outweigh the potential benefits of further resistance. The better the player the longer they can prolong the resistance and turn it all into a scramble from which they do not concede a score or even better, turn it into a win. What one athlete sees as a losing position can be another athlete’s opportunity to score. Most moves have a fairly long time delay between the onset of execution and completion. In that time an astute player can play a spoiler game that undermines the ability of the executing athlete to establish control at the end of the move. Here, Garry Tonon uses his leg as an elevator (yoko sumi gaeshi) to counter a takedown and create scramble conditions that will make it very difficult for his opponent to establish any meaningful control after the takedown and thus set up his own counter offense

There is a direct correlation between the amount of time you can maintain a dominant position and the likelihood you’ll finish from there. Don’t focus exclusively on the finishing move - that’s the last chapter of the story. Make sure you put sufficient focus on training the less interesting but no less important skill of riding and maintaining the pin, because that’s the best predictor of whether the finishing hold will actually work

We usually think in terms of attacking with a move that we select. This can certainly work. Once you get to a certain level of opponent it will start working less and less. You need some form of antecedent action that breaks your opponents stance/balance/concentration. One of the best such actions from bottom position and standing position is to attack your opponents balance. This will always create an immediate and highly effective cover for your actual attack. Make it a habit when you’re sparring to think in terms of attack his balance first and employ the move second - this will keep you being successful Mo matter what the level you are competing against.

Let the position do the work for you: When you get a good position in Jiu jitsu there is usually a small feeling of partial triumph in your mind. It natural to feel ant to extend that further and go on to something even better such as a submission. That’s a good mindset, but understand that there’s usually no need to rush it. The onus is on your opponent to escape. Let him do the work while you pin him down. You did the hard work getting to the position, now let him do the hard work trying to get out. When you feel him getting tired and frustrated, NOW is the time to go for the extra points or finish. Understanding who carries the onus of work is important for your pacing and tactics. Sometimes the right thing to do is nothing or very little, until a better opportunity arises, and when an opponent is tired and over extending himself to escape a bad situation, THAT is always going to be a good opportunity

There are few moves that fully encapsulate the ideals of Jiu jitsu technique than a well executed foot sweep. Foot sweeps rely on timing for their effect. When an opponents foot bears no weight it can be swept away in a fashion that completely floors even the biggest and strongest opponents. A big man falls just as easily as a small man when he slips on an icy floor - so too when his foot is swept by an adept opponent. Size and strength won’t protect you from a well executed foot sweep. As such the principle of maximum effect from minimum effort is beautifully demonstrated by these moves, along with the ideal of being to be able to overcome greater size and strength with lesser. Foot sweeps can be a little frustrating to learn when you start out because they either work brilliantly or don’t work at all. As with any difficult task, start with small goals. Get a partner stepping in a predictable cadence and try to disrupt his stepping pattern by brushing his ankle when his foot bears no weight between steps. Timing based moves always take a little longer for most people to learn so don’t give up early if your timing is off initially, keep working until you have one successful sweep. If you can do it once you can do it again. Then it’s just a matter of experimenting with stepping patterns and grips to generate the motion that makes footsweeps possible.

Your main submission holds must be sufficiently strong that at a minimum, they can elicit a strong defensive reaction every time you apply them. Of course it’d be wonderful if every submission you attempted was wholly successful, but that’s not realistic. Just so long as you can force a strong reaction that’s enough to get positive work done. A strong reaction will break an opponents stance and that’s enough to create and sustain an attacking momentum that at the very least will create a winning exchange in your favor and in some cases may lead to total victory. Train your mechanics. Develop a lock or strangle that any opponent MUST respect and even failed submission attempts will still be of value to you

It a lot easier to predict what an opponent will do next if you have him so thoroughly controlled that he has only two or three possible options for movement. Even more so when two of those three are bad options. We don’t have the ability to predict the future, but we do have the ability to restrict an opponents choices so severely that we can know with something close to certainty what his next move will be. Every time you get a really dominant position such as mount or rear mount, spend some time focused not on immediate submission but rather control of movement and in particular, gaining an understanding of preventing some forms of movement but allowing others. This is how you gain the ability to foresee an opponents next move in the chaos of competition. Play with different positioning of your body, different grips etc - each has a noticeable effect on your opponent’s movement. In time you’ll be able to have almost as much control of your opponent’s movement as your own.

If you can keep your mind calm when your body is in chaotic motion you’re already ahead of most of your competitors. When your body is exhibiting maximum stress symptoms prior to a match and maximum fight/flight response during a match, it’s very easy to let your mind follow the same path. When you’re sparring, practice your observational skills. Try to see opportunity and select moves in response to them. This can only be done from a relatively calm mindset and as such, is a way of controlling and disciplining your thought processes as you engage in maximal physical effort. Like any other skill, it requires practice. The good news is that every sparring session is a chance to practice it just as you would your moves.

One of the few certainties you can count on in this game is that your opponent will resist every move you attempt. You can either see this as a source of frustration or a source of opportunity. If you play Jiu jitsu one move at a time, that resistance will be a source of frustration any time you’re evenly matched against a tough opponent. Every move you attempt will be stopped in its tracks and you’ll have to move on to an another move which will probably get the same result. The other option is to pair all your moves in opposing directions, so that maximum resistance to the first move leads into the second move. This means that every act of resistance leads to an opportunity for the second move. This is the way of good jiu jitsu. Don’t think in terms of moves; think in terms of paired moves that work as a dilemma. If the opponent doesn’t react to the first move, he loses. If he reacts defensively to the move he gets beaten by the second. The day your mind makes this fundamental shift is the day you enter into a jiu jitsu you can use forever against even the best opponents

Training tired: There are going to be many days when you go to work out that you’re tired and drained before the workout even begins. If it’s so bad that it feels insurmountable then there is no harm in taking some time off to recharge. In less extreme cases you can still get a very profitable workout. First, how you subjectively feel prior to a workout can be changed very quickly through activity. Very often you can be yawning and feeling dull and a good brisk warm up can completely change your energy level. I’ve had workouts where I felt completely drained at the outset and after some drills and light movement went on to have some of my best workouts. If after the workout you still feel below par, lower the bar of the workout. Perhaps just work on a very specific skill in a specific position. This lowers the physical element of the workout but raises the cognitive demands. In this way you can get a solid days progress even on a day that didn’t seem promising when it began. Compound this over time and it can add a lot more progress over a year than if you’d abandoned all those days where you weren’t feeling optimal.

Whenever an opponent puts barriers in your path - frames, knee shields etc remember this: The more an opponent stacks his defenses in one zone to stop you, the more he must sacrifice defense in some other area(s). There are no exceptions to this rule. Your mindset when you run into stout defenses will determine your actions. Most people will turn it into a contest of will and hammer away at the defenses in front of them. If you’re stronger and in better condition this can work. If your technique is noticeably better than your opponent it can work. The artful player however, will instinctively look for another route through an area where there is no defense. This is always the most efficient way. When you see nothing but barriers in front of you ask yourself “where did he sacrifice his defenses in order to construct this defense?” If you know with certainty that the sacrifice was made, it’s just a matter of finding it

Just as we are all shaped over time by the friends we keep, so too is our Jiu jitsu shaped over time by the people you train with. All of them have something to offer that can help your progress, even if it’s not always immediately obvious. When you train with very gifted athletes it’s usually very obvious how they can benefit your game. When you train with someone with an unusual body type or some exceptional physical attribute such as strength or flexibility, again it’s obvious how this might help your progress. In the case of less gifted training partners you will find that if you dig a little there will be some aspect to their game that presents an interesting challenge that will benefit your game in some way. You will encounter partners who are awkward and stiff, well, so are some of your opponents, so spend time training with them to get used to the different feel of them. You’ll have partners whose game overall may not be well developed but some attribute they possess makes them very proficient at one move - practice defending that one move. Everyone always offers something that can be of use - it’s up to us to find it and make use of it

I’ve seen champions with every type of body and every type of personality. I’ve seen champions who favored bottom position and champions who favored top position. I’ve seen champions who were supremely confident and champions who were terrified before every match. I’ve seen champions who used a vast array of moves and champions who used only a small number. Success is not limited to one type of body, one type of personality or one way of playing the game. Getting to the top is much more about working with what you have already got and finding the adaptations you need over time through experimentation to be successful than it is about changing yourself into a supposed ideal outside of yourself.

One of the last things most people learn in Jiu jitsu in pace control. You can’t drill it, can’t learn it by watching someone else, can’t get a realistic appraisal of it in flow rolling since the intensity doesn’t match what you can expect in a real match - you have to figure it out in competitive sparring. Just what constitutes a good pace varies widely according to the match length and the opponent. Some opponents favor a brisk pace in which case you may want to slow things down, others prefer a slow pace working predominantly out of static positions, in which case you may be interested in speeding things up and creating scrambles. You’ll have to experiment and find your own preferred pace but also be able to adapt pace according to different match lengths and opponents. As always it starts in the gym. Experiment during sparring with pace. Don’t focus only on technique, spare a thought for the pace in which you apply those techniques in sequences. Once you’re cognizant of the issue you can start learning, but until you’re cognizant you’ll never make progress in this crucial area

Momentum: Every competitive match has ebbs and flows between times of offense and times of defense. Part of this is physical - you get into a good position and it’s be physically difficult and demanding for an opponent to escape so you’ll be in command for a while. Part of it is psychological - when someone gets a string of moves on us we tend to go into a defensive mindset that prioritizes survival over offense so we keep ourselves in a reactive/defensive orientation until there is a break in the action. You have to take advantage of these ebbs and flows when they’re in your favor as a fisherman takes advantage of the tides. When the momentum is your favor and you’ve got an opponent on the run it’s time to press the action - keep going! Don’t stop when all the momentum is in your favor - that gives the opponent a chance to recover and develop his own momentum. Learn to read the momentum of the match and when it favors you, extract maximum benefit by pressing your attacks with confidence

Attacking a problem rather than running from a problem: When I began jiu jitsu I trained the same way most people do - I found some favorite moves and positions that I had some natural talent for and spent most of my time working on those. I also discovered many positions and moves that I had no natural talent for and avoided them like the plague! As a result my white belt game was a lopsided and disjointed affair where every match in training was a case of me hoping that I got to my favorite positions and hoping that I didn’t get out in any of my least favorite positions. This approach does not get good long term results. I discovered early on that you’ve got two choices with your problem areas - you can either hide from them as I was doing, or you can actively attack them by studying the problematic elements and training hard in that specific area - this is the way of long term progress. Any hard sparring session will immediately tell you where your current weaknesses are. Then it’s up to you to make the choice. Do you turn away from them or towards them. One path is easy but limiting. The other is hard but will unshackle you from your current limits.

Hammers and nails: If there is one certainty in this sport it’s this. Some days you’ll be the hammer and some days you’ll be the nail. Everything is easy when you’re the hammer but it’s hell when you’re the nail. As a result most people tend to unconsciously (and sometimes consciously) bias their training towards offensive skills (hammer) and sacrifice training time on defensive skills (nail). This is not good. I don’t care how good your offense gets, as you go against better opponents you will find yourself in bad positions. You don’t want the realization that you ought to have spent more time working on defense to happen in the middle of a tough match as you are exhausted underneath a strong pin with no idea what to do next. Make sure you allocate a large percentage of your training time to defense, especially in your early days of training. It may not be pleasant, but like a bitter tasting medicine, it will be the thing that saves you when you’re in trouble.

Good times bad times: I’ve had Jiu jitsu workouts were I felt on top of the world, where every move seemed to work perfectly and new ideas sprung to my head and even new moves seemed easy to perform. I’ve had other workouts where I couldn’t do a single move correctly, stumbled around like a fool and couldn’t figure out a solution to any problem I faced. Most workouts were somewhere in between those extremes. I imagine your experience is similar. Don’t get too upset about bad workouts, they come and go. You’re not judged by any one workout, but rather the average of all your workouts over long periods of time. So when you have a day where nothing goes right, don’t get despondent. Just do a little self analysis afterwards to see where you can improve tomorrow and have faith that it’s the totality of your workouts that count, not the best ones or worst ones.

The greatest need for precision in Jiu jitsu is probably in the application of joint locks. This is the clearest case of the use of levers and fulcrums and blocks/wedges in the game. As any engineer will tell you, the difference in force generation between properly using the full length of the lever with a well placed fulcrum is far greater than a hackneyed application where you use them inefficiently. This is a case where a little extra time to set things properly pays big dividends - so don’t rush the finishes at the expense of precision with your joint locks.

All your grappling techniques from neutral positions begin with push and pull punctuated by movement. As soon as you come to grips with an opponent make the habit of push/pull/move. From the reaction you get you will be able to read your opponent by touch and feel in a similar way to blind main reading braille. When you can read your opponent’s energy and direction through your hands you can apply the appropriate attack rather than some preconceived attack that may not be suitable at that moment.

There are a thousand ways to make a move better - but there is one thing that NEVER makes a move work better and always makes it worse - PANIC. Your first duty in a dicey situation is a mental duty - stay calm - only then do you get physical and start performing moves. Jiu jitsu is a game of decisions. You find yourself in a given situation - you make decisions as to how to act. Decision making is never aided by a mental state of panic. The first prerequisite of making decisions is a mind calm enough to make a rational decision in the first place. You can practice this every day in sparring. Don’t just train your moves - train your mind to remain calm as you spar - because every move is preceded by a decision and you’ll need a calm mind to make wise decisions

Happy New Year to you all! Thank you so much for following along the crazy story of my students progress through Jiu jitsu. Wishing you all the best for your progress throughout 2025 towards your jiu jitsu dreams. Remember always that small incremental progressions over time is what builds a deep game that will prevail against many types of opponents in many circumstances.

You can learn to direct your bodyweight and make top position far more effective. I’m sure you’ve had the experience of sparring with an opponent who didn’t seem particularly big or heavy who nonetheless felt extremely heavy whenever he was on top. This is because he knows how to maximize the use of his body weight. In this way a smaller athlete can feel considerably heavier than a less skilled bigger athlete. There are three keys to this skill. The first is learning to direct your weight through stance. Here I am using a tripod stance to direct my weight in a way that takes very energy but which directs a large percentage of your weight straight into an opponent. The second is learning to use the best parts of your own body to impose that weight on to your opponent. Shoulders, forehead, knees are good candidates. The third is choosing the best parts of your opponent’s body to impose your weight. You’ll want to choose weaker, softer areas of the body or some area that can be used as a lever in your favor, such as jawbone, the diaphragm, floating ribs etc. When you can direct your weight through a hardened part of your body into a weaker part of an opponents body, you’ll be using your top pressure very effectively

I’m going backwards!! Probably one of the most common sentiments among Jiu Jitsu students is that they are getting worse as they’re trying so hard to get better. This is understandable. There are in fact many times when you actually DO get worse in this game. Any time you’re trying to learn an important new move you will begin with a low skill level. When you try to employ it in sparring it will feel like a disaster. As you spend more time trying to improve your skill with this new move, you will have to sacrifice training time with your old established moves and so they will dip in effectiveness as well, creating a temporary period where your overall effectiveness may drop slightly. Don’t be upset. PROGRESS IN JIU JITSU IS NOT LINEAR. progress in this game resembles progress of the stock market. There is a general upward trend over time but this is punctuated with many fluctuations up and down that make progress seem like chaos on a daily basis. Those downward fluctuations can be rather depressing if you obsess over progress day by day. It is very important that you look at your progress the same way a good investor in the stock market looks at progress. They don’t get upset at the wild fluctuations day by day. They take the long view and measure progress not in day or weeks but in years and decades. This how you must be. Like a good investor, only your investment is not in companies, but in yourself and your development; and just as a company can start small but gain great value over time to become a powerhouse despite many ups and downs along the way, so too can you become a jiu jitsu powerhouse if you stay the course and don’t become despondent at temporary fluctuations as you try to progress

The threat of submission will always create predictable reactions from your opponent. Your job is to know those reactions and have a plan worked out ahead of time to each of them. When you know what the next move is going to be ahead of time it’s a huge advantage.

When it’s time to destabilize an opponent don’t just go into it with a preconceived notion of what direction you want to take him. Read where his weight is first and then make your decision where to take him. When his weight is on his heels - take him backwards. When his weight is in his toes - take him forwards. Best of all, use push and pull to get him resisting so you can feel the weight transfer between heels and toes and then make your decision

In a game where sometimes you only have to perform one move successfully to win there will always arise a question as to whether you should persist with a move that has failed several times in the past during a match or switch to another. I’ve seen very tough matches won with both approaches. Some athletes just hammer away with the same move until an opponent goes down with exhaustion. Others switch immediately in response to failure and use an approach based in technical diversity and slickness to win. There is no right or wrong to this. Athletes with a wider repertoire of moves will probably favor the idea of switching to another move. Athletes with a narrower repertoire will probably the persistence approach. You will have to make up your mind based upon your skill set and what you see unfolding in that particular match. For example sometimes you can just instinctively feel that despite some failed attempts, you have what it takes to score with the first move if you persist. Other times you feel that move will never work on that opponent on that day, in which case persistence is clearly the wrong approach. Which approach you favor will determine your whole approach to Jiu jitsu. If you favor persistence as your tactic, train for excellence in just a few moves. If you favor switching attacks, you’ll need to train to develop a broad enough repertoire to make it in competitive sparring.

Every pin in Jiu jitsu is greatly improved by the addition of elbow control. Remember that the basis of the bottom athlete’s defense begins with his elbows - if you control them, you negate his defenses before they can even begin. In addition, control of the elbows is the single best set up to upper body submissions out of the major pins. So don’t just settle for the pin - go the extra distance and control those elbows!

Much of competition success arises from endurance. Pressure takes a heavy toll on even well trained athletes and before long their mind and body will be looking to take a break. If you can keep going when the other fellow wants to stop you’ll win even if he has a better developed skill set than you.

Pressure over time adds massively to the effectiveness of every move: Every move you perform in this game is done against the resistance of your opponent. The stronger he is, the stronger will be that resistance. Pressure over time will fatigue and weaken an opponent. Later in the match moves that weren’t working at all will suddenly start working just because the resistance level has fallen dramatically. Learning to apply pressure over time is thus one of the most essential elements of effectiveness. Only when that pressure has been applied long enough will it become realistic to get your moves working against your toughest opponents. Remember always that you only have to get a good move to work once to win - if you can exert sufficient pressure long enough, the chances of you getting that move to work late in the match is very high.

When the action is fast there won’t be time to problem solve. Your success or failure will be determined by the habits you build over time in the gym. If you’ve been neglectful in the gym, so too will you be on the stage. If you’ve been diligent in the gym, you will be successful on the stage. It’s so easy to walk into the gym and do enough to sweat so that you can tell yourself that you were good and did your workout. If you want to be as good as you can be, you’ll need to hold yourself to a higher standard every day and build the habits that will make you shine against the best.

When your opponent is faster than you, you will need to maximize body contact with him in neutral positions. Body contact always has the effect of slowing things down - the more the better when the other guy has a big speed advantage. Best of all is when your body contact covers both upper and lower body - half guard for example - this will slow down even the quickest opponents. Just as the fastest sprinter will be dramatically slowed if you shackle a heavy lead weight to his ankle, so too the fastest grappler will be slowed down if you connect a big percentage of your body to a big percentage of his body. Make yourself into an anchor and the match to slow to a pace that suits you better.

The closer you get to a match the simpler your understanding of what you want to do to win must become, until by the time you step on the mat you can reduce your game plan to just one or two sentences. Have a general sense of what you plan to do. Anything too specific will often get shredded in the opening minutes of a match where there is so much chaos and unexpected events. A very broad idea of what how you want to engage will survive the shock of combat, but an overly detailed managerial plan will often get derailed in the opening seconds by something you could never have foreseen.

Stay the course: Whenever you’re matched against a very tough opponent who is close in skill level and size to yourself, there will inevitably be times in that match where you are losing. In this ADCC finals match against the very tough Lucas Hulk Barbosa, Giancarlo was at one point caught in a pin straight into a very tight kata gatame stranglehold. If you began watching the fight at that moment you’d assume that Mr Barbosa would easily win. Yet just a few minutes later and the fight joke match had turned around and Giancarlo won via stranglehold. In grappling as in life - a lot can happen in a short time. Some of it will be very bad and some of it very good. Never count yourself out when things get bad. Remember always that no matter how bad things seem now, you’re never more than a short sequence of well executed moves from turning it around in your favor. Train your mind to weather a storm and you’ll be amazed how often you can go from seeming disaster to triumph

Once an opponents body leaves the ground he has VERY few options in terms of resistance to any sweep or takedown you’ve used. You can’t always completely take someone of the floor when sweeping them, but when you can it adds a whole new dimension to the move and make the chance of success much higher. Most of your opponents resistance is predicated on some kind of contact with the floor which they can use to build a base of support - when they leave the mat completely and all contact with the mat is lost, building some form of resisting base is out of the question. So if you can lift them off the mat, do it. It often takes a sweep from good to great.

All grappling control begins with grip. If you can reliably break an opponents grip whenever he is taking a potentially controlling grip upon you, then he will find it almost impossible to exert any kind of meaningful control over you. This is a wonderful skill to have, but be aware that over reliance on this skill can make you excessively defensive in your overall game. Use it when needed, but don’t prioritize defensive grip breaking over your own offensive gripping. Remember, you don’t win by constantly frustrating and escaping an opponent, you win by exerting your control over him. So yes, develop the skill of grip breaking, you’ll definitely need it against tough opponents, but don’t make it the centerpiece of your game or you’ll become excessively defensive.

Consolidation: Jiu Jitsu is a game of action and consolidation. There are brief bursts of action where both athletes strive for a goal. When one of them attains that goal it’s often beneficial to stop in a control position that is sufficiently immobilizing that you can consolidate your gains. This will allow you to recharge your energy levels and figure out potential next steps. Don’t move the whole match. Move to consolidation positions, stop and reconfigure, and then move on to the next - that’s the pattern of Jiu jitsu

Everything you do in Jiu jitsu you do from a given stance. The better that stance is, the better your results. Your stance will change according to the scenario in which you find yourself. These scenarios will typically change often, sometimes second by second. There won’t be time to ponder whether you’ve changed correctly or not. Your stance must be automatic. If you’re thinking about stance you won’t be able to think about how to beat your opponent. The more tasks you can put In automatic, the more bandwidth you free up for problem solving - stance is one of those skills that has to be on automatic. That doesn’t take from its importance - in fact nothing is more important, as all those other tasks are dependent on a solid foundation and that foundation is always your stance. One of the best things you can ever do is watch elite Jiu jitsu athletes in matches and stop focusing on their moves and instead focus on their stance and how it changes from scenario to scenario. Try to carry what you see over into your own training - you’ll soon notice good results

When you can move effectively under an opponent’s bodyweight, even tight pins will have little effect on you. Keep your elbows and knees close together and movement will disrupt your opponents base to a degree where he has to let go of his position.

There is an unmistakable feeling when a move is performed well in Jiu jitsu, whether it be drilling or sparring. It is a feeling of efficiency. Of the maximum result from the least effort with no waste. Likewise, there is an unmistakable feeling of clumsiness and inefficiency that comes from a poorly executed move. For most of us we begin with that feeling of clumsy inefficiency and have to work upwards from there. Your goal in drilling is to work towards that feeling of relaxed efficiency. You’ll know it when you get there. If there’s any doubt, you aren’t there yet.

Work behind your head and your hands: It’s never a pleasant feeling to have to try to attack an opponent while he is attacking you at the same time. Everything rapidly degenerates into a dogfight where the faster athlete typically wins. Instead, you want to be able to control the outset of every exchange so that you can attack without being interrupted by your opponent’s attacks. This means shutting down his offense prior to launching your own offense. The best way to do this is to make food use of your initial defensive tools - your head and your hands. If you can preoccupy your opponents hands he won’t be able to grip and if he can’t grip it’s going to be very difficult for him to attack. If you can keep your head checking his head he’s going to find it very difficult to close distance in a way that enables him to attack effectively. Intelligent use of head and hands will negate much of your opponents initial offense at the start of each engagement. Once that’s done you can focus on your own attacks without interference

The more you attack with angles through the side doors, the more your opponent’s defensive reactions will open up the front door for a direct attack up the middle. When you get advantageous angles, even if the attacks fail, you’ll get a good result insofar as the opponent has to move and react so much to counter the threat that they inevitably open themselves up to a simpler, more direct attack. Next time you’re in front of an opponent, make the move to angle your first move - you’ll be impressed by the results.

If you can follow an opponent through all his evasive turns and twists and stay in good position for long periods of time, THAT’S when your finishing percentages really start to increase. The longer you can hold opponents in a compromised position, the more likely they’ll break mentally. Remember always that in most cases submission starts as a mental state. If you can break the mind, the body will follow.

Put enough pressure on an opponent from chest to chest position and he will be happy when you finally pass just to feel relief from that pressure. Precise technique is good, but precise technique combined with physical pressure is great. When you get tight to your opponent in passing position make him feel your weight by directing it precisely on to his jaw and diaphragm and you’ll double your passing effectiveness

Your whole body working in unison on the attack - your opponents whole body as a target: Your mind focused only how to continuously press the attack into final victory - your opponents mind focused only on how to survive. That is the spirit of jiu jitsu that will make you stand out from the crowd.

The reward of good position is submission. You can get submissions from anywhere if you’re creative and your opponent lacks defensive skills. But when you’re matched against the best, you’ll be far more likely to to get that submission if you preface it with the pressure over time you can create from good position and the asymmetry between your ability to attack him while he can’t attack you.

Identify the elements that are stopping you from performing a move you want to execute and get them under your control prior to execution. This will make it significantly easier to execute that move. Identify the obstacles to move - then remove them - now the move can be performed much more easily. A classic example is strangulation. The toughest initial resistance always comes from your opponents hands and arms. If you can remove them from the equation early, suddenly strangles that seemed impossible become very doable.

The more you threaten an opponent with sweeps and reversals from bottom the more they must extend and post out their hands and feet to stop you. The more they extend themselves to do so, the more vulnerable they become to submission attacks. Play this dilemma constantly from bottom position. Threaten the sweeps, attack with submissions as they post. Threaten submissions to force them to retract their posted hands and feet and their lack of base will make them easy to sweep. Play in this fashion and you’ll always find success.

The positional game of Jiu jitsu has many subtleties but its overall nature is very simple. It prioritizes two positions above all others - the mounted position and rear mounted position. These positions get the highest scores in the sport. Whenever you are sparring in Jiu jitsu, make the attainment of these two positions a priority. Remember always that if you don’t have a definite goal in mind, you will drift aimlessly. Your positional goals are two fold - mount and rear mount. When you see the positional game through this simple lens you always know what direction to aim yourself and what the next step is.

If you can make an opponent carry your weight through the head and shoulders for extended periods of time whilst denying him the ability to do the same to you, you will exhaust him to a degree that the end of the match will look like a formality. When your opponents are in bottom position they want to carry your weight on their legs - you want them to carry your weight on their head and shoulders - the longer the match, the more this respective battle for weight carrying will determine the outcome

The biggest sale of the year is on! All my instructional videos at 50% off for Black Friday sale! Link in my instagram story

Whenever you’re in a tough match you’re going to get tired. I don’t care how much conditioning work you’ve done, what diet you’re using, how much sparring you’ve done - if you’re in a match against someone your own size and skill level and neither of you accepts the idea of losing - you’re going to get tired. That’s ok. It’s normal to get tired under those circumstances, in fact there would be something wrong if you DIDN’T get tired. It’s ok to be tired in a match SO LONG AS THE OTHER GUY IS MORE TIRED THAN YOU. Fatigue is normal and totally acceptable as long as you aren’t worse off as your opponent. Just as it’s ok to have only ten dollars in your wallet when you live in a world where everyone else has only one dollar, so too it’s ok feel exhausted when the other looks like he’s ready to expire. It’s all about relative endurance - how much do you have left in the tank relative to the other guy? So don’t panic because you feel fatigue building inside you as you grapple - just make sure it’s building up faster inside the other fellow and all will be well.

Attack one side of the of the body - your opponent will always concentrate his defenses on that side - but he can only do this by sacrificing defense on the other side - that’s exactly when you switch sides and attack the undefended side. Use this simple pattern and success will find its way to your doorstep often.

We all have a tendency to want to rush the finish. You’ve done so much work to get there and there’s always that concern that the longer you wait the more opportunity for your opponent to escape at the last moment and force you to start all over again. In most cases however, you will generally do better by slowing things down rather than speeding things up. Most of the major submission holds have an immobilizing/pinning element to them - use this to slow your opponent down and also be sure to slow YOURSELF down. Small errors in the use of levers and fulcrums and wedges are much more likely to make the move fail than an overly slow pace. So when your mind is racing with the thought of imminent victory - SLOW DOWN - precision is more important than pace in these situations

Most of your time in jiu jitsu is spent among many people - your teachers, your training partners and friends - but once you step on to the mat to engage an opponent you are alone. Make sure every day you train that you build an unshakable faith in your technique and your ability to manifest it through your physical fitness and strength, because that loneliness will test your confidence like nothing else. Whatever doubts you have about your skills and fitness will amplify in that time before the match and the only thing that silences those voices of doubt is the fervent belief that you’ve done the work required to build the skills and conditioning required for the test you’re about to face.

Keep pushing to be the best you can, but don’t despair if your technique isn’t perfect. Remember, your technique only has to be good enough to beat the fellow in front of you. If he isn’t perfect, your technique needn’t be perfect. As your aspirations rise, your technique will need to rise with it as the opponents in front of you get better and better - but by then you’ll have trained longer and consequently your technique will have risen in proportion to balance everything out.

Commitment: There is always an open question about how hard you should commit to a given move when executing it. You need a minimum level of commitment to get a move to work against a tough opponent and that minimum is often very high. If you go in too relaxed you simply won’t be effective. However, if you are at maximum intensity for every move you’ll be exhausted before long. You have to find a happy medium between intensity and relaxation. The more relaxed the better your endurance but the easier for an opponent to fight his way out of your moves. The more intense your execution the more likely you’ll succeed on first try, but the more likely you’ll tire when a skilled opponent recovers from a series of high intensity attempts. My advice is to develop the skill of relaxation for the majority of time of a match, especially when in neutral positions interspersed with brief periods of high intensity action when you feel you’ve got a good chance to complete a move successfully. Don’t pour your resources into a hopeless project - if the opportunity doesn’t look promising, don’t go in with all your energy and needlessly exhaust yourself. Learn to vary your pace and intensity according to demand and learn to recognize real opportunities from bogus ones and you’ll soon find a satisfactory balance between intensity and relaxation in your game

If you can slow down an opponents movement as you work a submission hold the chances of success rise considerably. The ideal would be to totally stop movement, but that is not likely on someone your own size and skill level. Seek to slow or impede movement as much as you can - the more the better. Excessive movement always loosens connection and tightness of connection is one of the core themes of good submissions. So before you go to apply your submissions, focus on inhibiting your opponents movement as much as you can.

Few things in life are born fully grown and mature. Jiu jitsu is no exception. Every new technique you learn will begin as weak as a child. If you judge it according to the standards of your mature techniques you’ll abandon it before it ever gets a chance to grow in strength and effectiveness and you’ll have lost a chance at overall improvement. Understand that techniques enter your game as we enter the world - weak and in need of nourishment and support. Give them time to develop and they will grow strong enough to win your toughest matches for you. Even the most powerful lion was once just a harmless cub. If you dismissed the cub you lost the chance to develop a lion.

I am very happy to announce the release of my latest instructional video MASTER THE MOVE: THE AMERICAN LOCK. The American lock is one of the first moves beginners are taught and universally considered a foundational move of the sport. If you watch any beginners class you will often see it being applied successfully as the basic versions of it are easy to understand and apply for most people. However, there is a dramatic shift when you go into more advanced classes or competition Jiu jitsu where the success of the classic American lock noticeably drops off. This video is about understanding why this drop off occurs by pointing out the central problems of the classic American lock and then showing very clearly how changes in the way you perform it can make it far more effective against skilled opponents. Once you understand these nuances the American lock can become a much more effective part of your game. We also look at how the American lock can combine with other locks and play a crucial role as a set up to some of the highest percentage attacks in the game. Traditional American locks are applied from top position, but some of the most effective variations are from bottom position. We look in detail at how these can become an important part of your bottom game arsenal. There few better feelings in Jiu jitsu than taking a move you learned as a white belt and which you’d overlooked for years and getting it to work on your toughest training partners and opponents - make it happen by taking a new perspective on the American lock and see what it can do for your game! Link in my Instagram story

Any time you can make Jiu jitsu a battle between the strength of your legs versus the strength of your opponents arms - you’ve put yourself on a winning track. Most of the best submissions in this game prioritize legs versus arms - that’s how smaller, weaker people beat bigger, stronger people. That’s how you can realistically control your most powerful and explosive opponents.

Pins and submission: The biggest scores in Jiu Jitsu come from the most dominant pins. Never forget however, that the highest goal of the sport is submission victory. The pins themselves focus their attention upon your opponent’s torso, but submission occurs at the neck, arms and legs - the limbs. When it comes to submission, pins have little value until you can ISOLATE A LIMB. If submission is your goal you cannot be satisfied with the pin. You must go beyond and isolate a limb in addition to whatever pin you have. Carrying this mindset at all times is what separates those who use pins to score and control versus those who use them to finish.

You’ll never use your offensive skills to their full potential if you’re afraid to take a risk - and the single best way to lower the perceived risk of using any given move is faith in your defense. If you believe your defense is so strong that an opponent has no way to breach it, you’ll take any risk simply because you believe that you can get out of any bad situation that might arise from a failed move. However, if you doubt your defensive skills you’ll always be reticent to take a risk because you believe that if things go awry then you’ll get into trouble that you can’t recover from. So work your defense as hard as you do your offense - ironically it will make you a much better offensive player

Have you ever taught a friend who has absolutely no background in a jiu jitsu a move? It can be an interesting experience. You have to explain many things that we all take for granted but which are not intuitive or obvious to a complete beginner. The first thing you learn is to be patient. You can’t expect immediate success from a total beginner. Instead you lower your expectations and give them extra time to make up for their inexperience. Now contrast this experience with your own experience when you’re trying to learn a new move. When you come to learn a new move, you become a beginner again, at least in that one specific area. So take the same mindset you had when you taught your beginner friend. Lower your initial expectations and give yourself some extra time. Don’t just give up because you don’t get immediate success. It’s rare to get immediate success with a new move. Go back to your beginner mindset and be happy with even moderate success or even the sense of potential for success in the future. Budget extra time for your development - you’ll probably need it if the move is totally new to you. Having realistic expectations about rates of progress and a belief that you don’t have to excel today when you’ve got tomorrow will keep you involved in the development of a new move rather than stop trying the minute you experience difficulties or failure.

When you start to master the use of a submission hold make sure you learn not only how to attack with it, but also how to defend it. It is irresponsible to learn only the offensive side of a submission hold without the defense to that same move. Just as an assassin who makes use of a particular poison must also carry the antidote lest he fall victim to his own poison, so too a good submission hold exponent must be equally good at stopping the same hold being used against him. Interestingly, the more you learn about defending a hold, the better you become at applying it, and vice versa. Every hold has two sides to it - offense and defense. To maximize your knowledge, learn both - to ensure you never get hanged upon your own gallows - learn both.

The more Jiu jitsu is done in the context of fighting, the more control of distance becomes a top priority. You want an opponent either too close to strike you or too far away so fighting Jiu jitsu tends to favor either minimal or maximal distances. The more Jiu jitsu is concerned with pure submission grappling with no strikes, the more it favors the middle distance where you have sufficient space to maneuver into submission holds and positional attacks but are not so far away that you can’t be constantly at grips with your opponent to initiate your grappling attacks.

The more you put an opponent into constrictive, debilitating positions, the more you want to slow things down. Any position that puts an opponent under stress through weight, breath restriction, misalignment or any other form of physical disadvantage/hardship is a position you want to hold for time - the longer the better. Time spent in these situations will exhaust an opponent and sometimes even create panic reactions that open them to new attacks. You may have to move quickly to get TO these positions, but once there, SLOW THINGS DOWN. Let time work in your favor now

Whatever advice a coach can offer an athlete during a match is insignificant compared with the advice he offers over the months and years prior to the match. It’s better that your voice be heard subconsciously inside the athletes head as automatic reactions from years of training rather than shouted out in the midst of a chaotic match. The more the athlete follows his own creative vision in a match and the more the coach realizes that the best time to influence a student is king before the match rather than during it - the better the result.

Immobilization is the key to submission. The more freely an opponent can move, the more difficult it will be to bring sufficient pressure to break or strangle an opponent. Don’t just focus on the breaking mechanics. Make sure you practice immobilizing him as best you can prior to the application of hold.

I had a blast today teaching in Boston at my student @placisantos He is the proud teacher at his new school @5stonesjiujitsu and is doing a fine job of growing his school and teaching the ways and methods of Jiu jitsu. I just completed another instructional video with @bjj.fanatics with Bernardo Faria and Micheal Zenga and we stopped by Placido’s school afterwards. Congratulations Placido on your success with the school! Now it’s back to Austin!

Not all victories are good: I remember an incident when I first began jiu jitsu. I tied a smaller opponent up in a pretzel with a poor rendition of a cradle and squeezed as hard as could on the smaller fellow white belt. He tapped presumably out of discomfort. I was happy to win. To my surprise, my teacher Renzo Gracie rebuked me and said quite correctly that it was a poorly conceived move that only worked because of my size advantage and the inexperience of my partner. It taught me a valuable lesson very early. There can be victory that has no merit. Only victories that can be replicated and would work against someone your own size and skill level are worthy of merit. It’s not just winning but winning in a way that sets up more future wins against increasingly capable opponents that should interest us.

The more an opponent can keep his hands together and in front of him, the stronger his arms feel. The more you can separate his hands and out one of his hands behind his back, the weaker his arms will feel. This game isn’t just about making yourself stronger. It’s also about making your opponent weaker, and the single best way to do that is force him into a biomechanically weak position such as a hand trapped behind the back. Develop a knowledge of the innate weaknesses of the human body and look always to put your opponents into those positions which express those weaknesses and you’ll make significant progress in your quest to level the playing field of strength on the mat.

The gentle art…well…most of the time: Often Jiu jitsu is described as a gentle art where the smooth flowing technician effortlessly defeats the brute. This may be true when there is a massive skill discrepancy but is not true when you’re more evenly matched. For most of the time of a match you want to be as relaxed as you can be to get work done. This is important for your endurance. However, we need to be realistic and say that there will be certain times where something close to maximal effort for brief periods is required. You must be able to transition efficiently from longer periods of relatively loose and relaxed states to shorter periods of high intensity tension. When you’re trying to finish a submission, a sweep, a takedown or a guard pass - it’s time to grit your teeth and increase the workload and tension. Just make sure when you push yourself into redline you don’t stay there too long. Use your strength when necessary - but don’t use it too long. Keep it gentle most of the time but when it’s time to push - PUSH - just don’t push too long and you’ll be able to navigate the need for tension and strength at certain times in Jiu Jitsu

Next: When you have a hard match in training there is that moment when you have to select your next partner for the next round. What choice should you make when you’ve just had a hard competitive round? If you’re getting ready for a competition this is the time to immediately look for an equally tough next round so that you develop the mental fortitude to work through fatigue and still be effective. If you’re working mostly on skill development it’s probably better to take an easier round so that you can slow things down and work on a particular area that you wish to improve in. Let the mission you chose at the start of class guide your decision rather than what you feel immediately after the round and you’ll stay true to your program rather than what you feel at the moment.

Your opponent will always seek to gain connection to you and his primary connectors are his feet and hands - therefore make it your mission to control his feet and hands as much as possible at the onset of every exchange. This will aid you in your attempts to gain connection on your terms rather than his - this is crucial against good opponents as typically the athlete who gets good connection first tends to outperform the other over the length of the exchange.

Nobody has ever arrived at a complete knowledge of even one move of Jiu jitsu. You don’t need to. You only need to know enough to get the job done against the opponent in front of you. As your opponents get better, you’ll need to know more. Keep researching as a higher belt as relentlessly as you did when you were a white belt and you’ll always be adding to your knowledge and evolving. Complete knowledge is unattainable but that won’t stop you from striving to get as close to completion as this life permits you.

Use your head: Admonitions to use your head usually mean that you should think more - but in jiu jitsu you’ll often want to understand it more literally. You should use your head as you would your arms and legs to control your opponents movement and assist in the action of pushing, pulling and setting wedges around your opponent to inhibit his ability to move. Most beginners are shy to use their head as part of their attack. Good players use their head whenever they can to push and pull and block - the more the better. Once you get even halfway past your opponents legs and settle into chest to chest or chest to back positions, a big part of how effective you’ll be at controlling your opponent’s movements will come down to head positioning

The most well known adage of Jiu jitsu is quite possibly the least used. The day you begin Jiu jitsu you are told - “push when you are pulled, pull when you are pushed - work in accordance with action/reaction.” It seems so obvious, so simple. Yet actually doing it consistently on the mat while under pressure is the hardest thing. A coach says and students say “yes, yes, I know that.” They know it as a sentence but they don’t manifest it as a principle. Knowing something and doing it every time are two different things. Next time you’re on the mat don’t focus on moves - focus only on the principle of action/reaction and you’ll see it’s not easy to apply it throughout a match. You may hear it uttered often, but it’s easily forgotten under pressure. Make sure it remains in your consciousness at all times when sparring - that’s when it counts

In some team sports you have separate teams for offense and defense. In Jiu jitsu you go out on the mat alone - you are responsible for both offense and defense. Good offense will take you a long way when you first start out because many of your opponents at lower level do not have very sophisticated defensive skills and a good offense will get you many quick wins. However, as you go higher everyone has good defense and it gets harder to harder to get quick breakthroughs with your offensive game. As the skill level rises, defensive skill becomes more and more the determinant of who wins and who loses. You’ll need strong defense to stave off a skill d opponents attacks and stay in the game long enough to wear a tough opponent down and get late wins. I know defense is not the most glamorous part of Jiu jitsu and doesn’t garner much interest in comparison with exciting offensive techniques, but let me assure you time invested in this skill early will pay big dividends later in your jiu jitsu journey.

Loss: We humans are programmed to abhor loss more than we treasure victory. You’ll forget most of your good days on the mat but you’ll never forget your worst ones. You have to learn to use this psychological trait we all have positively. Loss can strongly provoke us to analyze and analysis gets us asking questions and seeking solutions - which is the basis of all forward progress. In this match a young Gordon Ryan lost to the great Leandro Lo in an exhibition match shortly prior to the 2017 ADCC world championships. He lost mostly due to tactics rather than technique. The loss forced him to assess what he needed to change. It motivated him to improve his positional game, top game and standing game and showed him that it’s not enough to just be good at bottom leg locks and back attacks. You’ve got options when you react to loss. You can laugh it off and keep doing what you’re doing. You can get frustrated and quit. However, you’re serious about maximizing your growth you can ask yourself what went wrong and then start experimenting with solutions so that the loss creates a positive evolution in your game.

Many will watch - few will see: We are given countless opportunities to watch Jiu jitsu. Every time you go to the gym you can watch teachers, friends and training partners engage in sparring. Every competition you attend you’ll see dozens of matches in a day. We all have the tendency to “zone out” and watch mindlessly until the only thing we see is the result. This is a wasted opportunity. Each time you observe a match it’s a chance to train your mind to observe events and make sound decisions. Don’t just watch - interpret the action as it unfolds and make assessments as to what the athletes are doing and what they could be doing better. The better you get at this skill, the better your game will become. Good jiu jitsu is largely the skill of sound decision making under stress. This is a skill like any other and thus requires practice and one of the best forms of practice is interpretive observation of matches. Make it a habit and soon your improvement will show itself in your game.

When you can turn your opponents offense into your offense you add a whole new dimension to a match. It’s one thing for an opponent to stop your move - it’s quite another for him to turn your move into his score. When you can strongly counterattack an opponents moves he will go into a passive state where he becomes reluctant to initiate moves at all for fear of your counterattacks. When you can put an opponent into that passive mindset victory is almost assured. Make sure you don’t limit yourself to moves that stop an opponent but also add some where you aggressively counterattack and turn his offense into yours - success in this regard will greatly enhance your ability to take charge of a match.

Gain a little knowledge and a move that wasn’t working well can become one of your most effective weapons overnight. The single best trait you can possess in Jiu jitsu and in life is a restless curiosity that makes you ask questions and seek answers in the areas and subjects that are important to you.

Anytime you’ve gotten a grip upon your opponent where they can’t even think about offense, but must focus solely on escaping that grip before they can move on - you’re winning. Hunt for those grips aggressively and use them aggressively and you’ll create a situation where your opponent is more worried about what you’re doing to him than he is thinking about what he should be doing to you - if you can maintain that you’ll be winning far more often than not.

Your opponents hands and feet are the primary means by which he can form connection to you. If you can control his hands he will struggle to make effective connections and if he can’t connect to you he can’t control you and if he can’t control you he can’t submit you. Hand control is a big part of this game. When you train always be cognizant of your opponents hands and nullify them as much as you can whenever you feel they can cause issues for your next move.

When you play a tight game you have to find a delicate balance. If you play TOO tight then neither of you can move. That’s no good. If you play too loose, your opponent simply isn’t immobilized enough for your technique to be effective. You have to be right enough to inhibit your opponents movement but loose enough to move yourself so that you can advance to the next position. The key is to make one section of your body tight enough to immobilize your opponent in the relevant areas, but the rest loose enough to move and advance. Here you see Gordon Ryan playing exceptionally tight with arms, head and shoulders in a bodylock pass, but keeps his legs loose enough to move through/around his opponents legs and pass efficiently. It’s rare in Jiu jitsu that total body tension is desirable. Most of the time you tighten one area of the body but keep the rest supple enough to move effectively to the next goal

In a good training room you can make solid progress just showing up every day and following along - but if you want great progress you must bring a clear set of personal goals with regards skill development to every class aligned within a long term plan to acquire the skill set required to attain your career goals. Don’t just show up - show up with a purpose - if you can maintain this over years you’ll outstrip the room

Vision: Your eyes are your first line of defense and the initiator of most offense in all combat sports. They identify threats and opportunities before they materialize. They are the early warning radar that informs all of your decision making. The key is to develop interpretive vision. LOOKING at something doesn’t guarantee that you’ll SEE what is happening. You have to see and become cognizant of threat and opportunity. This requires interpreting what you perceive. Different athletes will interpret the same sense data differently based upon their knowledge and training - that’s fine, just so long as you’re effective with what you’re doing. The main point is always to really think about USING your eyes. Don’t just LOOK - SEE and READ what your opponent is doing - the earlier and better you can do it the bigger the edge you’ll have in a tough match.

The higher you go in this game the more you replace “position before submission “ with “position OR submission.” The threat of submission elicits defensive reactions designed to stop that submission, but which often do nothing to prevent an immediate follow up positional attack. So too, a positional attack elicits a defensive reaction designed to stop that specific positional attack but which does nothing to prevent a subsequent submission attack. Position and submission are two very different skill sets. Defense to one does little to defend the other since they are entirely different categories. Thus one of the most profitable dilemmas you can create is the threat of submission followed by positional attack and vice versa. Rather than simply seeing position and submission as a sequential pair with one antecedent to the other - see them a dilemma pairing that breaks the rhythm of your attacks in a truly effective way.

We learn attacks as a single move, but the key to successful application is to apply them in clusters: Every time you go to apply a move you must have another in your mind to follow up that move should it fail. Just as a good general never commits all his forces into battle at the same time, but always keeps a reserve to exploit the openings created by the first push, so too, the good jiu jitsu player has a follow up mapped out even as he is committed to his current move. Don’t let this way of thinking reduce your commitment to the first move - you have to really make the opponent think the first move is the real attack and really try to defend it - that’s what will create the openings for a second or third move. Tunnel vision is never the right perspective for good jiu jitsu. Keep your mind thinking in terms of the next move while really trying to apply the first move and you’ll be very successful in this game.

There’s always more than one option: It’s easy to get tunnel vision in the intensity of a match where it’s always so difficult even to get one move to work. It’s not easy to think in terms of three or four moves together when even one seems damn near impossible. Yet that’s exactly how you must learn to think if you are to beat your toughest opponents. In the space of a few seconds Gordon Ryan went from a triangle variation to juji gatame armbar threat to American lock finish combined with triangle control. That ability to put all your effort into the move you’re executing, yet leave some room in your tactical mind for the possible application two more more appropriately selected follow up moves is the key to getting past tough defenses

A perfectly executed attack is highly like to succeed against an opponent your own size and skill level. However, a perfectly executed attack is unlikely to occur against an opponent your own size and skill level. Under the pressure of match conditions there will almost always be imperfections that reduce the mechanical efficiency of the move below the levels required for success. Therefore just as important as mechanical efficiency in execution is CONTINUITY OF EXECUTION where you seamlessly go from move to move in a way that lowers resistance level until an imperfectly executed second or third or fourth move can get the breakthrough. Each move will subvert the opponents ability to defend the next. Three decently executed moves in a continuous attack will often outperform a single move executed extremely well against a very strong defense.

Right before the action starts: Understand this: Your opponent is always tougher and more difficult in some ways than you anticipated he would be and weaker and easier to deal with in other areas than you anticipated he would be. Don’t put too much stock in your anticipations and predictions, there will always be some surprises - some will be pleasant and some unpleasant - they never play as important a role in the outcome as deliberate focus on the second by second action and navigating past the problems you encounter to get to your goals.

Making an opponent carry your weight for extended periods of time is the single best way to degrade his skill set over the course of a match. Your ability to manifest skills is directly correlated with your physical condition. Fatigue lowers skill execution. An opponent who has a very high skill level in the opening minutes can realistically be reduced to several levels lower in skill level if sufficient pressure over time is brought to bear on him. Using your weight as a weapon is thus a big part of your development in Jiu jitsu. Practice it as a skill in itself while sparring as often as you can.

In this game of Jiu jitsu sometimes learning a single new detail on a move can take it from useless to one of your best weapons. Every time you drill you get a chance to learn some new insight on a move. Don’t just drill mindlessly. Drill with an enquiring mind that identifies problems, seeks solutions and then tests them in sparring.

Understand where the human body is strong and where it’s weak and you’ll go far in this game: We tend to think of people as strong or weak per se, but each one of us is only as strong or weak as the positioning and posture of our body at that time. For example, the arm of the strongest man is made weak when it’s put behind his back. The fastest man is made slow when his ankles are tied together. Take every opportunity to put your opponent into a physically compromising position and those impressive physical attributes of his will be severely degraded to a level you can manage easily. Deepen your understanding of these positions of weakness and soon you’ll be able to exploit them to your advantage on the mat

When you can stop your opponents movement you are close to victory. When you can let them move but only in the directions you want - you are even closer to victory. When you can funnel their movement in the directions you want and have a trap already prepared at the end of the funnel - victory is yours already.

When we first learn a move in Jiu jitsu our primary concern is mechanical efficiency. When it’s time to apply it in live sparring however, it’s equally important to execute with a sense of commitment. Remember that timidity has spoiled the execution of moves as often as mechanical deficiencies. You must execute with confidence and authority, so be sure to build this attitude into your drilling once you feel you got the mechanical aspects to a satisfactory level. As your confidence grows with a move start applying it with an increasing sense of authority and elan. The spirit in which you apply moves is as important in real world application as the method.

Somewhere between push and pull is where the magic happens: An old adage of martial arts is to pull when pushed and push when pulled. There is much wisdom in this. Any time you push or pull an opponent you will get an opposing reaction. Learning to read that reaction and capitalize on it is a huge part of your development. I would have no hesitation in saying that a big factor in what distinguishes beginner from expert in Jiu jitsu is this ability to play between pull and push and use the opponent’s reaction to advantage. Don’t just put your hands on people for no particular reason. Use them to initiate pull and push. When you feel the response, that’s your opportunity - that’s when you push when pulled and pull when pushed - that’s when doing so will stumble an opponent and take them out of balance and create the openings you need to win.

The more explosive and powerful your opponent is on bottom the greater the need for a wide base of support to prevent being unexpectedly turned over. However, that wide base often comes at the price of control. Since your hands and feet are wide apart on the floor there is nothing holding your opponent and hence a lack of control. Your goal will be to find a satisfactory relationship between your need for a wide base of support and some kind of controlling grip that maintains enough control to get the job done while maintaining that wide base. Trapping your opponent’s head between an underhook and your own head is often the best choice. This does a good job of keeping a satisfactory compromise between width of base and physical control over the opponent.

You can only USE a position if you can HOLD a position: Gaining positional advantage is a big aspect of Jiu jitsu. “Position before submission” is the mantra repeated by many. How successful you will be in using the dominant positions of Jiu jitsu is directly proportional to how long you can hold these positions against strong resistance. Submissions take time to be applied. Time in position is fatiguing to that opponent. The more fatigued they are and the more time you have to set up and control the execution of the submission - the more successful you’ll be. So when you first start getting into good position - make a habit of seeing how long you can hold the position. Don’t always be in a rush to finish. Each passing second in good position is money in the bank for you. So next time you work yourself into a good position set an internal clock in your mind and let it run. Be confident that each passing second is to your advantage and will greatly help when it’s time to build your submission skills.

If you can generate enough horsepower in your sweep you can take anyone over - but if you can break their balance and stance first you can sweep them with no need for extra horsepower. The stronger your opponents stance and balance the more power you’ll need to sweep them. If you can degrade that stance and balance first there will be little resistance when you initiate the sweep. Don’t just perform the move - start with the precursor and THEN hit the move - and understand that the precursor is always the breaking of their stance.

Pressure before submission: The difference between trying to apply a submission on a fresh opponent who is at peak strength and explosiveness versus an exhausted opponent who has been worn down physically and mentally via unrelenting pressure over time is as different as night and day. THE ROAD TO SUBMISSION IS BEST PAVED WITH PRESSURE. Everyone is difficult to submit when fresh, but very few can maintain that when exhausted. Learning to make opponents carry your weight in mechanically inefficient ways for time is the best form of pressure for most people to develop and feeds well into your submission game.

When two people clash in a contest they both want to win, victory will go to the one who wins a long enough sequence of little second by second battles until he gets a potentially fight winning scenario and carries it through.
