In the midst of all that there there will likely be times where you lose a sequence of battles. That’s when you have to be able to hold yourself together and stay in the fight. As long as you don’t get finished and there is time on the clock recovery and reinvigoration is always possible no matter how dark the situation may appear at the time. Remember always that wars are won by winning a series of battles. You’ll win some and lose some, but when you can string together enough wins in a row to break an opponent mentally or physically, the war is won

If you can pin the head and shoulders as you navigate past the legs there is very little an opponent can do in response. All forms of control are valuable, but none can surpass control of the head and shoulders, especially when you’ve got their back on the ground and are past one of their knees. Try to trap their head between your head and your under hook - this controls both head and shoulders and confers real control.

If you make an opponent think your doing one thing and then do another, you’ll always have an advantage. There is more to combat sports than technique. Technique must be housed within tactics and among tactics, surprise and subterfuge are king. Whenever you’re sparring, try to sell your opponent the idea that you’re about to attempt something different from what you actually intend to do. If an opponent has any skills and experience he will quickly ascertain what you’re attempting and counter; but when you can fool him as to what’s about to happen his defense will be in the wrong direction and by the time he corrects himself you’ll have gotten the breakthrough. Subterfuge is a skill like any other. If you don’t practice it you won’t be good at it. So incorporate it it into your daily training like everything else you want to excel at and you will see for yourself how much it adds to your game

Anticipation: Imagine how easy combat sports would be if you could see two seconds into the future in every match. Knowing what your opponent is about to do next before he does it would be tantamount to a superpower. Well, in a grappling situation you can often do this in a very practical way. Learn to listen and read your opponents body as you grapple. Many of your opponents will unknowingly convey to you their next move. Some will sharply inhale before they try to explode out of a pin. Some will tense up right before they go to exert themselves. Most will move in ways that can only lead to a very small set of possible options given the circumstances and thus be very predictable. Most people are blind to these reads because all their focus is on what they’re doing instead of observing what the opponent is doing. If an opponent is telling what he’s going to do next - don’t ignore him! The ability to discern what an opponent will do next is a huge advantage. It’s up to you to use it. Next time you’re on the mat don’t just fixate in yourself and what you want to do. Learn to observe and read your opponent so you can take advantage of what he offers

The worse your situation on the mat the more you must play with a compact body with limbs retracted, elbows and knees close to your torso. Just as you would shutter your windows and hunker down in a hurricane, so too must you hunker down in a threatening situation in Jiu jitsu. The worse it gets the more compact you get. When you get good position then you can open up and extend yourself. But when times are bad it should be reflected in your posture.

The best time to attack is when an opponent is in the middle of executing a move. Most people work in steps. When they execute a move there is a clear beginning, middle and end to that execution. When they complete the execution there is a consolidation period where they return to their stance and a mindset of defensive responsibility. However, in the midst of execution of a given move they are preoccupied with what they want to do and are not so defensively responsible. Moreover because they are moving, any interruption of that movement tends to have a more dramatic effect on them than usual. Thus if you can attack in the middle of their execution of a move, this will be highly disruptive and easier to initiate. Most people tend to get defensive when they see an opponent executing a move and concern themselves only with shutting it down. Switch your mindset to attack and you’ll discover that this is a time of opportunity rather than of fear

I’m filming a new instructional with my great friends at @bjj.fanatics in one of my favorite cities - Boston! I always love New England in the fall. It’s quite the change from Texas and reminds me of New York. My super talented student for so many years in New York City - Brian Glick - is also filming right next door so we are laughing all day recounting crazy stories from the old days in the blue basement! We will wrap up tomorrow and then it’s back to Texas! Wishing you all the best from Boston!

I am very happy to announce the release of my latest instructional video MASTER THE MOVE: THE STRAIGHT ARM BAR UDE GATAME: THE FASTEST ARM LOCK. The straight arm bar (ude gatame) is one of the best moves for a Jiu Jitsu player to learn. While it doesn’t figure as highly as more well known submission holds in success rate, it is much easier and faster to enter into and creates big defensive reactions in opponents that open them to other attacks and gives you the attacking initiative in a match - which is the surest pathway to victory overall. In this video I look at classic ude gatame, my own style of ude gatame, the link between ude gatame and other submissions and positional attacks - all organized into a simple system of attacks designed to present solutions to tough resistance until a breakthrough is achieved. Every jiu Jitsu athlete has to find a set of moves that defines their games and is responsible for most of their success. Ude gatame is one of the best candidates for this! If you want a fast and relatively easy to learn attack that can transform your game in a short period of time - you couldn’t do much better than developing a strong straight arm lock! Links in my Instagram story

When you have attained a position that allows you to relax while at the same time puts your opponent into maximum physical and mental stress - take your time! This is exactly the opportunity to make the clock work for you. The most productive use of time in jiu jitsu is when you’ve created this asymmetry in work rate - that’s exactly when you can exhaust a tough opponent whilst preserving your own strength and endurance. Learn to differentiate those times when you have to get to work and those where you can rest - that’s the key to both to extending your own endurance and reducing that of your opponent

Angle is advantage: It’s tough to win by charging in through the front door. Slip around the side and everything gets easier. The human body is set up to deal with frontal threats but poorly adapted to deal with threats from the rear. Take this to heart. Every degree of angle you get towards the back makes your job a little easier. Make it your habit to get angles early and often and tough matches will get a little easier

All of your opponents defense is based on the integrity of his stance. Without stance there is no effective defense. Therefore, whenever attacking - begin your attack with an attack on your opponents stance and only then launch the attack itself. Breaking through a strong door is so much easier when you unlock it first

There are few clearer examples of lever and fulcrum being used to effect in Jiu jitsu than the classic arm bar juji gatame. Whenever you’re employing a move where it’s obvious where the lever and fulcrum are and how they’re used - take the extra time to GET TO THE END OF THE LEVER and SET THE FULCRUM PROPERLY. This is truly an instance where a small adjustment can double the effectiveness of the lock at virtually no cost to you. Oftentimes in Jiu jitsu delay can cost you - but not in this case. Take that little extra time to set the fulcrum and get to the end of the lever and you can immediately go from a good lock to a great lock!

You have two types of strength. The first is your raw strength as measured by objective tests such as the ability to lift a weight. The second is your perceived strength. This is the strength that an opponent will feel on the mat. This is a mix of your raw strength plus your ability to use leverage and positioning to maximize your use of that raw strength in the performance of grappling tasks. Thus an athlete who performs at a very ordinary level in raw strength measurement can feel shockingly strong on the mat due to excellent use of leverage and positioning. Obviously developing your raw strength to a solid level is very much in your interests, however the increases you get there will be relatively slow and taxing compared with the dramatic increases you can get in your perceived strength as a result of using levers and fulcrums and body positioning more effectively. Next time you go to secure some kind of submission hold or pin on an opponent, take the time to get to the end of the lever and position the fulcrum properly. Next time you’re pinning an opponent take the extra time to adjust your position into a more nuanced hold down that really shuts down your opponents ability to move effectively under you. Don’t be satisfied with the first grip you get. Make adjustments until you can feel the mechanical improvements. In a matter of minutes you can shift the mat strength you bring to an opponent up a level that will shock you.

When you’ve got ‘em on the run - keep ‘em on the run: It’s not easy to get moves working on an opponent in strong stance. Once you do get a strong attack going it will temporarily break the opponents stance and open him to attacks that previously were having no effect. So once you get that breakthrough - keep attacking because that’s exactly when subsequent attacks are most likely to take their effect! So often athletes perform a good move and get a result but then immediately back off just when possibilities were opening up. So next time you get an opponent in trouble - keep attacking so you can keep him in trouble long enough to get a decisive result.

Your first concern in Jiu jitsu is developing the minimum physical capacity to perform the moves fluidly and effectively. It does not matter how much knowledge you possess if your physicality is so limited that you can’t exhibit that knowledge as skill. Once you’ve attained that prerequisite, the next goal is to develop refined technique. If your technique is far enough ahead of your opponents then you’ll never need tactics since the technical disparity will overwhelm them. However, at championship level everyone has good technique so then you’ll need tactics in which to embody your technique and gain tactical advantage to atone for technical stalemate. Physicality, technique and tactics in that order as you start out on the long road towards your jiu jitsu goals

Every time you establish a threat you change your opponents thinking: An opponent who feels no threat from you will approach you with full confidence and have no concern for what you will do to him and focus upon what he will do to you - this will be a difficult opponent to contend with. An opponent who feels a genuine threat from you at every moment of a match will approach you with a spirit of timidity. He will be focused primarily upon what you might do to him rather than what he must do to you - this will be an easy opponent to contend with. Make sure then, that you establish a genuine threat to your opponent at every opportunity - it will have a massive effect upon his disposition toward you and therefore upon the outcome of the match.

The more contact you have with an opponents body, the more control (provided you’re in good position) you’ll be able to exert, but the slower your movement and progress will be. When you less contact, you’ll generally have less ability to control an opponent’s movement, so you’ll be required to make bigger and faster movements to get to dominant positions. Contrast say bodylock passing with toreando passing. In a bodylock you have maximal contact, so you’ll have excellent control but it will come at the price of speed. You’ll have to inch around your opponents guard step by step, often delaying to overcome resistance. In toreando passing you’ll have to substitute speed and movement to make up for the lack of control and so your passing will have a totally different look to it. Make sure you can play both games. It’s natural to favor one over the other, but different opponents present different challenges and quite often an opponent who is very resistant to one style of Jiu jitsu is surprisingly vulnerable to another. So make sure you can play both the slower close contact style and also the faster distance based style - they look and feel very different but seek the same goal.

The single most accessible part of your opponents body that is a viable target for submission at any moment in a match is the head from front headlock situations. In any hard fought match it is inevitable that your opponent’s head will be exposed at some point (and usually several) to a serious attack from front headlock. These attacks are not only readily available but also highly effective and intimidating to an opponent. Make sure you have at least one good submission attack out of front headlock that you trust can get the job done against a tough opponent. This will make you dangerous at all times and give you the ability to snatch the initiative of a match at any time regardless of what has transpired previously

Momentum: A successful attack creates momentum in your favor. Some of that momentum is physical. Once your opponents position is compromised it takes time for him to recover. In that time you can launch a second, third and fourth attack which build increasing momentum in your favor. Another aspect of momentum is psychological. Success gives you confidence to attack again and often creates a negative effect in your opponents mind where now he becomes more concerned about the danger you present to him and often shifts into a more defensive mode. Once you establish a positive momentum - keep it going. Don’t attack and then stop. This will cause you to lose that momentum and let your opponent get back in the game and reassert himself. Make sure you compound one success upon another to maximize its effect on the match

Physical pressure requires sufficient application time to have an effect upon an opponent. If you can maintain it long enough you can exhaust an opponent physically and break him mentally. But how does one keep that pressure on long enough for it to take its effect? POSITION. If you can stay in a dominant position - the best are those where you are past your opponents legs and on top of him either chest to chest for chest to back - then you’ll have a much easier time riding an opponent while he has to carry your weight on his head and shoulders through his arms. This is truly exhausting for even a very well conditioned opponent. Get your position and then USE your position to make an opponent carry your weight in mechanically inefficient ways for time. If you can do this victory will be a constant companion for you.

Most good athletes in combo sports have a small set of SIGNATURE MOVES. These are moves that they use very frequently which they seem to be able to apply on anyone at any time. The Japanese in their wisdom even coined a phrase for this important idea - tokui waza - favorite move. You need your own signature moves if you are to maximize your progress. They form an essential line of progress for your overall game. As soon as you become known for a signature move your training partners will develop defenses. This means you’ll need to increase the sophistication of your application of those moves. As your opponents defense evolves, so too must your offense. This is one of the best ways to ensure continued progress in your game. It’s ironic that a very specific skill can be disproportionately responsible for your progress of your total skill set. Always keep your mind active first in deciding upon your signature moves, and then second, tirelessly striving to improve and add to them so that you can break through even the toughest defenses so that you can write your signature on every match!

Every piece on a chess board has a distinct character. They move in different ways and can attack and defend differently. They have each have their respective strengths and weaknesses. Each has its limitations. As you become more knowledgeable of that character you use the various chess prices more effectively. You learn to match the capabilities and character of the piece to the task at hand. The submission holds of Jiu jitsu are similar in this regard. Each has a unique character. Each has its own strengths and weaknesses. In order to maximize your effectiveness you must learn to match the submission hold to the task at hand. The deeper your understanding of the character of each hold the more success you will have when using them. Just as you have favorite pieces on a chess board, you will have submission holds that you favor. Just as some chess pieces are more powerful and versatile than others, so too, some submission holds outperform most others. The Queen, Rook, Bishop and Knight are ranked in power just as rear strangle, guillotine, heel hook and juji gatame arm bar stand highest in the ranking of submission holds. Just a a chess expert knows his pieces as well as he knows himself and does not ask of them anything above or below what they’re capable of, so too must you know your submission holds and apply them as expertly and appropriately as the chess master.

Lock one of your opponents leg in place and you’ve got a great situation from which to attack - lock both of his legs in place and you’ve near total control of his movement that makes it possible to choose the time and method of your attack at your leisure.

Do you ever get lost in Jiu jitsu? Not sure what to do next? Which move to select? Where to go next? There’s only one answer that’s ALWAYS the right answer to any question in this game - GET BEHIND YOUR OPPONENT AND TAKE THE BACK. If you engineered a game that only consisted of back takes from everywhere and nothing else you could have a stellar career on that alone. Make sure a SIGNIFICANT part of your training revolves around this skill - it will never point you in the wrong direction and in almost every case get moving in the right direction regardless of where you are and how confusing your situation might be.

Damn! You never know who is going to show up in this town! Great training today with @georgesstpierre @izaakmichellbjj and @lexfridman What a trio! Every aspect of martial arts covered with these guys!

When you go out on to the stage your mind will tell you that this is something completely new and different that you’re experiencing. Everything looks strange and unfamiliar and you’re being scrutinized by strangers. Your training takes place in a small gym , among friends and you know the people you grapple as well as you know yourself. Now you’re facing someone you’ve never grappled before in an environment that looks nothing like what you’re used to. This is the time to remind yourself that despite appearance and feeling - this is the same as what you do every day. The differences are superficial. The same truths you live by every day in the gym are true here too. The laws of physics, the principles of biomechanics and the canon of Jiu jitsu techniques and tactics don’t change with the scenery. They are as eternal as the sky. You trust them every day in the gym - in this moment where doubt and fear is all around you - trust them now more than ever and if you can apply then better than the stranger in front of you - victory will be yours

It’s always good to see old friends in town. Georges St Pierre began training with me so long ago and was always an exemplary student who personified the ideal of starting from nothing and through constant investment in himself and the self belief that small incremental success builds over time he became the best. He was an innovator who helped change the sport of MMA into its modern form. The time I spent on the mats and arenas with him will always be a highlight of my career

When you watch Jiu jitsu don’t look at THEM - actively observe and decide what YOU would be doing in their stead. Most people watch Jiu jitsu for entertainment. Take a different path and watch for growth. Every time you watch you get a chance to practice the single most important aspect of the game - sound decision making under the pressure of time.

Every time a move fails after a strong attempt there are two ways your mind can go. The first is to doubt your effectiveness and sense that you expended a lot of energy without result. This will make you reluctant to attack again since you’ve told yourself that your last attempt resulted in failure and fatigue. The second is to feel that you almost won and that your opponent will be much more nervous on the next exchange and that he burned a lot of energy defending himself and that if you try again this time you can succeed and thus you redouble your attacks. The same action and result can produce two very different psychological reactions. The first leads to increased timidity and withdrawal. The second to increased activity levels and offense. Make sure always you keep your mind directed in the positive direction that increases your activity and pushes you forward into the action of the match by controlling your inner dialogue after a setback or failure. As the old Stoics noted - your reaction to events is more important than the events themselves - this applies as much to Jiu jitsu as everything else in your life.

Good times Bad times: Nicholas Meregali sustained a severe injury at the 2024 ADCC. Sometimes an athlete has to make a split second decision in a high risk situation. Mr Meregali was thrown with a forceful uchi mata and opted to post his arm to save position. This can definitely work, but it can also generate kind of impact that results in severe injuries. Over the last week the extent of those injuries became clear - completely torn labrum, rotator cuffs along with joint separation and torn biceps. Today he underwent surgery in Brazil as part of the long process of building back. Jiu jitsu and life sometimes slams us so hard that it’s difficult to conceive of coming back. Be assured that no matter how bad it seems, time and industry can get you back to where you were and beyond. Remind yourself of the old saying that when find yourself having to start over again with your morale at rock bottom - you aren’t really starting with nothing - now you’ve got the benefit of knowledge and experience to better guide your return. No matter how dark things seem in those times, remember some of your past successes and how there were times those once seemed impossible to you but you achieved them nonetheless. You can do it again. Nicholas, remember that in the last two years you’ve beaten by dominant submission this years ADCC absolute champion, over 99kg champion, under 99kg champion along with winds over the absolute, under 99kg and 77kg silver medalists - keep your head up brother. When you get back you can remind everyone what you can do.

“I’m tellin’ ya bro…there ain’t no way these two bums win gold medals…”

It’s a game of details and insights: Sometimes you can work on a move for months with little progress and then a single detail or insight that you pick up, sometimes by accident, sometimes by experimentation and sometimes by instruction, can transform it from mediocrity to excellence. It stands to reason then, that the most valuable asset you can have in this game is a curious and restless mind that constantly seeks knowledge. Without that curiosity you’ll settle wherever you are and accept that level, but when your mind pushes for insight that brings improvement you’ll never be caught on a plateau for long.

Old friends: One of the best aspects of the grappling game is the friends you make over the years. Two people I’ve known a long time from the good old days with @georgesstpierre fighting in Las Vegas are @crissangel who was always a very gracious host when Georges would fight in Nevada and big @thefrankmir who lived and trained in Vegas through this legendary career. Both of them came to watch Gordon Ryan prepare for ADCC when we were training at @10pvegas It was great seeing them again and reliving crazy stories! Frank and I discussed his outstanding MMA Jiu jitsu and his version of the American lock from guard, nowadays often called a “Mir Lock” came up - then two days later at ADCC 2024 the outstanding Australian ATOS athlete @adelefornarino won gold using exactly that lock! It was great to see it action again! I wonder if Frank was watching! 😜😜

In Boston working with some of my favorite people in Jiu Jitsu on a new video! Thanks always to @bernardofariabjj and @michaelzenga and the worlds greatest uke @placisantos of @bjj.fanatics for all the help they give to my students and for expanding the learning of Jiu Jitsu students around the globe!

One of the more remarkable aspects of the recent ADCC World Championships was the performance of some of the older competitive athletes. In a young persons game three athletes over forty years old had outstanding performances and came away with a silver medal each - an incredible performance. All three of them have always been among our toughest rivals and they showed just how effective Jiu jitsu technique combined with good tactics can level the age playing field and create very impressive results! @vagnerrochabjj @cyborgbjj and @lovatojrbjj came out with all guns blazing and battled their way through to the finals! Here I am congratulating our long time rival Wagner Rocha who took a silver medal in the ultra competitive 77kg division. Well done fellas!!

Big Dan Manasoiu had an outstanding World Championship performance at ADCC this year, winning a bronze medal at 99kg. He had two fine submissions victories, a points win and his only loss was to team mate Luke Griffith. Very few big men play guard offense like Dan - he moves like a lightweight into leg locks from underneath - very unusual for such a big man. He showed that in the bronze medal match with a beautiful classical entry into an inside heel hook to win in seconds against a very tough opponent. So often heavyweights are taught to play a different game from smaller athletes. While this is often sound advice I’ve seen some big men who can move in surprisingly supple ways to and perform moves usually expected of the lighter divisions. As Dan showed, you can never completely compartmentalize athletes based on body type - there’s always someone who can surprise you!

Luke Griffith showed the world just how far he has come when he won silver at the ADCC World Championships last weekend and came within inches of gold in a dramatic final where he took down and got to the back of the great Felipe Pena. He won his first three matches via submission. He has a very fluid style for a heavyweight and seems to grapple like a much lighter man. With this silver medal and a recent submission victory over the double gold medalist of ADCC Kaynan Duarte it’s clear that’s he’s arrived as a heavyweight contender. It’s incredible to think just a few years ago he showed up at the local Renzo Gracie Austin gym, unknown and alone as a purple belt who’d just lost his first match at European ADCC trials and now he’s fighting for gold against the best of the best - it’s an inspiration to us all that if we have the focus and discipline what seems impossible can be made possible. Well done Luke Griffith - you made your family, team, fans and South Africa proud.

Helena Crevar became the youngest athlete ever to win a medal and get to the finals in the history of ADCC World Championships competition. It was an incredible journey for the seventeen year old phenom. Winning a silver medal and very nearly taking the final match shows she is very close indeed to the top of the game already and with a lifetime of improvement still ahead her potential is vast. Much of the credit for this success must be given to her parents who have given her tremendous support from her origins in the sport. Every day they come to watch her train, transport her to and from the gym and come to every match, even small local shows to give encouragement. Great credit too must be given to Helena’s early coaches in Las Vegas who have her a strong foundation in jiu jitsu. A good lesson for us all is that it’s not your chronological age or years in the sport that counts in this game, it’s the hours you accumulate in training and skill development that determine your level - you can amass many more hours in fewer years if you’re prepared to work hard, and nobody has put in more consistent work than this athlete. Congratulations Helena Crevar!

Giancarlo Bodoni made history at ADCC by becoming the first 88kg athlete to win two gold medals in successive events. I’ve always said, it’s extremely difficult to become a world champion, but it’s ten times more difficult to STAY a world champion. The moment you get to the top you immediately become the most studied athlete on earth. Everyone knows your moves, your tempo, your pace etc. They quickly develop a game to counter every aspect of your game. Even on a psychological level staying at the top is tough. You’ve already been declared the best, maintaining the resolve to continue hard training needed to repeat what you’ve already done becomes a real challenge. The key is to keep evolving. This requires a genuine love of the game and learning every aspect of it - especially the new skills that you’ll need to keep sufficient adaptation and evolution to stay ahead of the pack. Giancarlo did that in fine fashion. He started work the day after his last gold medal and worked hard every day of every week up to his next gold medal - that’s how you stay at the top!

I saw with my own eyes every day how much this man had to go through and overcome for this event. His physical condition was so bad three weeks prior to ADCC that I counseled him to pull out. He could not complete a ten minute round or work two consecutive days. I sent a text to the promoters telling them to prepare an alternative athlete for the superfight. Somewhere inside himself he found the resolve to push through and fight two of the greatest ADCC champions of all time back to back. He did what he has done his whole career - win. Gordon has had better matches, better displays of technical mastery, but never a better display of overcoming adversity to push through into victory. I saw what your situation was throughout this camp - the world only saw the result. Never has there been a bigger dichotomy between what you had to work with and the result. Well done, brother, well done.

Victory in Las Vegas!! We had a truly great time at the ADCC World Championships this weekend here in Las Vegas. It was a very dramatic event with many upsets and unexpected turns. When the dust settled we walked away with the three gold medals, two silver and a bronze. Gordon Ryan won both his super fights first against Felipe Pena and then against Yuri Simoes - to defeat two such great ADCC athletes back to back under extraordinarily difficult health circumstances was truly a great achievement. Giancarlo became the first 88kg ADCC athlete to win back to back gold medals with all matches won in dominant fashion. South African phenom Luke Griffith won silver in 99kg division with teammate Daniel Manasoiu winning bronze in spectacular fashion with three submission wins out of four matches. Teenage grappling sensation Helena Crevar won silver in the women’s -65kg division and gained a place in history becoming the youngest medal winner of all time in her debut - a truly incredible achievement. There were so many outstanding performances from all the teams and their top athletes - watching it all unfold was an unforgettable experience. Thanks to @adcc_official for another amazing event and thank you all for following the ups and downs of our athletes as part of ours and your jiu jitsu journey!

One of my favorite aspects of ADCC is the being among the gathering of all the outstanding athletes and coaches of the event. This sport has so many remarkable people involved - when they all get together it’s an amazing synergy to be around! The first person I ran into today was none other than the legendary Xande Ribeiro! He has had an incredible career in ADCC stretching back to his great debut in 2003. He will be inducted into the ADCC Hall of Fame this weekend - nobody deserves it more! @xanderibeirojj

Team New Wave had a great workout with our friend @coachcasey10 at his gym in Las Vegas @10pvegas Getting those last workouts in is always a special feeling. It’s always Good to see teammates in a new environment - it always adds something special to the workout!

Settling down in Las Vegas: Team New Wave arrived in Las Vegas in preparation for The ADCC World Championships this coming weekend. I have so many memories of this place going back so many years. My coaching career began in MMA and so many times we traveled here to Vegas and stayed at the MGM Grand as part of the UFC days - great times great memories. Now it’s the very different game of grappling but it’s the same excitement in preparation. Vegas has been the premier town for all combat sports as long as I’ve been in America and I’m thrilled to see that grappling has grown to a level that it now stands alongside the other combat sports in this great city!

Given enough time, a tough opponent will always find the weakest aspect of your game and exploit it. You can’t go far in this game with a weakness that opponents can find and exploit. It’s up to you to identify the weaknesses in your game and begin a program of improving them. It can be mentally hard to do this - but it’s never as hard as the frustration and disappointment of getting repeatedly beaten in one area. So take an honest inventory of your game periodically and ask where the holes are. Where do you struggle? Where do you lose most often? Once you get the answers you can make a positive effort to rectify the problems. Remember always that positive progress in Jiu jitsu comes in two forms - developing strengths and removing weaknesses. Both are equally important but we all tend to focus on the first above the second - don’t make that mistake.

I always loved the painting by Jacques Marie Gaston Onfrey de Breville Young Napoleon Studying. There is much here for a student of Jiu jitsu to consider. The painting illustrates the themes of potential through study and knowledge acquisition. Young Napoleon sits alone at a desk, unknown and inexperienced, but the light throws the unmistakable silhouette in shadow of the powerhouse that he would one day become. It speaks of the power of knowledge and study to transform an unknown student into a world dominating force. Napoleon studies alone, his only partners are the voices in the books of the great generals of the past whose craft he learns from. This represents well the path you too must follow. You’ll have friends and acquaintances, but ultimately it’s you and you alone who must provide the energy and resolve to walk the long road to your future self. You’ll need to find inspiration and education in the work of the masters who came before you, but adapt them to your own purposes and needs, after all it’s YOUR shadow on the wall, not theirs. Have faith if you immerse yourself in that study long enough, your future self can throw a far greater shadow than you thought possible

Attack the whole body: A theme common to all combat sports is to attack different locations of your opponents body in order to break through defenses. The moment you go to attack a good opponent he will throw up his defenses. If you can make him believe that an attack is directed at one location and then launch the real attack at another location you will be able to bypass his defenses. It sounds simple in theory but you’ll be judged by your ability to apply it in practice - so make sure you practice diversion into attack every day in sparring. Make a habit of beginning your attack at one spot and switching off to another as seamlessly as possible. This will make your game unpredictable and nuanced in ways that get good results!

When you gain momentum keep momentum until the next goal is reached: Tough matches in Jiu jitsu involve a lot of time in deadlocks with little action. Then there is typically a burst of action followed by another deadlock. In situations like this - if you build offensive momentum and get your opponent on the run - keep going until you score. So often athletes will make a good initial attack and then stop themselves when it runs into resistance. This is exactly the time to renew the attack and sustain that momentum until you get to a score - then you can rest and go on to the next stage. Even if you’re feeling yourself burning energy - remember that in most cases the other guy will be burning more and will often end up conceding the position if you can maintain momentum long enough.

Every second of a jiu jitsu match offers a vast array of possible choices for your next move. Which choice you make is a highly individual thing - but once you made your choice - don’t second guess yourself. Go in decisively with the choice you’ve made. Moves performed with commitment usually do well - moves performed half heartedly rarely work. Make a decision and go with it!

Specialist in offense - generalist in defense: When it comes to defense in Jiu jitsu, a little knowledge goes a long way. As long as you can see danger emerging, you can start your defensive protocols before the danger becomes too great and get to safety fairly easily. Due to the fact that you never know how your opponent will attack, you need to have at least a rudimentary knowledge of many moves - just enough knowledge to identify a threat and defend early. Offense is a very different thing. Then it’s better to limit yourself to just a few moves and build very extensive and deep knowledge around them, much deeper than your opponents knowledge of how to defend them. This way you’ll be able to break through their defenses even when they know what’s coming. So build breadth of knowledge across many moves for defense and depth of knowledge among a few moves for offense to build a strong overall Jiu Jitsu game

Each move in Jiu jitsu has its own character: The various moves of Jiu jitsu each have a character to them which you must know so that you can align your actions with that character. For example, the armbar (juji gatame) from S mount is a move performed over a relatively long time. It is done from a very dominant position. If you went for the arm bar and failed it would be a considerable loss since you’ve lost a great position. As such it’s a move where you’ll want to take your time and methodically work from step to step with grinding pressure all the way to maximize the chance of success and minimize the potential for positional loss. When assessing the character of a given move you’ll need to take into consideration aspects such as execution time, degree of control, potential loss in case of failure, ability to go into follow up moves should the initial move fail. Soon you’ll know the strengths and weaknesses of all your main moves and you’ll be able to make good decisions as to which one you should select for a given situation.

Precision: Jiu jitsu can seem like a wild game at times. Often your opponents are moving erratically and explosively and it’s tough to get any semblance of control over them. It’s natural to want to fight fire with fire and go just as hard and erratically right back at them. That’s ok for a while if you want to tire them out - but recognize that at some point you need to apply moves in a technically sound fashion with a sufficient degree of precision to make them work against tough resistance - so make sure you build those technical nuances deep into your game so that no matter how wild it gets out there you can call upon your knowledge to end it in your favor

Your first responsibility in top position is to maintain your balance/base. Without that you have nothing. Learning to ride your opponents moving bodyweight is in some ways akin to learning to ride a bike, a skateboard or a wave - you can be a taught what to do but at the end of the day you’ve got to get on that bike or board and spend time just getting used to the feeling of instability until you can ride for long periods of time. Prioritize balance above all - only when that is secured do you move to your offense.

There is always two simultaneous battles going on in Jiu jitsu. The first is the battle to negate your opponent’s moves, the second is to impose your own. Over emphasis of one over the other always creates problems. If you ignore what your opponent is doing you’ll be be fighting for survival every time your opponent attacks before you and it’s only a matter of time until you get caught. If you fixate only on negating your opponents moves without any thought on imposing your own, your game will be too cautious and defensive to be effective and you’ll never be a threat to an opponent, only an annoyance. Get your tactical sense in balance. Start with negation and then move immediately to imposing your will - that way you will have a game that respects your opponent’s ability but doesn’t undersell your own.

Better to have one move that you can apply from a dozen different positions and scenarios than a dozen moves that you can apply from one position or scenario each. Why? Because when it’s time to complete that move against tough resistance you’ll have twelves times more experience to draw upon and twelves times the training history.

Every so often you’ll make a big leap in progress in Jiu jitsu - but understand clearly that this is the exception rather than the rule. Most of your progress will be incremental and barely perceivable among the hard sessions you endure. Remember that many things that happen gradually right in front of your eyes will be difficult to see day by day. Do you notice the passing of the seasons? It happens so slowly you don’t even notice until one day it’s hot and the trees are in full bloom where your last memory was of the leafless cold winter. So too with your jiu jitsu progress - it’s happening in front of you but it’s only every so often that you get the perspective to actually see it clearly.

Just as it is easier for a soldier to escape being taken prisoner if he attempts his escape as early as possible after capture, so too in jiu jitsu - the earlier your escape attempts begin, the easier the escape overall. The longer you delay, the further you get taken into the lock and harder it will be to extricate yourself. Knowledge is a powerful tool here. If you have enough knowledge to recognize an emerging threat early you can respond early - and it’s the early responses that have the best chance of success

One of the most valuable things you can ever bring into your grappling game is a favorite starting position that you can readily get into and from there negate your opponents offense and commence your favorite attacks. You will need a favorite starting position on your feet, from bottom position and top position. In a world of chaos it’s extremely valuable to have a base camp position that makes you secure and gives you strong base of operations into your offense. Moves are never performed in isolation but rather always in a context - when the context is something that your very comfortable with it elevates your game and performance

Follow through: In the application of some Jiu jitsu moves there has to be sufficient drive that you will follow through your opponent as a golfer follows through the ball when he drives it. This is often seen in the case of driving takedowns and most joint locks. To make the moves work against resistance you have to be able to go past the point where the move starts to take effect and keep pushing through for a successful completion. Obviously you’ll want to be cognizant of safety when doing this to training partners. Real world application of some moves requires this follow through beyond the point where the move starts to take effect towards the point where it can’t be stopped

Would you rather have one hundred superficial friends who are fun to be around but are only around for the good times or three friends who are there in good times and bad and who will back you up no matter what when everyone else has left you? We all have this choice in daily life. We have a similar choice in Jiu jitsu. Would you rather have a passing knowledge of one hundred moves that are fun and trendy and which you can use to highlight reel the white belts in the gym or extremely deep knowledge of three very high percentage moves that you can trust to work even in your toughest opponents? History is quite clear. Most great champions have a smaller set of favorite moves that they develop great expertise with and which they can use in any situation and on any opponent. Deep knowledge of a few outperforms superficial knowledge of many. Having a solid friend in bad times is a great thing - having a move that you have total confidence in even in the toughest match of your life is similarly great. Make sure you find those trusted moves - they will benefit your jiu jitsu as a true friend benefits your daily life.

Surprise: One of the most powerful weapons you can ever make use of is surprise. An attack that has no warning and hence you have no time to put defenses in place or a technology that you have no clue how to fight against will always be a devastating attack. Tactical surprise is all about concealing your intentions prior to attacking. Your opponent may know what your weapons are but if he has no warning he won’t have time to put his defenses up. Technological surprise is the use of a new weapon that an opponent simply has no idea how to fight against. Learning new techniques that your training partners aren’t familiar with and learning to cloak your intentions when using the techniques they are familiar with is a huge part of being effective in sparring and competition. This is why it’s important to keep adding to your repertoire and to develop your skill in the use of feints, misdirection, change of pace/rhythm and all the other means of obfuscating your real intentions. Remember it’s not all about technical prowess - tactics are just as important - and among the tactical elements that bring success - few are more important than the ability to surprise an opponent

Your first line of defense in Jiu jitsu will always be anticipation. When you can see danger arising before it fully materializes, escape or avoidance will be easy. When you have to dig your way out of a fully locked submission or pin, it will be an ordeal. This is one of those times in Jiu jitsu where a little knowledge goes a long way. If you have even a passing familiarity with a given move and can recognize the warning signs of its imminent application, you’ll be able to avoid/escape it before it becomes a serious threat. The worst traps are those we walk into in a state of ignorance. Normally Jiu jitsu requires very deep knowledge and skill to get a move to work, but to avoid a move requires only superficial knowledge - so if you want to build your anticipatory defense take heart that you don’t need a vast amount of knowledge about many moves, even a superficial knowledge of those many moves will usually suffice to give you the awareness to be a difficult target. Photo @jordanhfoto

Most of the time I’m always telling my students to relax as they grapple. We all have a natural tendency to tense up a little more than necessary once the action starts and this can lead to premature fatigue and lower the quality of your movements. There is however one time when tight and tense body tension is appropriate - when you’ve locked in a submission hold and you can feel it is well set. NOW is the time for some serious isometric tension in your muscles. Don’t forget to breath and if the hold should prove ineffective due to slippage, back off the tension and switch to something else rather than hold on grimly and squeeze with all your might with no result other than exhaustion. So yes, most of the time, incline yourself towards a more relaxed disposition, but when you’ve got him in your sights - put some tension on and keep it in king enough to get the job done!

Your arms and legs are the connection to your opponents body - but your hips are the source of your power. The arms are the transmission but the hips are the engine. The stronger your hips the stronger your game overall. Understand that dexterity that allows your to place your hips with precision in the most mechanically advantageous leverage points will outperform raw strength - this is the real means of exhibiting the power of your hips.

Everything begins with your stance: There are myriad different scenarios in which you’ll find yourself in Jiu jitsu. Understand this - for every scenario there is an appropriate stance that minimizes the effort you’ll need to expend to get work done and maximizes your ability to negate an opponents attacks and assert your own attacks. Learning to unconsciously get to and hold that stance and seamlessly switch to another when the scenario changes is one of the core skills of the game. The single most obvious difference between watching a beginner versus an expert is in there ability to find a solid stance that changes in the appropriate way during the second by second action of a match. Your first challenge will be learning the stances. Your second will be developing the discipline required to maintain them throughout the entire duration of a match no matter how tired you get and whatever your opponent does to break your stance and the last lifetime challenge will be to do this automatically with less muscular tension so that your strength derives from the efficiency of the stance itself rather than constant, inefficient muscular exertion. Trust in your stance and understand that it is your stance that is the basis of your endurance and mat strength rather than your raw strength and conditioning

There will be many moments in Jiu jitsu where you feel like you’re seconds away from losing. There will just as many moments when you feel like you’re seconds away from winning. Remember this - no matter how bad a situation may seem when grappling an opponent - you are only one move away from total escape and a chance to turn it all around. Conversely, no matter how good your current situation may seem, one mistake that allows an opponent to escape and you’ll have to start all over again. Let this simple insight give you hope and encouragement when things are bad and make you circumspect and watchful when things are good.

When you know ahead of time where the problem will arise as you attempt a given move - nullify the potential problem first - then do what you originally intended. When you go to strangle someone you know already that his hands will be the first line of defense, so begin by trapping the hands and then strangle so the problem is overcome before it begins. When you go to break someone’s arm or leg, you know already that the first line of defense is the other arm or leg, so trap/control that secondary arm or leg first and remove the problem before you start. Attack the anticipated problems first and the main objective second and you’ll break through the toughest defenses.

Opportunity doesn’t wait: The best attacks in Jiu jitsu are a marriage of opportunity and good technique selection to match that opportunity. A big part of your journey towards Jiu jitsu excellence is learning how to be fast enough to take advantage of opportunities within the appropriate time frame, but slow/poised enough to execute well and stay within your energy expenditure limits. Jiu jitsu doesn’t require that you be the fastest fellow in the room - but it does require that you avoid procrastination. When the opportunity presents itself - go!

Build from one advantage to another: One of the central tenets of Jiu Jitsu is that every attack should be preceded by some form of prior advantage that makes the attack more likely to succeed. You must put this principle into operation from the very start of every engagement you make. Don’t just come in and lackadaisically grip your opponent and see what happens. Have a clear sense of what you want to accomplish and how you’re going to do it. Maneuver into positions that give you small measure of advantage before you even grip and then build from there. A series of cumulative advantages and resultant gains will soon lead to a big advantage, but understand that you start small and work your way to big. Don’t dismiss those small early advantages - they may not seem like much but they add up to something big.

If your opponent is out of balance then being under their weight is no impediment to you. If you can interrupt their balance there is nothing they can do to you until their balance is recovered - and in that brief time there is a LOT you can do to them. Once you get a grip on your opponent, step one is to attack their balance - everything else you plan to do becomes easier when you start with this.

Committing to a move: In jiu jitsu moves that almost worked don’t count. A move has to be taken from start to completion if it is to have an impact on the match. Timidity is thus not a virtue when performing moves against resistance. Even if you are using a move to set up another, there has to be sufficient commitment to its application to get a real reaction out of your opponent to set up the following move. Going into a move half heartedly actually makes you quite vulnerable to a counterattack since you have to sacrifice your defensive elements in order to enter into offense. If the opponent is not threatened by your move he can simply counter attack in the middle of it and easily score. So when it’s time to go - GO!! Enter the move with positive force and elan you’ll have a much better chance of success. Don’t hang back and second guess yourself - now is the time to believe in the skills you’ve worked so hard on and go in with commitment

When you can bind up an opponents head and arm with your legs you put yourself in one of the very best control/submission positions in the sport. Any form of head/arm control is good, but performed with the legs it’s even better due to the strength discrepancy in your favor. There are many situations you can readily use this - side pins and turtle situations are the most common.

There are many forms of physical control in Jiu jitsu - positional control, balance control, grip control - the list is long; but the single most important form of physical control is control over an opponent’s movement. If you can achieve this, victory is just a matter of time. What you will find is that control over an opponent’s movement is really the sum of all the other forms of control added together. It is the final ideal of control that you must seek.

Sometimes the game feels like an upside down topsy turvy game of twister leaving you with no idea where to go next. Whenever you feel like this you need to make use of default options. This is the choice you make whenever the next step is unclear. The default option in Jiu jitsu is almost always THE BACK. You can never go wrong by choosing the back as your next goal In Jiu jitsu. The back is always a correct answer to every problem in the sport. Like a pole star guiding lost travelers in the night to their destination - make the back your pole star and navigate your way to there from everywhere and you’ll never lack direction in jiu jitsu again

Your whole body versus their one limb: One of the essential principles of Jiu jitsu is to create temporary physical advantage by seeking to use their strength of your entire body against a single isolated limb of an opponent. The idea is simple but profound. An opponents whole body may well be stronger than your whole body - but one of his legs or one of his arms is certainly not stronger than your whole body. If you can create a temporary strength advantage over an important part of your opponent’s body - a knee, an elbow, a neck - then you can overcome an overall strength disadvantage by creating a temporary localized strength advantage in your favor and you can hold it long enough to end a match. This is one of the theoretical underpinnings of Jiu jitsu - make it the centerpiece of your approach to the game

Any hold that utilizes your legs against your opponents arms, shoulders and head will be a winning formula. No matter how much bigger and stronger your opponent is, his arms are not stronger than your legs (if they are you should be fighting him with a shotgun rather than empty handed 😂😂). Have faith in one of the most foundational principles of Jiu jitsu - the lower body is always stronger and possessed of greater endurance than the upper body - so as much as possible fight your fight pitting your legs against your opponents arms and more often than not you’ll prevail

The back is the single biggest positional target on the human body - and the best - aim for it it every chance you get. Whenever there is confusion or lack of direction in your mind as you engage in a tough match - seek the back is always the answer.

As a student of Jiu jitsu, never lose sight of this fundamental truth. More than anything else you must become a student of the strengths and weaknesses of the human body and mind. Every problem you face in the sport, every mystery you have to solve and every barrier you have to overcome will come down to learning how to maximize the strengths of your body and mind and exploiting the weaknesses of your opponents body and mind. An artists medium is paint and canvas, a sculptor uses stone and chisel, a physicist the language of mathematics - a martial artist’s medium is body and mind.

I am very pleased to announce the release of my latest instructional video for BJJ Fanatics MASTER THE MOVE: ARM DRAGS. This new series MASTER THE MOVE is based around taking a single important/high percentage move that can have a significant impact on your game and exploring every aspect of it so that you can quickly develop the kind of depth and proficiency you’ll need to get it to work against your toughest opponents. Remember always that the pattern of offensive excellence in Jiu jitsu is always to show great depth of skill with a few trusted moves that you can use successfully even when opponents are expecting them simply because your knowledge and skill in executing them is greater than an opponents ability to stop them. MASTER THE MOVE is based on this key insight. Learning to develop your arsenal of favorite moves is the most important phase of developing your offensive game. My students have gotten great results with arm drags in top level competition. We have a quite different approach to arm drags both on the ground and standing that complements very well the more classical approaches. They’ve worked very well for us - I’m certain they can work extremely well for you too! Links in my Instagram story

Start with a plan: Every time you come toward your opponent from a neutral position there are myriad opportunities for both of you. One possible approach is to let your opponent initiate and react to that. Many athletes do very well with that approach. I generally prefer to see athletes take a more proactive approach, particularly in matches with a shorter time limit. Map your plan out BEFORE you make contact and you’ll have an immediate sense of direction that will put you in the drivers seat from the start. Of course you’ll still need to be able to adapt and improvise in response to rapidly changing circumstances since we never know exactly how an opponent will respond to our moves, but as a general rule it’s better to have an opponent of equal size and skill level reacting to you than rather than you to him, especially in matches of shorter duration

Happy Fourth of July! Wishing you all the best from Austin Texas! Keep working towards your goals. Remember always that even a single insight or item of knowledge added a day, even a single step closer to your goals, even a minor addition or refinement of your repertoire, performed day after day will compound over time and take you to something great no matter how unpromising you may feel today. Time and continuous, planned effort can transform you - keep going 🇺🇸🇺🇸

Some moves come naturally…others…not so much: I’m sure you’ve had the pleasant experience of learning a new move and everything just felt smooth and natural from the start and within a short timeframe you were applying the move successfully in sparring against good people. I’m equally sure that you’ve had the unpleasant experience of learning a new move and feeling like it’s damn near impossible for you to even demonstrate it satisfactorily forget about employ it on a fully resisting opponent. Don’t get too discouraged by this. I’ve had plenty of moves that initially felt absolutely hopeless when I first learned them. My experience was that typically persistence brought the level of these difficult-to-learn moves up to a satisfactory level. They never felt like a favorite move but they were good enough to get reactions out of opponents and lead into my favorite moves - and that’s good enough to make the struggle to learn them worthwhile! Remember - a your execution of a given move doesn’t have to be perfect to be effective. It only has to be better than your opponents ability to defend it. An imperfect move often slips past an imperfect defense

Whenever you’re operating underneath an opponent you will need space in which to move effectively under their bodyweight and pressure. This will require you to emplace frames with your arms and legs, and in particular your knees and elbows, so that your torso can move freely inside that space you’ve created. Building the habit of forming frames is a huge part of the bottom game. Make sure you adapt your frames to the moment by moment changes in your opponents body positioning - a frame typically works well in one scenario but is often ineffective in another - so keep them moving and changing in accordance with the changes you see unfolding in front of you

Vary your pace: People often ask if there is an optimal pace for their game. If you look at world champions you will see that there is great variation among the best. Some are blindingly fast, others very slow moving - both approaches produced champions. When it come to pace I always suggest you work at a pace where you can accurately recall most of the techniques you employed in the course of your sparring round and can last the full time of the round without exhaustion, but you’re still fast enough that you can follow an opponent’s movement and successfully complete moves. If you can’t remember a thing that happened and/or you can’t make it to the end of the round, chances are you’re going too fast for your mind to comprehend what was happening and you’re just operating on instinct leading into physical exhaustion. If you are simply too slow to get into position to execute moves then you need to speed things up a little. Experiment with pace until you find a satisfactory compromise between these elements

Keep searching for knowledge. Even at the highest levels of the sport a single new insight can bring about a performance increase that has a significant impact on your capabilities. A move you’ve been doing for years can be transformed with the addition of a single item of useful information. The minute you close yourself off from new knowledge and adopt the belief that you know everything there is to know about that move you nail the door shut on performance increase.

When it comes to progress in this game there are two mindsets that are at odds with each other but which you must hold simultaneously. The first is always a constant demand for the fastest possible progress seeking always to add new elements to your game that can improve performance. The second is realization that all progress takes time and that you need to be realistic about how fast skills can be built. Learning to balance the demand for progress at the fastest possible rate with the fact that all progress takes time to develop is a key to sustained development over time. Too much demand for new skills creates incomplete skills and an unsettled game. Too much acceptance of the notion that all skill development takes time often leads to complacency that everything will be alright at the end and a lack of daily drive that results in needlessly slow progress. Balance then together - be demanding in your pursuit of new skills but realistic about how long it will take to master them

A comfortable opponent is a dangerous opponent. The single fastest way to make an opponent uncomfortable is to break their balance. The second you do so their demeanor changes to a scramble to get back in balance. When someone stands over you or in front of you, breaking their balance is a very high priority indeed. If you can reliably do this, then they will never be comfortable enough to methodically work against you but instead must scramble from engagement to engagement always a second or two behind you and in danger from all your attacks.

So much of this game is about staying defensively responsible by maintaining a compact stance to reduce vulnerability to submissions and scores. The converse is very important for your offense. If you can get an opponent extended you greatly increase your chances of successful submission or score. Learning to get opponents knees and elbows out and away from their torso is a truly key skill in Jiu jitsu

Every so often in Jiu jitsu you make a big jump in performance level in a short time frame. This is usually caused by the adoption of a new technique that you have a natural affinity for and which gives you great results. Most of the time however, your progress will be barely noticeable cumulative additions to existing skills that accrue over time. Let those small accumulations compound over time and one day you’ll surprise yourself with how far you’ve come without ever realizing it in daily practice

When you go to make contact with an opponent it helps a lot to have a clear sense of purpose. You have a sketch in your mind of what you want to do and how you will do it. However, the reality of any combat sport is that chaos can break out any second and in a moment all your fine plans can go to hell and you will have to immediately adapt second by second to circumstances you never anticipated. This means that within you must cultivate two mindsets. One of the tactician/technician when things go well, the other of the improviser/ adapter/survivor when things go badly. Too much of the perfect planner mentality can sink you when things go awry. Too much of the improviser/survivor can turn what should be easy matches into needless wars with sub optimal outcomes.

One of the main functions of pinning is to create asymmetric work rates. Your pins should create a situation where an opponent has to work much harder to escape the pin than you have to work to maintain the pin. Even if he escapes, his energy levels will be degraded to an extent where his performance will drop until he recuperates. Don’t get frantic - you’re winning already - every passing second works in your favor.

Helena Crevar gave another winning performance tonight here in Austin at a great local show @main_character_jiujitsu where she won the 145 pound title in less than two minutes via stranglehold. It’s great to local grappling getting so strong - there were many outstanding matches tonight. Now it’s back to training in preparation for the ADCC World Championships!

What speed should you go when sparring? When a group of beginners begin sparring they typically go as fast and hard as they can - using speed to make up for their lack of control. When you see two experts sparring they will use speed in conjunction with tactics - when they feel it’s in their best interest to slow things down, they go slow; when they feel it’s in their interest to speed things up, they increase the pace. If you’re unsure what speed you should be working at when you begin sparring I would make two suggestions. First, don’t exceed a speed that you can maintain for the full duration of the round. Nobody is impressed by someone who goes at full blitz speed for two minutes and then can barely move for the next six minutes. Pace yourself for the full duration of the round. Second, don’t exceed a speed where you can make sound decisions. If the speed of your body exceeds the speed of your sound decision making - problems are around the corner. Follow these two simple recommendations and you’ll soon find a good working speed to operate at for most of your sparring rounds.

You will rarely ever feel you’re making significant progress in this game: You are in a sport where you train alongside everyone else in a room where you all learn the same techniques and strategies and employ the same training methods. The direct implication of this is that you will all progress at roughly the same rate over time. This means you can go for considerable periods of time feeling like you’re not getting any better at all. Don’t be dismayed by this. It is a natural consequence of working in a room together. Have faith that it’s well nigh impossible to work hard over time in a decently organized manner without making any progress. Have faith too, that learning rates generally change over time. Partners that started long before you seem so advanced that you’ll never catch up, but don’t worry, your progress at the start will be higher per unit of time than theirs so it is indeed possible to catch up and surpass people who who started well before you no matter how unlikely this may seem today. Keep working diligently and just as a small some sum of money can grow over time into a fortune by adding regularly to over time and letting compound interest work its magic, so too can small additions to your knowledge and skill compound over time into Jiu Jitsu excellence.
