He won against the extremely powerful Australian grappler Josh Saunders via outside heel hook after a period of positional dominance. This year has been a very difficult one for Mr Ryan as his serious long term stomach illness became even more severe. This was a test run before ADCC and despite all his troubles, Mr Ryan was able to take a decisive win. The event overall was excellent with very strong team performances from ATOS Jiu jitsu, B Team, AOJ Jiu Jitsu and Six Blades Jiu jitsu - but in the end it was the return of the King that brought the sold out crowd to the arena and he showed again his remarkable ability to shrug off severe physical problems and show his unparalleled skills. Hope you all enjoyed the show!!

Gordon Ryan returns to professional grappling tomorrow night in Austin in the WNO @flograppling event in a fifteen minute match against the powerful Australian ADDC trials champion Josh Saunders. Mr Saunders trained alongside us for the last ADCC where he gave a great performance against the great Felipe Pena and showed the world he belonged in the big leagues. He is a very physically powerful man with great speed for a heavyweight. This will be a tough test for Mr Ryan as he tries to prove he is fit for the upcoming world championships after battling against a recurring severe stomach illness most of the year. There are many other excellent matches in the card - it’s the last big show prior to the world championships so many of the biggest names of the sport will be using it as part of their final preparations for ADCC and CJI events - hope you all enjoy the show!

It’s not just a matter of weight: Top pinning pressure is one of the keys to high level Jiu jitsu performance. Most people associate pressure with physical weight. Weight certainly does play a role in how much pressure you can generate from top - there’s a reason why there are weight divisions in all grappling sports after all. However, even a heavyweight on top of you doesn’t feel so bad provided you can maintain a stance/posture that enables you to bear weight effectively. What kills you is when an opponent breaks your stance/posture and THEN applies his weight on you when your body is so poorly positioned that you can’t bear the weight efficiently. So when you’re pinning out your primary focus on getting your opponent extended and open and only then will your weight be truly effective in creating the pressure you seek

Make sure your pins drain your opponent of energy and will. Just as a good boxer saps his opponent of energy by punching repeatedly to the body so that later he can finish with a KO blow to the head; so too a good grappler uses pins to exhaust an opponent and finish with a well applied submission hold to the arms legs or neck. Don’t just see pins and a means of scoring points, but also as a vital component of a war of attrition that culminates in submission

When submission and position become one: Jiu jitsu is usually understood in terms of two elements - position and submission - and in most cases position comes first. Quite often athletes tend to specialize in one over the other. Some are predominantly positional players who win by points: others are submission hunters who win via submission and sometimes even have disdain for positional scores. The situation that unifies those two elements is the rear mount. It is the highest scoring position in the sport (tied with mount) and the at the same time the launchpad for the number one submission in the game - the rear strangle. Make sure you look to emphasize this unity of position and submission through rear mount and rear strangle - no other aspect of Jiu jitsu does such a fine job of unifying the two primary elements of the game in such a winning way

Victory in England!! Helena Crevar continued her winning way with a unanimous decision victory after several powerful heel hooks and rear mount strangle attacks in @polaris_professional grappling to win the 70kg title and belt - an impressive feat as this is above her weight class against an established champion. Now it’s back to Austin to continue preparation for the upcoming ADCC world championships which now has three female weight divisions and a first ever female absolute division! Congratulations Helena - keep up the good work!

It’s much easier to refine an existing move than learn a new one: when it’s time to make fast progress we usually think in terms of learning new moves. The fastest improvements however, usually come from improvements to moves you already employ. The difference between the successful execution of a move and an unsuccessful one is often very small - so just a few extra details or additions can make a substantial difference to your performance. Due to the fact that you’re already familiar with the move, these additions are usually easy to make. Starting from scratch with an entirely new move takes far more time - thus Refining an existing move often garners better short term results than adding a brand new one.

When a technique has an effect and breaks through there will be an immediate period afterwards where you have two choices - CONSOLIDATION or EXPLOITATION. There are pros and cons to both. Consolidating is usually the safer option since it holds the match in the situation where you’ve just achieved a significant breakthrough and ensures you don’t lose what you’ve just gained. Exploitation entails immediate forward movement after your score. The advantage of this is that your opponent will usually be discombobulated and often easier to score on for a short period after a successfully applied move. The disadvantage is that your own stance is often compromised as well and the resultant chaos can sometimes result in a total loss of position in your moment of triumph - never a pleasant thing. Experiment with both - they are very different approaches - risk averse personalities tend to be attracted to the consolidation approach - maximizing personalities tend to favor the exploitation approach - see which one you favor

Have a clear sense of what you want to accomplish next as you grapple. If you’re in a bad position, you need an eye for what the first action required for your escape must be. If you’re in a good position you’ll need a sense for the first action required to start an offensive chain that can break through your opponents defenses. If you’re in a neutral position you’ll need to identify a viable first action that can set up an entry into offense. Remember - everything starts with a small first step - but until that first step is taken - nothing happens - so work on identifying that first step quickly regardless of where you are - that way you’ll always be progressing quickly towards your bigger goal

To restrict movement - maximize connection and application of bodyweight. To create movement maximize - space and distance. To control - restrict movement. To escape - create movement. Keep this simple guide in mind as you spar and you’ll never be confused as to how you should be interacting with your opponent second by second on the mats.

Finding a favorite move: The higher you go in Jiu jitsu the more it becomes a deeply personal sport. When you start out you have to try and learn the foundations - you don’t have any say in what the fundamentals are - they’re the same for everyone so you learn EVERYONE’S game. Once you’ve passed that level it’s time to learn offense you learn YOUR game. You have to acquire a small set of favorite moves - the Japanese call them “tokui-waza” These will be the main means by which you fight for victory. What choices you make will depend mostly on your body type, your personality and your coaching heritage/lineage. Your choices can change and evolve over time, but it’s crucial that once you’ve become competent in the foundations that you start the search for your favorite moves and start gaining a deep knowledge of their use - how competent you become in them will determine how far you go in the sport.

Variation of speed and intensity: A very common error among people new to Jiu Jitsu (and even among many who are not so new) is the tendency to begin a match at a given intensity level and speed and maintain it as long as they can until they either win, lose or get exhausted and stop. Usually this results in fatigue in a short period of time. You must learn to vary the intensity and speed to a level appropriate to the second by second changes of the match. You don’t need to squeeze at maximum intensity for more a few seconds in most matches. For the majority of most matches you can be a surprisingly relaxed state, interspersed with a few brief periods of very high intensity and some others of moderate intensity. You can’t be overly relaxed all the time or you simply won’t be effective, but you can’t be squeezing like a maniac all the time either as you’ll soon be exhausted. Learning to vary intensity to situations is one of the biggest developments you must make and represents a significant step up in your sophistication as a grappler

I always encourage athletes to train every day that they can - but I don’t encourage them to train as hard as they can every day. Learn to switch up the intensity levels when training. Every time you step on the mats you have an opportunity to improve your skills, so the more times you do so, in general the faster your progress; but maximum intensity training is very arduous and leads quickly to injury and exhaustion. Learning to lower the intensity allows you to increase the volume. Sometimes you need a hard workout, conversely, sometimes you need a softer workout. Learning to vary the intensity allows you to increase training volume and that’s a key part of progress

Advantage gained is only useful if that advantage is kept and used: Jiu jitsu is a game of accumulating some form of advantage, be it positional, grip, balance or whatever, and then exploiting that advantage for further gain. When you see two Jiu jitsu athletes sparring you will see then immediately and constantly seeking to gain some form of advantage over the other. Understand always however, that you can only use that advantage effectively if you can hold it long enough to do something constructive with it. So GAINING advantage is only half the battle - KEEPING advantage long enough to do something more is the other half. Make sure every time you move into a good position you set your mind and body for the new challenge of KEEPING what you’ve gained - don’t let complacency result in losing immediately what you’ve worked so hard to acquire

Work step by step to your goals: The application of Jiu jitsu technique mirrors life. You never go straight into a completed move or hold. The application of moves is a process, not an event. It begins with a set up, then an entry, then series of adjustments towards a completed technique and finally completion. Like all processes the key is to avoid skipping steps or rushing through the steps faster than allows proper application. Take this approach and you’ll soon find your techniques having more effect in sparring and competition

If you can undermine an opponents balance you will create a temporary situation where his only thought is to recover that balance. It’s in that moment that he will not be focused on defense and therefore much easier to attack than would normally be the case. Defense requires a solid base. An opponent with a broken base of support must first stop whatever he is doing and recover his base of support - that’s exactly when your attack is most likely to succeed

When in positional trouble keep moving, but not frantically. When someone gets a strong position on you it’s natural to feel some concern. Most people understand that staying still will make a bad situation worse as you spend lingering and longer in bad position an opponent can advance uncontested to an even better position or a submission hold. Too often athletes think that the opposite approach must therefore be beneficial and engage in frantic mission to escape. This often creates big openings to a skilled opponent to get to better positions and tires you out without reward. Motion is indeed necessary, but don’t make it frantic. Keep moving in a purposeful manner towards a specific goal. It’s unlikely the first escape attempt will work on a good opponent, so be ready to smoothly transition to the next until sufficient space develops to complete an escape. Just the action of continuous movement towards a goal will make you a difficult opponent to control and greatly facilitate your eventual escape attempt. Then you even create situations where the attacker becomes more fatigued than you as he tries to complete a score.

Every match is won and lost one move at a time. Whenever you’re excessively worried before a match or very tired during a match, it’s natural to feel anxiety. That anxiety can freeze you and make you ineffective. Whenever you’re heading down that path just focus on what will be your next move. Too much concern about the outcome or your opponent never helps performance. The only thing that helps performance is a well executed and well chosen next move - so let that and that alone be your focus.

The best submissions always offer several different paths to submission from the same hold. Single option submissions will work up to a given level and then stop working, but when you can play between two or three options and keep an opponent guessing as to which option you’ll take, it will break through against even the toughest defenses. Given a choice, favor those submissions that offer multiple paths to victory.

Victory in Austin! Teenage grappling prodigy Helena Crevar was victorious tonight in a grappling bout against talented UFC fighter Mariya Agapova at the Austin TX @karatecombat event tonight. She won via heel hook in thirty seconds, showing once again her impressive leg lock development and her submission hunting style! It’s a great show set in a modern style pit. Now they’re into the karate bouts - it’s impressive how modern karate has evolved along much more MMA inspired lines and become very effective indeed as a basis for MMA. She will compete again this weekend at the IBJJF World Championship - that’s the kind of competition schedule that creates great champions!

Good stance can make a weak person strong and poor stance will make the strongest person weak. For every scenario in the sport there is a stance that most efficiently corresponds to it. The further you deviate from that ideal stance the more your muscles will have to compensate and the more fatigued you’ll become. Remember always that in the mat you’re only as strong as your stance - so do everything you can to train yourself to maintain it throughout the entire duration of a match

Going first or going second: Combat sports have always been split between those who actively initiate action and look to proactively control the flow of events and those who wait for their opponents to move first and then reactively counter during moments of vulnerability. Great champions have come from both styles so it’s not profitable to argue over which is better - the only interesting question is which is better for YOU and UNDER WHAT CIRCUMSTANCES? Your personal disposition/personality will be a big determining factor in whether you go the proactive or reactive route. It is also important to consider the match and the opponent. Longer matches often favor a reactive approach, shorter matches generally favor a proactive style. In training, experiment with both. You’ll often find against certain opponents one style gets better results than the other. When you can play both styles you can become a very versatile player who matches well against any opponent and in any circumstances

When you’re under attack, your first priority will always be to stop the attack - but the second you’ve accomplished that, your mind should already be mapping out potential counterattacks. Don’t be satisfied with stopping an attack - that’s good - but go from good to great by immediately putting your mindset back into offense. Remember always that a failed attack inevitably puts an opponent into an extended, vulnerable position and that a counterattack has a great chance of breaking through. The second you stopped the attack and have begun working your way free, it’s time to start plotting your counterattack to take the action back to the other guy!

A small number of moves packaged into a cohesive group that compliment each other will always outperform a vast number of moves that bear no relation to each other. Your ability to integrate moves means far more than your ability to acquire them.

Grip with a purpose: All Jiu jitsu technique requires some kind of grip upon your opponent. So the first positive action of any exchange is the act of gripping. Most people come out to grip with a “let’s see what happens” approach. This is acceptable in a relaxed class setting, but when it’s time to play your A game against a tough opponent you’ll need to do better than this. Learn to correlate your grips to your purpose. Every move you can make is associated with a specific grip. Have a clear picture of what technique you want to open with and fight for the specific grips that will be required for that technique. This will give your gripping game an immediate sense of direction and purpose that will lead immediately into attacks and move you towards an attacking game overall.

Whenever you can use an attack to load your opponents body into a position where his weight is concentrated on a single post of support - it will be a simple matter to complete the technique simply by attacking that remaining post. Learn to maneuver your opponents on to loaded position like this and the sweeps and takedowns become considerably easier. As an opponent posts out in response to your initial attack and recovers his balance - don’t see that a failed technique - see it as a lead in to the simple act of eliminating the remaining post and the second move will topple him every time.

Getting behind an opponent is the single most positive move you can make in Jiu jitsu. There are very very few scenarios where getting behind someone works against you and a tremendous multitude of scenarios where it’s among the best things you can do. Seek the back at all times and you’ll always have a positive and winning sense of direction in your game!

Reading your opponent: When you meet a stranger for the first time you immediately begin reading them based on what you see and the experience over your lifetime that form your beliefs about people and their behavior. You should do the same thing in Jiu jitsu. When you first come to grips with an opponent their look, their behavior, their demeanor - all give clues as to what their intentions, skills and attributes are. Whenever we interact with someone we consider it advantageous to know as much about them as possible. The same holds true for in Jiu jitsu. Rather than just come out gunning for whatever moves you planned to do, spend some time reading your opponent and gathering information - it often results in a more efficient path forward

If you compare your progress to other people in the dojo around you, you’ll always be miserable, since there will always be others who progress more quickly than you or began before you. If you compare yourself to where you were yesterday, you’ll always be happy, since with a just a little work it always possible to make yourself a little better today than you were yesterday.

We are all vertebrates: The human body, like all vertebrates, is essentially a skull connected to a spine and everything else hangs off that. That means that whoever controls the head controls pretty much everything else. Whenever you can gain head control in meaningful ways it’s a pathway to score. However, you can’t recklessly reach for the head as it’s typically well defended and over extension can be severely punished in this game. The main barrier to the head in Jiu jitsu is the legs - the guard position is designed expressly to keep your head as far from an opponent as possible when in bottom position and create a barrier with the legs from which many strong attacks can be launched. Once you get past at least one of your opponents legs however, the head can become a target. Keep your eyes looking for this simple formula - beat the leg(s) and get to the head - much of the control game is based on this simple insight

Tactile warnings: When it comes to physical threats the first warning we usually get comes from our eyes. We SEE the encroaching threat and then react. In Jiu jitsu you are almost always in physical contact with your opponent once a match starts. This means that the first warning you will receive is something you FEEL in a tactile way. When you have hands on an opponent you can literally feel a sudden change in muscular tension that signals the onset of an attack. The problem is that we carry our usual approach of trying to SEE threats instead of FEEL them. Next time you’re grappling try to improve your ability to read an opponent through tactile sensation rather than visually. The closer you get and the more physical contact you have with an opponent, the more you’ll feel attacks coming rather than SEE then coming. Vision is more important when distance increases and contact diminishes, but when you’re in close proximity with heavy contact - and that describes most Jiu jitsu scenarios - use that contact to read your opponent rather than your eyes

When you use the power of your hips and legs you draw upon your greatest potential for strength and endurance. If you can predominantly utilize your hips and legs against an opponent who is predominantly using his arms and shoulders, you will almost always prevail in a long match. Human nature and instinct has us fight primarily with our arms and shoulders, but sound training makes us fight primarily with our legs and hips. This is one of the clearest distinctions between beginner and expert in Jiu jitsu.

As soon as you stop an attack, your mind must make a switch from defensive mode to attack mode. Failed offense almost always puts your opponent in an extended vulnerable position and consequently, much easier to attack than he would be under normal conditions. Never be satisfied with stopping and attack - make sure you exploit your defensive success with offensive follow up whenever possible

I am very happy to announce the release of my latest instructional video THE FASTEST WAY TO BECOME EFFECTIVE IN STANDING POSITION
with @bjj.fanatics As with all the videos in the FASTEST WAY series, the emphasis is on a simplification of a skills in a given area of Jiu jitsu. The idea is to focus on a smaller number very high percentage techniques that have proven easier to learn for most students yet still shown to be highly effective at the highest levels. This allows students to make rapid progress in a an area that may have previously been difficult or even just outright absent for them. In this video we look at a simple but very efficient program for standing position - takedowns and takedown defense that can be learned and assimilated in a short time to transform your ability. You will often see my students make use of the techniques seen in this video. It enabled many of them to greatly increase their effectiveness in standing grappling in a remarkably short time frame despite having a very limited background in this skill previously. I am sure it can help your progress too! Link in my Instagram story!

Once you get a good position the two primary demands are first, that you KEEP it, second, that you EXPLOIT it. Don’t try to exploit it until you feel confident that you can keep it. Maintaining ground takes priority over gaining ground in most cases. Remember that you are in a sport where loss of good position usually creates a deeper loss than the possible gain of advancing to a new position or submission attempt. Obviously you want to avoid excessive caution and timidity, but even more obviously you want to avoid recklessness. In general positional recklessness tends to be a worse vice in Jiu jitsu than excessive positional cautiousness

When you focus a big percentage of your bodies strength against a small percentage of a vulnerable point of your opponents body - then you can defeat bigger and stronger opponents. It doesn’t matter if an opponent is twice as strong as you overall, if you can fight 10% of his strength with 80% of your strength over a battle ground so vital to his ability to continue using his strength that if you win, he will not be able to continue the match. That is how smaller, weaker people defeat bigger, stronger people. It is always about concentration of force into a vulnerable area that if taken, the opponent cannot recover.

Victory in Dallas! Nicholas Meregali had another tremendous victory against three time ADCC medalist Vagner Rocha here in Dallas tonight at the @flograppling WNO event. The match began in standing position with both athletes looking to secure top position. Mr Meregali was able to control the gripping exchanges and take it to the mat with a fine arm drag. Once on the floor he was able to use his incredible top pressure and back control to pass and pin from mount and back. Mr Rocha showed great resistance and courage under fire but Mr Meregali showed his tactical depth by switching to arm bar (juji gatame) and taking a fine submission win against one of the toughest athletes in grappling. This is the fourth time in succession he has won via submission against an ADCC medalist - an incredible win streak. A recent addition to the team is youngster @evanleve_bjj who won his WNO debut after a powerful heel hook attack and showed the early results of his hard training. If you can create a threat you can create a reaction. If you can keep an opponent in a reactive state - you lead the dance. If you can lead the dance long enough, it’ll be extremely difficult for an opponent hold you off from victory. When an opponent feels no threat he approaches you very differently - full of confidence and looking to initiate offense. When he is in the midst of a threatening attack his entire mindset changes and his actions will reflect this. Learn to create a viable threat from all positions and you’ll be able to lead the dance to victory

Getting ready for the show! Nicholas Meregali has trained extremely well for another very tough match up against the great Vagner Rocha this Friday night in Dallas TX at the @flograppling WNO grappling show. In his last three matches Mr Meregali has come up against three ADCC champions and won all three by dominating submission. Now he is matched against a three time ADCC medalist. Mr Rocha has always been among our toughest opponents and is a fearless competitor who takes on the toughest matches without hesitation. This will be a very good headline match amongst many other great matches that evening - keep your eyes open for this one! It’ll be a great show!

Jiu jitsu often mirrors life. In everyday life, any time you’re engaged in any given project, a good start makes for an easier consolidation and conclusion. So too in Jiu Jitsu. Every time you engage with an opponent, if you begin well, you’ll find it easier and have to perform less work to take things through to a successful conclusion. A bad start means a lot more work has to be done and if your opponent has a higher skill level than you, often means the beginning of the end for you. So when you first come out to grips, whether standing or on the ground, make sure you’ve got a plan in place to protect yourself from attack and work your way reliably to an advantageous grip. Victory comes at the end of the match, but who gets there is very often determined by who consistently gets off to a good start.

Neutral position: Positions in Jiu jitsu are always understood in terms of dominant, inferior and neutral. Most of our time in sparring is spent in neutral positions since it’s not easy to get dominant positions on someone your own size and skill level. The whole game and its scoring system is built around this understanding of position. Understand always however, that an apparently neutral position (insofar as it is a non scoring position) can be rendered advantageous at any time by a skilled grappler. In every neutral position there are myriad subtle changes in balance, grip, inside/outside control etc that can make a position that is ostensibly neutral quite heavily weighted in your favor. Doing this won’t result in a score - but it will definitely give you a greater ability to go forward into a score or submission in your next move. Every time you’re in a neutral position, look to explore small changes in head position, hand position, grip, balance and stance that make your ability to control your opponent greater than his ability to control you. Remember - just because it SCORES neutral doesn’t mean it has to BE neutral - even a small advantage is still an advantage and oftentimes that’s enough to swing a tight match

The old cliche of a chain being only as strong as its weakest link applies very well to Jiu jitsu. Your game is a complex conglomeration of skill sets. Those skill sets are never all equally developed. Some will be better than others. If you are to be defeated by an opponent, the most likely avenue of defeat (especially when you train repeatedly with that person or have one very long match) will be the weakest of your defensive skill sets. The solution is to be honest about your weaknesses - you can’t fix a problem until you admit there is a problem. Learn to assess yourself honestly and accurately - this is usually quite easy in jiu jitsu since repeated defeats in a pattern make it clear where the problem lies. Start putting extra time into reinforcing that weak skill set. Time spent removing a known weakness in your game is far more profitable than time spent on some new move that you happen to be recently interested in.

Attrition: Jiu jitsu is all about exercising control that leads into submission holds. The submission holds create a threat of damaged joints or unconsciousness - that threat is what makes opponents surrender. There is however, a different kind of surrender that isn’t signaled by tapping the mat, but which happens inside the mind of an opponent. Instead of creating one big instantaneous threat via submission hold, the idea is to create a constant pressure and extreme physical discomfort over time that saps an opponents will to fight. This creates submission in the mind of an opponent - a feeling that it would be better to stop the suffering than to go on. Once this has been established, the opponent will willingly submit to the first submission hold you apply. Building pressure and discomfort over time breaks opponents mentally and once the mind is broken the body will surrender soon afterwards.

Did you stop because your opponent made you stop or because you stopped yourself? Jiu jitsu is a game where victory in any given exchange is usually taken by the last one to keep moving. If one athlete stops and the other one keeps moving, the fellow who stops loses. Now sometimes a good opponent just flat out MAKES us stop with a submission or a great pin, but in the majority of cases if we look honestly we will see that we stopped ourselves when we felt tired, frustrated or just ran out of ideas. Next time you’re in an exchange with a tough and equally matched opponent, be sure to see how long you can prolong the action - it’s almost always longer than you think you can - and recognize that if if you can prolong it longer than the other fellow - you win.

Jiu jitsu and metaphor: The history and practice of martial arts is often linked to metaphor. The movements of a given style are often modeled or understood in terms of an animal or element that represents key themes of that style. So for example, the imagery of water is often used as a metaphor for various martial arts as it has properties that exemplify important themes of many arts. Other times you will see animals, snakes, cranes, monkeys and others used to describe and inspire the movement and mindset of a style. One of the most interesting and informative metaphors comes from the great Judo master Kyuzo Mifune. He compared Judo (and it applies equally to Jiu Jitsu) as a ball. His reasoning is very interesting. A ball can never lose its balance due to the fact that every point on its surface is equidistant from the center - it simply rolls in response to every push or pull regardless of what direction or force the push or pull is applied with. Mifune’s main concern to symbolize holding balance in standing grappling (extremely important in judo), but the ball analogy holds true for ground grappling also. A ball can bear a heavy weight and still move effectively underneath it - just as a good jiu jitsu player can round out his spine and thus move effectively under a pinning force. What metaphors do you use to enhance your understanding of Jiu jitsu or help explain it to other people? Whether it be animal, elemental or some other analogy I’m sure you find it helpful or illustrative

When you’re underneath opponents or in danger - stay compact. Over extension is bad for weight bearing and leaves limbs available to be snatched by a good opponent. The more compact you remain the more defensively responsible you’ll be and the better you’ll be able to bear an opponents weight for extended periods of time. Elbows in, knees to chest, head inside and spine rounded - you’ll feel the difference immediately

Seminar with Gordon Ryan in Dallas TX: I am very happy to teach a seminar in Texas as part of the upcoming WNO 23 Grappling show on Thursday May 9th at 6pm in Arlington Texas. The show headlines with Nicholas Meregali vs Vagner Rocha and the day before Gordon and I will run the seminar going over many of the techniques and tactics that made him the best of all time! Due to space limitations at the event center it is limited to just 70 people so early booking is recommended - I hope to see you there! Link to seminar booking in my Instagram story

Probably the single most liberating feeling you can have in Jiu jitsu comes from confidence in your escapes. If you feel that if an opponent pins you you’ll never get out, that fear will subvert everything you try. You’ll hang back when it’s time to attack for fear that if you fail you’ll be pinned and rendered helpless. In short, if you fear that any mistake will result in you being helplessly pinned, you won’t take the risks required to attack and win - you’ll shy away from every opportunity for fear of being controlled if it doesn’t work. When that fear is lifted from you by the belief that you can escape any hold at will, suddenly your attitude towards risk completely changes. Now you’re happy to take risks because the dire consequences of failure no longer apply to you. So work hard on your ability to escape. It may not be the most glamorous part of Jiu jitsu, but you’ll never play a glamour game if you shy away from risk.

Seeking perfection knowing all the time that you’ll never get there: When you see a perfectly executed technique it’s an inspiring moment. Watching a great champion perform a perfect throw or submission hold is one of the strongest motivators to improving your own performance. The relentless pursuit of perfection is the basis of all improvement for all of us. In truth however, we all know that perfection is impossible, and even just very good performance seems a long long way away when you’re beginning. So how do we stay motivated here and now when perfection is a futile quest and even average championship performance levels seem like a long long way away? Understand this - perfection is an ideal, but it’s not necessary for victory. To win you only need technique to be executed at a level to surpass the defenses of the person in front of you here and now. Use the ideal of perfection to navigate your way forward towards your goals like a guiding pole star, knowing that you’ll never reach it but inspired nonetheless; but confident within yourself that so long as you’re executing better than the guy in front of here and now you can win today.

Slow things down first - counter second: It’s great to have a strong sense of counterattack but trying to counter in the midst of a strong opponents attack can make a bad situation worse very quickly. It’s almost always a good idea to start by SLOWING THINGS DOWN - get into a position and stance that allows you to gain some control and slow your opponents movements, THEN start with the counters.

Moderate your response to the level of threat you face: Whenever you’re threatened in Jiu jitsu it’s normal to react strongly. When you first start out every move your opponent attempts feels like mortal danger. In truth however, many attacks are not nearly as threatening as they initially seem, particularly when you see them early. Good players know this and carefully measure the level of their response to the actual threat they face. This saves you a lot of energy which you will need when the real threat comes. Learning to accurately gauge the real threat level is one of the skills that typically takes longer than most to develop, so don’t despair if it doesn’t come to you quickly. A surprisingly important factor in your skill development here is your demeanor. As an attack comes, keep a calm face. Excessive tension almost always manifests in the face first - keep yourself calm there and tension will be slower to migrate around the rest of your body.

A threat anywhere creates openings everywhere: It’s difficult to attack a well fortified position. When an opponent operates behind a sound defensive stance and puts barriers in the path of your attacks it feels like you’re trying to attack a castle wall. You need one really strong attack that you feel truly confidant with. That attack doesn’t need to fully succeed - it just has to create a strong defensive reaction. That reaction will inevitably create openings in other areas that will enable you to get a breakthrough. That’s a value of having a few really strong attacks that you have great faith in. Better to know a couple of good attacks in great detail than to have a dozen mediocre attacks with which you have only a passing knowledge.

Triangles are among the best expressions of the ideal of Jiu jitsu - to use the strongest element of your body (legs and hips) against a weaker element of your opponents body (head and shoulder) in a way that can end a bout (strangulation plus joint locks). In addition, constant practice with triangles builds a dexterity and skill into your legs that has great carry over into other skill areas. Perhaps most importantly it creates a mindset within you that seeks always to make your legs rather than upper body the centerpiece of your game. The vast majority of your strength and endurance is in your legs - the more you play with that simple insight in mind, the more successful you will be

If you can make an opponent carry your weight in a mechanically inefficient manner for an extended time you can rapidly wear him down to a physical state where his skill set evaporates. The ability to enact a skill presupposes the physical condition to be able to perform it - attack the physical condition and by extension you attack the skill.

Knowing moves is obviously important, but when it’s time to beat a tough opponent it’s not the total number of moves you know that determines winner and loser - it’s typically the athlete who correctly identifies and knows the NEXT move who wins. Learning the move is never enough - you need to be able to place it in the context of the match flow you are currently in and identify it as the follow up to a prior move that has been stopped by a skilled opponent - you have to be able to identify opportunity and then execute faster than your opponent can follow you.

If you can get an opponent of equal skill level reacting to what you’re doing, then you’ve taken the lead in the decision making game - and once you take the lead in decision making it’s only a matter of time until you take the lead physically as well. The athlete who controls the set ups gets to write the narrative and he who controls the narrative usually gets to write the ending.

Arrange all your defenses in layers. When attacks come, they are not instantaneous, rather, they come by degree. There will be a set up, an entry, the attack itself and then the completion. You must layer your defense accordingly. You don’t want to overreact to a set up. At this level simple awareness and vigilance is appropriate. Your reaction to the entry is typically fairly small as the intrusion is not severe enough yet to warrant a big reaction. When the attack progresses to the main move itself, you will need a major defensive movement to counter it as now your opponent is a serious threat. If the attack penetrates and enters into completion phase, now you need to take some risks and some quite extreme defensive measures to prevent a score or submission. It’s rather Luke taking medicine. You don’t want to treat every illness as life threatening when symptoms first reveal themselves, but rather respond in measure to the severity and progression of the illness, since in many cases the cure has its own risks and problems the more extreme they become. So layer your defenses in accordance with the level of threat

Unnecessary tension never adds to skill performance and indeed, often detracts from it. A huge part of your jiu Jitsu journey is finding that intermediate level of tension that lets you apply the skill successfully against strong resistance, but doesn’t result in you fighting against your own excessive body tension. Long after you’ve learnt a given technique, you’ll still be learning how to actually apply it with the appropriate degree of physical tension/relaxation against resistance

You are engaged in a sport where the number of effective moves is quite small in relation to the total number of possible moves. That means that in the performance of the really moves that will characterize most of your time in the sport there is no end of learning. Perfection is a distant dream or ideal, never a reality. Staying fascinated and restlessly curious about those few moves over a career is the key to success

When we first learn a move in Jiu jitsu it’s usually practiced against no resistance in a cooperative drill, but when you go to perform a that move on a resisting opponent in sparring, the degree of difficulty will be determined by defensive skills and attributes of that opponent. How successful you will be is almost entirely determined by how good you are at degrading this defensive skills and attributes of the opponent BEFORE YOU EVEN GO TO APPLY THE MOVE - the move itself is an afterthought - when a move is successfully applied ninety percent of the real work is done before the move is even initiated. Make sure your training takes this fundamental insight into account.

Wow!! UFC 300 was a long time coming. The most successful MMA sports organization in history wanted to put on a grand show to show its progression from fringe martial arts experiment to a banned and vilified event into the modern era of MMA superstars and mainstream acceptance building into a cultural and sporting phenomenon. The show started in difficulty, with injuries and cancelled bouts and the worry that the event lacked star power for such a high profile event but the athletes on the card showed that notions of star power are secondary to the expression of skill and martial virtues of courage, grit and tenacity. This was unquestionably one of the greatest UFC events in history. It was as though each pairing of fighters sought to outdo those who fought before them and it built up into an incredible event. There were too many outstanding performances to discuss them all in an Instagram post, but congratulations in particular to Bo Nickal who continued his great run with a beautiful exhibition of elite level wrestling merging with his rapidly developing top control into submissions. Charles Oliveira and Arman Tsarukyan had a back and forth war with Mr Oliveira showing beautiful submission skills whilst Mr Tsarukyan replied with top control elbow striking over time to take a closely contested win. Max Holloway and Justin Gaethe had what will doubtless become one of the most memorable lightweight matches in history that began mostly as a tactical kicker versus puncher battle and rose round by round into an all out war in the closing seconds into one of the most impressive knockouts in UFC history - an incredible display. The main event went even higher as the great Brazilian kickboxer Alex Pereira took on the very dangerous Jamahal Hill and in a sequence that will become the stuff of legend, wordlessly shrugged off an accidental groin kick and answered with a hybrid lead uppercut/hook that showed why they call him “hands of stone” as the blow didn’t appear particularly powerful but its effects were characteristically devastating. Congratulations to @ufc and @ufcfightpass on 300 great events building to this peak and all the incredible athletes on the show!!

Keep an opponent under pressure long enough and fatigue will lower his ability to execute skills to a degree where moves that would never work on him at the outset of a match will work with certainty late in the match.

Let good position do the work for you: There are many times in Jiu jitsu where you have to work hard to recover from an error or gain an initial advantage. Once we start working hard we have a tendency to keep working hard and if your work rate stays too high for too long it will result in exhaustion. It’s important therefore, that once you’ve gained a good position on an opponent, that you learn to dial back the physical intensity level and let the good position you’ve gained do the work for you. Now it’s time for the other guy to do the hard work. This asymmetric work rate is what creates a situation where you can fatigue an opponent while preserving your own energy levels. This is what enables you to beat tough opponents over time. Do the hard work to gain good position, but once there let the other guy do the hard work while you lower your energy expenditure - that’s the key to making position work for you.

Having a blast teaching in Norfolk Virginia: It amazing how jiu jitsu is spreading. Even relatively small cities and communities today have multiple jiu jitsu schools. When I began jiu jitsu there was one school in New York Ciry and only a handful of major schools on the entire East Coast - now you have multiple great training options in pretty much every city in the country no matter how far removed from what are considered the classic training areas such as Southern California, Florida and New York. It’s great to see how this growth is affecting the training at grass roots level. The widespread use of video as a training supplement to the gym has really made the level rise much more uniformly around the country and indeed, around the world, than was the case in the past!

Levers and fulcrums: When people talk about increasing their strength in Jiu jitsu they usually talk in terms of some kind of supplemental physical training program they do outside of Jiu jitsu training designed to increase their raw strength. This is an excellent addition to your training regimen and often proves beneficial. However, by far the greatest addition to your functional strength in Jiu jitsu comes from precision in the placement of fulcrums and efficient use of the incredible mechanical power made possible by levers. If you want immediate increases in the amount of physical power you can create on the mat - study precision in the placement of fulcrums and levers in the application of your moves. Nothing else will have such immediate and dramatic effect

If you can get under an opponent’s center of gravity in an efficient posture to lift and move him, then you can perform magic from bottom position. The more spherical or ball shaped you can keep your body when working from your back and the more straight and upright you can be when working from your knees, the more successful you will be.

What are your special talents? Ask yourself a simple question - what actions or skills do you get the most positive results from with the least amount of effort? In Jiu jitsu it’s the same as life. There will be certain moves that garner you positive results relatively early and with less effort. TAKE NOTE OF WHAT THESE MOVES ARE - they are your very likely going to be your specialty moves that form a disproportionately important part of your game. There is no telling ahead of time what these moves will be, and it’s possible they’ll change over the course of your career - but now at least, you must learn to maximize your development and use of these moves. Sometimes we fight to learn a game that isn’t really ours - take note of YOUR talents more than other peoples - that way you’ll be playing your own game - so when certain moves just seem to come easy and create more success than usual with less effort - take note.

Learning in Jiu jitsu is always a contest between the need to learn a sufficient BREADTH of technique to ensure you can cover all the usual scenarios in which you find yourself and and to learn with sufficient DEPTH of technique to ensure that your main moves cannot be easily countered by a knowledgeable opponent. Breadth makes you defensively sound, depth makes you offensively strong. The more you focus on one, the less you can focus on the other - this is a fundamental tension that will reside in all your learning in Jiu jitsu. At the start of your learning you must focus more on breadth, since everything is new and you need to develop basic insights into a wide set of subjects just to avoid being crushed by techniques you simply don’t know about. However, as your skill level rises, you will need to shift the emphasis towards depth, as you will need more and more refined and deep technique to get past the defensive skills of better opponents. Always be aware of this dichotomy in Jiu jitsu learning and use it to order your goals and plans out into the future.

Our team had a strong showing with the outstanding Helena Crevar @helenajiujitsugirl winning gold to become one of the youngest athletes in history to qualify for the World Championships at just seventeen years of age. She won all but the final via submission and finished the final match in a mounted guillotine as time expired, an incredible performance from this amazing young athlete. Oliver Taza had a great weekend winning a silver medal to make an exceptional tally of two silver medals and a bronze in this trials run. There were many other outstanding performances from the rapidly developing junior athletes who maintained an exceptionally high submission rate throughout their matches. Thank as always to @mojassim80 for his exceptional organizational in getting this amazing show together and @adcc_official staff for all of their work in making it happen. Hope you all enjoyed the show!

Every physical action you take in jiu jitsu begins as an idea in the mind - a mental recognition of possibility - which you then act upon physically. A huge part of your physical results comes from training your mind to see possibilities first for without that recognition of possibility there will be no physical action that turns that possibility into actuality.

The path to victory lies not in what you know, but rather in what you actually DO with what you know

The scariest situations in Jiu jitsu are always those where you know you’re in trouble but you have no idea what to do. As always, knowledge is the antidote. When you what to do in a situation you’ll keep moving in a positive direction even when it’s difficult. It isn’t difficulty that’ll make you stop and quit - it’s ignorance that develops rapidly into hopelessness - that’ll stop you every time. So make the acquisition of knowledge a priority and over time turn that knowledge into physical skills through training - then that feeling of fear will seldom show itself

Once you get some form of advantage it’s time to hustle! Opportunities don’t come very often against good opponents nor do they last long - so when is presented to you or you have created it - it’s time to act! Make sure you have the ability to vary your speed in sparring. You can move slow and relaxed most of the match but when you’ve gained that momentary advantage and opportunity is there in front of you - now it’s time to switch speeds and attack with decisiveness

When your opponents stronger: It’s never easy when your opponent is bigger and stronger than you. One thing that will make things a little easier however, is to put even more emphasis than usual on getting behind your opponent. Remember always that the human body is set up to apply strength to objects in front of it and poorly adapted to applying strength to objects behind it - so when you are in a situation where you’re weaker and smaller, pt even more emphasis than usual on getting behind your opponent early and often - and that strength disadvantage will be less problematic than otherwise

I am very happy to announce the release of my latest instructional video as part of THE FASTEST WAY series. This is THE FASTEST WAY TO DEVELOP AN UNPASSABLE GUARD. Guard retention is truly one of the key foundational skills of Jiu Jitsu. It is no exaggeration to say that all of bottom game in jiu jitsu is based upon pin escapes and guard retention since these two skills are what allow you to use your legs to fight an opponent from underneath. No amount of offense from guard position will have any value until you can hold guard long enough to actually apply them. It is very difficult to win matches consistently if you’re always trying to dig yourself out of pins because an opponent passed your guard. Guard retention without a gi is significantly different than with a gi because you don’t have access to collar and sleeves and pant grips. As such it has a very different feel and pace to it. In this video I also look at guard retention against the guard passing methods made popular by my students such as bodylock passes, half guard pressure passing, toreando passes and many more, along with easy to remember principles and concepts that will guide you regardless of the passing methods being used against you. If guard retention without a gi has been an issue for you - check it out! Link in my instagram story

Execution vs continuity: There are two paths to excellence in Jiu jitsu. The first is the path of perfect execution of technique. The goal is to develop a technique to such a degree of perfection that it can’t be stopped even when your opponents expect it. The second path is that of continuity of attack. The goal is to be able to chain moves together in sequences that an opponent simply can’t keep up with, so that even even though no single execution of technique is decisive, the combination of all them overwhelms the defense of the opponent. Both methods have their advocates. In truth however, it is very rare indeed an athlete can consistently win via perfection of execution of a move. Under match conditions perfection is rare. A more realistic approach is to chain moves together. However, the cabins must be of a realistic length. If you’re trying to chain a dozen moves together this is unrealistic since a good opponent would simply break contact and disengage by the time you’d worked through that many moves. The best approach is the combination of the two. You want your execution to be good enough that forces opponents into defensive reaction that enter you into a short chain of follow up moves, usually between two and four, that are executed well enough to get the breakthrough. Anything more than four is starting to get unrealistic and also suggests that your execution is not good enough for an opponent to be escaping so many moves. So work on both improving execution and continuity of moves, and keep it realistic by keeping those chains of moves relatively short and well executed and you’ll be highly effective in sparring and competition

One threat always creates another: Any time you threaten an opponent with a move it will elicit a defensive reaction. That defensive reaction, if appropriate will stop your move - but it will open up the possibility of another type of attack. There is no defensive reaction that doesn’t create some new type of vulnerability. The question will always be whether you are knowledgeable and alert enough to see the new possibility as the old one is shut down. Learning to use this cycle of creating an initial threat to elicit a reaction and attacking in a new way against that reaction is one of the core elements of good jiu jitsu. It begins as a habit of always looking for a new possibility whenever you run into strong resistance rather than simply giving up as most people do. As the old saying goes, as one door closes another will always open - you goal is to habitually look for that new opening every time you see one closing - if you can build this habit, you will be a very difficult opponent for your training partners

Control of distance is a crucial skill in all combat sports and jiu jitsu is no exception. As a general rule the more threatening your opponent is the more you’ll want to increase or at least maintain distance between you and your opponent. Distance control is the basis of defense and a crucial part of offense as well - though in the case of offense it gets a lot more complicated as distance will have to be correlated with your choice of attack options which vary widely. Learning to use your limbs as a shield or frame to maintain distance and control an opponents ability to close distance is key to your survival and success especially in bottom position where most of your opponents will be trying to reduce distance into pins. So like a knight of old would use his shield as protection against an attacker, so too you must use your limbs as a shield to protect the space in front of you - you will need to space to move into your own attacks so protect it at the earliest opportunity

I’m excited to teach alongside @gordonlovesjiujitsu at the West Coast ADCC Trials. We will run a seminar on Saturday March 30 from 6-9pm after the first day of competition at the Westgate Hotel. We will go over many of the core themes my students use in competition and how they can be of benefit to your development. Hope to see you there! Link in my Instagram story

The better a move starts, the better it will finish: It is very difficult to recover from a poor start when applying moving moves in Jiu jitsu. This is because the second you start your move you’ve showed your hand so to speak, and your opponent will immediately begin his counters. If your initial entry into the move is faulty it almost always gets worse over time rather than better just because your attempts at repair are done against active resistance. There is a need therefore, to enter into your moves with authority and precision to make it more likely that you’ll be able to follow through against the anticipated resistance - so make sure you know exactly what you want to do and build the skills to do it quickly and reliably under pressure - a strong start will maximize your chance of successful completion but a mediocre start will almost certainly condemn you to failure no matter what attempt at patching things up you make

If you can keep an opponent in a reactive mindset where he is only responding to your attacks rather than initiating his own attacks, it will be difficult for him to survive. Good opponents can defend single attacks well and even a short chain of attacks, but no one can hold off continual well executed attacks over extended periods of time. The outcome of most matches is usually determined by the athlete who initiates most of the action. Gain the initiative and keep it and you’ll experience success much more often than not

One of the fundamental skills of bottom position in Jiu jitsu will always that of getting deep under your opponents center of gravity. If you can reliably do this you will have an easy time moving even heavy opponents on top of you. Once you can move them, then you can sweep and submit them. Our natural instinct when underneath heavy opponents is to get out from under them - there’s nothing wrong with that - but the best outcomes usually come from getting FURTHER UNDERNEATH THEM so that you can maintain the contact required for sweeps and submission

Develop your strangles: Strangles are the most effective and versatile submissions in Jiu jitsu. They enable you to take out even the toughest opponents. Understand that that there are many options to choose from when it comes to strangles, but there are three main scenarios in which you must be effective. The first is chest to back in front of your opponent - front headlock type situations. The second is chest to back when you’re behind an opponent - some form of rear strangle. The third is chest to chest in front of your opponent. You must have at least one effective strangle that you have great confidence in for each of these three scenarios (and preferably more than one). This is your minimum requirement for your strangle program - make sure you satisfy it!

From the moment you approach your opponent it begins. Approach combat in the manner of a man climbing a rope or ladder. You don’t just lunge from wherever you are to the top of the ladder. You work your way up step by step. So too in Jiu jitsu. Work your way up the body to gain ever increasing amounts of control. From grip to off balancing to getting past the opponents legs into pins and then to submission. Don’t get discouraged if you slide down the ladder periodically - this is to be expected - just get right back to climbing. Each time you repeat the process your opponent will be a little more tired and the climb will get a little easier. Every time you try to miss a step and jump higher rather than climb higher you increase the likelihood of falling to the bottom again, so generally favor step by step rather than bounding ahead. This is the mindset of high percentage jiu jitsu

The earlier you start, the better you do: So often we approach lackadaisically toward an opponent and get to grips before we start making decisions as to what we are going to do. This is fine if you’re sparring some well beneath your level or if you’re in a more relaxed training mode, but in a more competitive situation you must start before you even begin contact. Even as you approach your opponent you should be scanning for openings and formulating a plan of attack. This way your first contact is made with a sense of DIRECTION AND PURPOSE - the hallmark of all effective action, including Jiu jitsu.

The safest place to be in a fight or a grappling match is behind your opponent. Ironically it’s also the place you are most dangerous to your opponent - so it’s a happy mix of safest for you, most dangerous for your opponent - that’s the ideal of Jiu jitsu.

Negating strength: The single best way for you to negate a strength disadvantage is to get behind your opponent. The human body is set up to exert strength to objects in front of it, but is poorly adapted to apply strength to objects behind it. This simple insight must be the foundation of your game when going against much stronger opponents. When faced with a much stronger opponent put your primary emphasis upon getting behind them early and often.

Know inside you what you want - but make the other fellow believe you want something else: If you have an opponent who knows ahead of time what your real intentions are before you act upon them, it will be extremely difficult to score even a single move upon him. If you can mask your intentions well you will score regularly. However, if you can DECEIVE an opponent into thinking you want one thing when in fact it’s another, you will score very often indeed. Make a habit of misdirecting an opponent but moving initially towards a fake goal and then swiftly changing direction towards your real goal and you’ll find success far more often than not

If you can sweep away an opponents base of support you will be in total control until he recovers that base - that recovery period is a goldmine of attacking opportunity. Make this simple insight one of the pillars of your game - attack the opponents base of support and attack in the recovery period - follow this protocol and you’ll have a lot of success

Be patient with your strangles: A common problem that afflicts many beginners in Jiu jitsu is a tendency to clamp on a strangle - squeeze as tight as they can and then release when it doesn’t take effect immediately. Remember, strangles take a little time, usually somewhere between five to ten seconds depending on how well set the strangle is and how skilled the opponent’s resistance is. Avoid starting with maximum physical intensity - that the power of your strangle can only diminish over time. You want the power of your strangle squeeze to INCREASE over time, not diminish - so start with enough tension to hold your position and increase the tension over time to build to a peak as the strangle starts to have its effect, rather the start with everyone you’ve got and weaken just as victory is in front of you. Here, talented youngster @davis_asare locks in a tight strangle but maintains discipline with body tension to make sure the power of the strangle increases over time rather than diminishes.

Know the tipping point: Every submission in Jiu jitsu has a tipping point - a threshold beyond which it simply won’t work. We’ve all seen those fellows who persist in working for a submission long after that tipping point has been breached. This is unprofitable work. When you know where that tipping point is, then you can use it as a marker for your next attack. If you play it well you can use it to gain the initiative in that crucial moment where the submission is lost and the pursuit of the next move begins - the athlete who starts that second move first has an advantage. The better your knowledge of where a move ends and the tipping point has been reached - the better you’ll be at getting into the follow up move ahead of your opponents

Stopping a move versus countering a move: When you get attacked by a move it can be intimidating. Initially we are happy just to stop the move and see this a triumph. As you progress you must try to go beyond this and see an opponents attack as an opportunity to strongly counter. Remember - AN OPPONENT IS NEVER MORE VULNERABLE THAN WHEN CAUGHT AT THE END OF A FAILED ATTACK. Train yourself to go beyond merely stopping attacks and see the possibility of countering as an opponent struggles to get back into position. Make sure you really do stop the attack first - don’t get into Hail Mary situations where you attack as he is attacking and hope your one works first. Stop the attack, gain a measure of control, then counter

Wisdom of the old West: The great American Lawman Wyatt Earp was asked about his proficiency in gun fighting at the end of his career. “Fast is fine but accuracy is final. You have to learn to take your time in a hurry. “ These words pertain well to our game as well. The two crucial themes they convey are first that the only moves that count are those that are completed. An arm bar that is 99% good enough doesn’t score and doesn’t finish a tough opponent. Only when it fully breaks through an opponent’s defenses does it end the match. Learning to get it right under pressure is everything. Second, don’t rush things under pressure. Learning to slow things down to a speed where you can do the job correctly under pressure is key. Every instinct in your body will be telling you to speed things up, but the athlete who can resist this and move at a pace that allows solid execution will be the one who prevails.

When you put hands on an opponent you’re always taking a risk - make it worth it: Any time you go to reach for an opponent you have to sacrifice defensive responsibility to some degree. A good opponent can take advantage of that - so make sure you make the risk worthwhile. What is the benefit of putting hands on the other fellow? When you come to grips you can MOVE him and BREAK HIS STANCE/BALANCE - that’s the reward for your risk. So make sure you never just complacently reach out and put hands on an opponent - if you’re going to grip - grip with a PURPOSE. Immediately get him moving or off balance or out of position - make sure for every risk you take there is a potential reward that justifies it
