An event featuring any two of these athletes would be good - watching all of them on one show going head to head will be epic!

If you’re going to bear an opponent’s weight - make sure your structure is sound. The fastest way to get exhausted in a match is to carry an opponent’s weight in a mechanically inefficient way. So whenever you have to carry weight - your first step is to get into a position or structure that enables you to carry it efficiently. This will vary according to the scenario. How you carry an opponent’s weight will vary greatly based on whether you’re working in a pin, a takedown, from bottom guard position etc. whichever scenario you’re in, there are efficient ways to carry weight and inefficient - make it your habit to quickly work your way into efficient physical structure so that you don’t exhaust yourself, and as always - spend as little time as possible in a match carrying an opponents weight anywhere other than on your legs and hips

Setting traps: You are engaged in a sport where your opponent knows most of the moves that you know and also knows the counters to most of those moves. When you reflect on this it can be disconcerting. If this is the case - how the hell does anyone ever win? Well there are many factors but one which is very important is that of setting traps. If opponents have a good sense of awareness of potential danger (they know the same moves you know) then often you have to give them a reason to take a risk. You can for example,
feign vulnerability and this will motivate an opponent to commit to an attack that he might not otherwise have tried. If you present too much danger to a knowledgeable opponent he will always back away and be very difficult to attack, but when you deliberately offer some kind of opportunity or opening - now he will commit in ways that you can exploit. Make sure your game isn’t always about aggressively chasing your opponent down. Add some subtlety to game. Learn to draw opponents in with subterfuge and suddenly opponents who always seemed so difficult to engage with become much easier to defeat

Jiu jitsu meets everyday life: I am certain everyone who reads this has had two life experiences. The first is that of walking along comfortably but perhaps focused on something else and then tripping over an unseen obstacle such as a curb and stumbling to the ground. The second is that of walking and taking a step on to a slippery surface such as ice and once again falling to the ground. You can create both of these everyday effects in Jiu jitsu through the use of your feet. Catching your opponent mid step with your foot recreates the curb trip. Sweeping his foot just prior to it making contact with the mat recreates the icy floor. Much of what seems like complex technique in Jiu jitsu is just a recreation of fairly simple ideas we have been familiar with since childhood set in another context

Pathways: There are goals that you want in a match - an opponents back, control of both legs, front head and arm - whatever the goal, there will be a pathway to it that you’ll have to navigate to get to that goal. For example, there are many pathways to the opponents back - one of the best is to beat your opponents elbow. An opponents elbow in good position is a closed door - if you can catch it out of position it’s an open pathway to the back. The body is full of such pathways and doors - learning to identity them and exploit them is a key to your progress

Nobody ever solved a puzzle better by getting emotional - jiu jitsu is just dynamic puzzle solving under time limits and physical stress - stay calm and detached and you’ll solve problems better.

I will be teaching a no gi Jiu jitsu seminar alongside Gordon Ryan at the Arnold Classic Fitness Expo in Columbus Ohio on March 3. The Arnold Classic has always been a huge yearly event with many different types of physical fitness activities represented. Jiu jitsu has always had a strong presence there so I’m really looking forward to teaching this three hour seminar! Hope to see you there!

The way of all progress: There is an odd element to making progress in Jiu jitsu and indeed, in life in general. Progress almost always entails the learning of new skills. Once those new skills are operating at a satisfactory level, they add to your game in ways that are beneficial, offering new paths to your goals. However, there is inevitably a time in which you are trying to develop those skills where your performance will actually DIMINISH. This is caused by the simple fact that skills take time to develop. As you devote more time to them, inevitably attention must be taken away from your main previous skills so they will slightly retrograde. This leaves you with a temporarily diminished skill set - the exact opposite of what you were hoping for! Don’t be discouraged. As the old saying goes - sometimes you have to take a step back to take two steps forward. ALMOST EVERY FUTURE PERFORMANCE INCREASE IS ON THE OTHER SIDE OF A TEMPORARY PERFORMANCE DECREASE - you have to get over the mental hurdle of temporary valleys to get to the next peak - it’s simply part of the process.

Strangleholds are the KO punch of grappling. They can end a match at any time regardless of the physical intensity and mental toughness of your opponent. You can choose your favorite submissions but make sure at least one of them is a strangle that you can employ from anywhere on anyone. Even better, have a solid strangle from front and back, top and bottom. It will be a huge benefit to your game overall.

Control is control: Much is made of the difference between gi and no gi Jiu jitsu. There are definitely some major differences in feel, technique and tactics when you switch between the two, nonetheless there are more similarities than differences. Ultimately Jiu jitsu is about control of the human body - not the cloth around it - accordingly the major principles and concepts of Jiu jitsu remain the same for both - only the details change.

Keep attacking their balance and breaking their stance and every subsequent attack will be easier. An athlete in balance and with a set stance is like a walled castle - but break their balance and stance and those walls crumble leaving everything else open to attack.

When you work in combinations the first attack has to either be real or indistinguishable from a real attack from the opponent’s perspective. Attacking in combinations is a key element of progress in Jiu jitsu. It’s unlikely your first attack will work every time on skilled opponents and in the vast majority of cases you’ll need to strong moves together so the second, third or fourth move gets the break through. When athletes start to attack in combination it’s easy to subconsciously go easy on the first move because you know inside your head that you’re banking on the second one being the real attack. However, when you do this the opponent will simply not react to the weak threat of a noncommittal first attack and the whole combination you had mapped out in your head will fail before it even begins. So make sure you put some pep in that opening move - even when you’re not expecting it to break through - all the subsequent move depend on it!

How do you know a move is good for you? Think about your everyday life. A simple test to assess what you are naturally gifted at is to ask - what activity do you get the best results with the least effort? Whatever is your answer is your natural gift in life. So to with your jiu jitsu. There are some moves that just seem to come more easily to you than others and you get good results with noticeably less effort than most other moves. Make a note of these moves - they can become your main weapons. You will find that you make easier and faster progress with them and in time they can define your game.

It’s difficult to enter into attacks through the front door on a skilled opponent. Most of the time you’ll need an angle to enter. As soon as you make contact with your opponent, start looking to get away from direct frontal alignment and out towards an angle. Most of the major attacks are great facilitated by this. Think always about getting outside your opponent’s arms or perpendicular whilst underneath and suddenly all those closed doors that shut down your attacks start to open

If you can limit an opponents hip movement whilst also shutting down his feet - much of the danger presented by the guard position is immediately negated. Understand where the danger comes from and you can attack it at its source.

Use your pins to create discomfort and you’ll create stronger defensive reactions upon which you can capitalize. A comfortable and relaxed opponent can problems solve and plan, an opponent suffering strong pains and duress will only be concerned with how to stop the suffering with no mind for how to get out and counterattack. Make your pins a source of great hardship for your opponents and you’ll soon observe the link between pinning and submissions

When you’re in a winning position have the presence of mind to consider what your opponents counters will be - remember it’s not only about what you want to do, but about what you want to do in response to what your opponent will be doing. It’s so easy to only only focus on what you want to do when it’s seems close to over, but the better your opponents are, the less likely it is that it’ll be over without some last minute resistance/counters - so even as you’re putting your finishing plans into operation, be mindful of what a good opponent might do and be ready to move accordingly - it’s never over until it’s over

Jiu jitsu always asks us to seek advantage before attacking. There are many different forms of advantage. They all have their good and bad points but they’re all much better than just attacking when you feel like it. Train your mind to attack in two stages - preliminary advantage first, the actual attack second. It’s much easier to penetrate a partially broken defense than a prepared defense. Study the relevant forms of advantage - positional, timing, balance/kuzushi, grip - the list is long but the spirit is always the same. Develop some favorites and apply them prior to your attacks and you’ll soon find that defenses that used to seem impenetrable to you are suddenly something you can reliably breach

Everything your opponent does from top position or standing position presupposes a working base of support - if you can successfully attack that - you attack his entire game. Make his base/stance your first target of attack and any other target you fancy will be attainable.

Your head is the basis of top pressure: When you’re on top of an opponent and looking to create forward pressure lead with your head. If you’re shy with your head , you’ll never create any real pressure, so lead with your head and let the rest of your body follow to create match winning pressure.

If you have a game that is specialized into one or two favorite positions your ability to win will be determined mostly by your ability to force an opponent into those positions. If you can’t, it’s only a matter of time until opponents figure out that they only have to play a tactical game and stay away from those few positions you favor in order to defeat you. This gives you two options. First, you can become so good at forcing the action towards your favorite positions that very few can stop you doing so. Second, you can develop a more complete game that enables you to create winning pressure in all the major positions so that an opponent has no safe zones to retreat into. The first option generally takes less time to develop but is also easier to counter. The second is a longer term solution but will result in a deeper game that even the best opponents will struggle to deal with. Understand that even taking the second option you’ll still have your favorite positions and moves, but they’ll be embedded in a more complete game overall.

The mount is a frustrating pin for many beginners in jiu jitsu. You are told on your first day that it is the ultimate positional goal of Jiu jitsu and that it scores the highest (along with rear mount) of all the point scoring moves - but when you first start getting there you often find that it can feel rather unstable compared with other pins from side and north south positions. This often motivates students to shy away from mount for fear of being reversed and to instead stay in side pins. Don’t let this kind of thinking stop you from aspiring you to get to the mounted position. The more time you spend there the more stable you’ll become. You can hasten that progress by always seeking to get under one of your opponents arms and controlling his head to lock his shoulders down to the floor. There are many benefits to getting mounted besides the points. There are reasons why so many of the best pinners in the game favor it above side pins. Don’t let early failures frustrate you to a point where you neglect it as a skill - long term development is far more important than short term wins.

If you can break an opponent’s posture and control his movement all of the other elements of Jiu jitsu are easy. Success or failure will always come down to your ability to do these two things - if you can’t, all the moves of Jiu jitsu seem impossibly difficult. If you can, they can become surprisingly easy.

The better your opponent the more important your second, third or fourth move becomes: When you begin Jiu jitsu it’s hard enough just to perform even a single move well against resistance so you naturally tend to focus on executing that one move as well as you can with no thought of what comes next. As you go against better and better opponents they defend that first move very well so that there is little chance of it succeeding. That’s when you need to change your mentality and start prioritizing the second or even third or fourth moves, since that is where the breakthrough will be achieved. Making this mental transition from a kind of mental myopia worrying about only the move in front of you to having a far sighted vision that extends out two, three or four moves out into the future is a key part of your transformation from beginner to expert.

Two points of control is always better than one: The game of Jiu jitsu is always about control. If you can nail down one significant part of your opponent’s body, that will certainly be a great step towards control. It is always better however, to nail down two major parts of the body so that between the two, your opponent cannot begin to extricate himself from one because of the control from the other. Each element of control protects the other from a potential escape. Here, my legs control my training partners head while the figure four grip on the arm controls arm and shoulder, making escape significantly more difficult than if just one of the these control points was being used

Pinning limbs: Pinning the upper body body is a foundational skill in Jiu jitsu, but going the extra distance and pinning a limb as well as the torso is always a good thing when trying to go from pin to submission. Almost all the major upper body submissions benefit from pinning down an arm prior to executing the technique. Make a habit of this and you’ll soon see that pins are not just for points but make your submission game much stronger

Risk from winning position: One of the hardest things to get students doing is to take a risk from a position were they know they’ve already won the match. If you’re mounted on an opponent and cruising to victory - your mind will be asking you - why take a risk of losing that winning position? That’s totally understandable and in some cases it’s sound thinking, but as a general approach to Jiu jitsu I think it’s far better for your long term development to take a maximizing mindset that seeks not only victory but the best kind of victory - submission. That often means taking further risks in situations where risk is no longer necessary to win. Using mount as an example - the closer you keep your chest to your opponent’s chest the safer your position will be - but the less likely you’ll finish via submission since the strongest submissions from that position usually require the input of your legs and thus moving away from the safety of chest to chest positioning. Building a mindset that encourages this kind of risk taking will distinguish you as a submission hunter who seeks the highest goal of submission and who goes beyond control and towards control that leads to submission

The long trek home: Heading home from Tokyo’s Narita Airport. It’s always a mixed feeling at this airport. Tokyo is one of my absolute favorite cities in the world so I’m always sad to leave. The city and people are incredible with an incredible mix of industriousness, aesthetic sense, uniqueness and social etiquette mixed with a warrior heritage that makes this a world capital of martial arts. It was a privilege to be among the best kickboxers, grapplers and mma athletes in this great city these last seven days. In this two weeks I saw my dear Mom on her 90th birthday, met up with my jiu jitsu friends in Sydney and Gold Coast Australia and then watched Garry Tonon put on a flawless MMA Jiu jitsu performance against a champion opponent. Now it’s time to head home to the USA and see my dear students back in Austin Texas and get ready for more challenges. I’m excited to get back stateside and keep pushing this game further. Wishing you all the best wherever you are in this crazy world of ours!

When you can fight an opponents arms with your legs you will always be operating from advantage. If you feel that you are someone who usually operates under a size and strength disadvantage you would do very well to make a serious study of those jiu jitsu movements which out your leg strength versus an opponent’s arms strength - this is a big part of how smaller, weaker people can defeat bigger, stronger people. Triangles and juji gatame arm bars are obvious candidates to start your study, but there is much more than this further down the road. You can’t trust many things in life, but one thing you can always trust in is the strength and endurance of your legs - use them to your advantage whenever possible

Start small and finish big: When it comes to taking your opponents back, it almost always begins with a small movement that gets you outside an opponent’s elbow, some other small movement that creates the possibility of full back exposure - this is your crucial foot in the door - it gives you the possibility of back control but not the actuality. Then there is a big decisive movement where you take the back followed by the task of cementing back control and controlling your opponent from there. Get used to this process of creating possibility with a small but crucial movement by beating your opponents elbow or head or spinning inverted between his knees and coming out the back door and then capitalizing with a sudden big movement to the back and finish by clamping into the great new position you’ve gained. Understand that like most big events in life - it begins with a small event that creates possibilities and then you have to show the skill and confidence to make it an actuality

Triangles from top: We usually learn the classic front triangle stranglehold from bottom position first. There is a reason for this - because your back is on the ground there are no issues with balance and your legs are not bearing any weight and thus move freely and relatively easily into the triangle. As a result most people learn and practice front triangle from bottom position. Don’t let this dissuade you however, from using front triangles from top. Triangles have a unique power to let you use the strength of your legs against an opponents head and arm - giving you a power advantage even against stronger opponents. It can feel like a challenge when you start out as now you have to find balance on top of an opponent as you try to finagle your legs into position whilst using them for balance and support at the same time. Stick with it - the potential benefits of the power of leg strangles will one day more than make up for some early frustrations.

Creating openings: Even the most perfect execution of a given technique will come up short if it is applied against a perfectly executed defensive structure. Alternatively, a technique that is far from perfect will work adequately if it is applied against a very compromised defensive structure. A huge part of whether or not your techniques will work is bound up with your ability to create temporary openings in your opponents defensive structures and attack through that window of opportunity. Try to build into your game the notion of prior set up THEN execution rather than just going straight into the move. The better your opponents the more this essential two step methodology must permeate your game.

I am very happy to announce the release of my latest instructional video THE FASTEST WAY TO BECOME AN EFFECTIVE NO GI GUARD PASSER. In this video I show a program to use a relatively small set of extremely high percentage moves proven by my students to work very well at the highest levels. These are more than enough to cover all the major passing scenarios you will find yourself in and work together synergistically to enable you to pass even against very strong resistance. By narrowing the focus of moves learning time is reduced and decision making performance under pressure is improved. If you want to improve your no gi guard passing performance in minimum time - check it out! I will post links to my videos ( including this latest one) in my Instagram bio and story.

Australia bound! At the airport waiting for my plane on the long haul to Australia to see Mum (Mom) family and friends. It’s these times you remember how big the Pacific Ocean really is! Looking forward to checking out the burgeoning Aussie jiu jitsu scene! Hope you are all doing well 🤗🤗

Movement creates space - space makes moves possible - when you’re shut down and immobilized - start with movement, nothing crazy that makes a bad situation worse, just enough to create enough space to get moves working again. When everything seems difficult - MOVEMENT BEGETS MOVES

How do you measure success? Some days are better than others in the gym. There’s times you come out feeling like a champion and times you come out wondering whether you’ve ever improved at all. If you measure success too harshly you can get strongly discouraged by a run of bad sessions. What you find is that even the worst sessions have some elements of goodness in them. You don’t have to win to have a good performance. When I first began jiu jitsu my three most important training partners were ADCC medalists/champions. Every day I got destroyed in terms of overall result. I measured success in terms of how long I could last, or whether I could stop or at least delay a move that they constantly used on me. When overall victory isn’t realistic, look for little victories within an overall defeat. It’s all about progress and progress can still be excellent even when you’re losing every match. In time you’ll get better, and as you do, your measurements of success will reflect that as you set bolder expectations for your performances. Remember always that success and failure come in many forms and scales and that the two are tightly woven together. When things seem tough and results aren’t in alignment with your hopes keep this in mind and find some comfort in your little victories

If you can break an opponent’s stance and structure - everything else gets easier. Look for ways to off balance an opponent or upset his structure in ways that undermine his ability to carry weight or move freely and suddenly moves that seemed impossible to perform become very doable.

Stay in the game: There are few routes as frustrating as combat sports. Everything you do is against the skilled resistance of your sparring partners who learn all the same techniques and tactics that you do and progress at a similar rate. This means you never really feel like you’re improving because they are improving alongside you. It’s natural to feel disheartened as time passes and each sparring round feels as difficult as your first classes. Hang in there - in thirty years I’ve never seen cases of students who trained diligently over long periods of time who didn’t make significant improvements despite what you feel. Stay in the game - that’s the most fundamental demand for continued progress. Yes, there are things you can do to speed progress, but nothing can make up for the most basic demand of all for excellence - longevity in the game

Once you’ve created pressure - keep it on: Oftentimes I see students perform a fine sequence of moves and upon completion stop moving. This is understandable if you’re very tired and need to compose yourself, however, in many cases once you create an opening - keep exploiting it to see how far you can take it. Very often an opponent will be discombobulated and confused at the end of a successful offensive cycle and open to subsequent attacks before he can physically and psychologically recover. This doesn’t mean attacking frantically - just keep moving positively towards the next goal. Big openings are difficult to create on a good opponent - if you have created one - use it!!

If you can restrict the other fellows movement without limiting your own - you will win. If you connect too tightly to an opponent you will shut down his movement but at the cost of your own as well, thus giving you no real advantage. Obviously if you’re too loosely connected your opponent will simply pull away from your attacks. The happy middle ground is to tightly connect to one important part of your opponent’s body, perhaps the head/shoulders or the hips, but leave some slack elsewhere. This will enable you to restrict your opponent’s movement but leave enough slack for your own body to move freely around the opponent’s immobilized body.

Directions of force: A big part of this game is applying forces upon your opponent in a way designed to fulfill some purpose or goal. How accurately you apply that force is a big part of whether you succeed or fail in achieving that purpose or goal. Accuracy usually pertains to a target, but in the case of applying force to an opponent it also involves how accurately you apply the direction of that force. Sometimes even a minor error in the direction of force can create a big drop in performance of a given move. Oftentimes you will need to change the direction of force in response to an opponent’s movements or resistance mid move. The key is this - don’t just choose a direction and push or pull with all your might. Try to feel where beneficial changes in direction can be made as soon as you start applying force and be alert to any changes that need to be made as you and your opponent start moving. You can easily feel when you’ve been stopped - don’t just quit - look for a new direction. If you change to a new direction and you feel you’re pushing into a brick wall of resistance yiuve clearly gone in a wrong direction so change again until resistance lowers. Learn to feel where resistance increases and where it decreases and move in accordance with this - this will make your game far more efficient and rewarding

Use your head! Being told to use your head usually implies using our ability to think - that’s always good advice - but in Jiu jitsu you can often take the advice more literally and use your head for what is is - one of the best methods of generating pushing force. Learning to push with your head is crucial for success. So many of the major movements of Jiu jitsu require you to go in aggressively with your head to push an opponent flat while your arms are preoccupied with other tasks. There is so much demand for your arms to do work in this game that often your head is the only available means left to push your opponent - so use it! Take some time to develop your neck muscles and get used to carrying weight on your head and neck by bridging until you feel you can push with confidence and strength. Make sure your head is as much a part of your game as your arms and legs!

Happy New Year to you all! Wishing you a great year of Jiu jitsu in 2024 and good progress towards all your jiu jitsu goals!! 🤗🤗

The more you can use your legs to pin and trap an opponents arms in dominant positions, the more successful you’ll be entering into submissions. Pins are great for controlling opponents but remember that most of his defense when pins begins with his arms. If you can take one of his arms out of action by pinning it or trapping it, you will add greatly to the danger your pin presents to the opponent. So when you pin whether chest to chest or chest to back, look to go the extra distance and pin/trap an arm as well and you’ll be be able to do more than immobilize an opponent - you’ll finish him!

Control your opponents head and shoulders between your knees and attack his isolated limb with your arms - that’s the pattern of so many upper body submissions, particularly when applied from underneath. Get used to the idea of sliding out to an angle and bringing your knees up to your opponents head and shoulders for control - it’s a movement you’ll be using throughout your time in jiu jitsu and which will bring you many of your best successes.

Use your weight: Once you’re in top position and you’re no longer being held between your opponents knees it’s time to start using your weight to create pressure. Of course it’s easier to do this when you’re big and heavy, but I assure you - even a small person can be shockingly effective using body weight to slow and immobilize a bigger opponent if they know what they’re doing. Sixty kilograms of well applied bodyweight will greatly outperform one hundred kilograms of poorly applied bodyweight. Try to FOCUS the application of your weight. Don’t just apply it across a broad area. Have a target in mind and use your legs to direct your weight on to that target. The head and shoulders is the most desirable target but you can’t always go straight there so learn to target other areas as well. Whatever weight you are - learn to maximize its use and you’ll be a formidable threat when on top!

One of the most impressive twelve months I’ve ever seen in grappling: Nicholas Meregali continued his meteoric rise in grappling this year in a way that few would predicted. In a game that has become increasingly specialized into gi and no gi athletes he got to the absolute top in both by beating the best of the best in dominant fashion and showed a truly incredible ability to reinvent his jiu jitsu mid career and take on entirely new skills and directions in a breathtakingly short time. In a truly sensational year and Meregali defeated the two most outstanding current gi athletes, both current world champions Victor Hugo and Erich Munis. He did so by winning Pan Ams double gold almost every match by submission and the IBJJF Grand Prix, again almost every match by submission. Shockingly his preparation for these events was a few weeks in between his no gi training! Then in the no gi realm he took on four athletes who I believe most people would agree would be the three best open weight active no gi athletes outside of our team/former teammates - Kaynan Duarte, Felipe Pena and Cyborg Abreau - and defeated all three in the most impressive way possible by submission. Moreover, he did this while exhibiting A completely revamped skill set from feet to floor. This has truly been one of the most remarkable transformations I have ever seen from an athlete who already a star with a fully developed game in one domain - to take on and dominate a whole new category of the sport with an entirely new arsenal in a year whilst improving his original domain was a truly amazing feat. Now he stands at the top of both gi and no gi worlds and is the best mixed domain athlete in the world. Congratulations Nicholas - that was one hell of a year!

What a year for this man! South African jiu jitsu star Luke Griffith @luke_griffithjj had a truly outstanding year in 2023. He won the coveted Grand Slam European Championship, Pan Am Championship and World Championship at brown belt IBJJF Federation. In between that he also won ADCC European trials to punch his ticket to the 2024 ADCC World Championship in Las Vegas. In addition he won his WNO professional grappling match against the current Black Belt World Champion and perhaps most incredible of all - every single match in all these tournaments was won by submission - an incredible feat. In fact I believe no one has ever won Euro, Pans and Worlds IBJJF every match by submission at Brown Belt level before. It’s crazy to think that when he first arrived here around a year and half to two years ago he had just lost at European ADCC trials and then lost two matches to local purple belts. From that inauspicious start he now stands very high indeed in the grappling world - a tremendous testimony to his exceptional work ethic and that no nonsense practical mindset that has made so many South African athletes successful across many sports over many generations - well done Luke!

Happy Holidays! Had a fun day training at Renzo Gracie Austin and Roka with the crew! Now it’s time to relax 😜😜 Wherever you are I hope you have a great day!

The basis of guard position will always be your ability to off balance your opponent. If you can do this all your attacks will be substantially easier and all his passing attempts substantially more difficult and risky. Learn to constantly threaten your opponents balance first and then attack second.

When you control inside position it’s tough for an opponent to apply his weight and strength on you: If you want to be someone who is difficult to pin down and control - keep your hands, elbows, head, knees and feet inside your opponents limbs. This will make it very difficult for him to control you with grip and weight. Be diligent with this - many people can maintain inside position for short bursts but only a few can maintain it throughout a match. A disciplined approach to gripping makes the rest of your skills much easier to apply

Advancing: This is a game where you rarely make immediate jumps to victory. The vast majority of your victories will be incremental. The proceed step by step, often after losing and regaining steps against fierce resistance. Train with this in mind. KNOWING THE STEPS to the next vantage point is half the battle when you’re starting out. If you want to control an opponent’s hips you’ll have to win prior battles for the feet and then the knees before you get the prize of the hips. Train the game as it really is - learn to fight a string of little battles victoriously in order to win wars.

Draining energy: Sometimes when we get a position and attempt a move it can be rather frustrating when that move doesn’t work as planned. Don’t get upset - remember that there are two games going on here simultaneously - the obvious one is to apply a move or series of moves to completion, the other is to exhaust your opponent physically and mentally. Each time an opponent resists a move from inferior positions he gets a little more exhausted. As long as you’re winning that battle it’s ok to fail with the application of some moves. Have faith that each time an opponent resists he’s drained a little more from his batteries and there will be a threshold where he won’t be able to effectively resist the next move.

Start with the extremities and work up to the core: The goal of Jiu jitsu is control. Humans are vertebrates - our spine is our central physical feature. Control over a human means control over the spine. The spine has two ends - the hips and the head - so those will be the two major points of control that you’ll want to end with. However, you can just immediately access head and hips against a skilled opponent since they will be well defended and reaching naively for them will be strongly countered. Therefore you must work your way towards them step by step. This usually means starting at the extremities of the body - the hands and feet, and working your way methodically to elbows and knees and then to hips and head. This will be the pattern you’ll follow most of the time - make a habit of it and you’ll get the control you seek with much lower risk

Everything you do in this game you do against resistance: Something you can always count on in Jiu jitsu is that any move you attempt on an opponent will be met with resistance. It is crucial therefore, that every time you go to apply a given move you pair it with an another move that goes in the OPPOSITE DIRECTION in order to take advantage of your opponent’s defensive reaction. If you apply this simple idea you will soon find that resistance can actually be exploited and used to your gain, rather than as most people experience it as a terrible barrier to success. Learn to pair all your favorite moves with another that works in the opposite direction to take advantage of defensive reactions.

Distinguished guests: One of the great pleasures of teaching Jiu jitsu is having distinguished guests and friends drop in for training. They don’t get any more distinguished than this fellow @jasonnolf Jason Nolf and @genomorellimma Geno Morelli - alumni of the great Penn State wrestling program - stopped by today and showed their wizardry on the mats. Mr Nolf showed his integration of wrestling and jiu jitsu by landing his signature Winn Dixie roll right into a kimura submission during sparring! One gets the impression watching him he could learn jiu jitsu very easily and rapidly! What a pleasure to see these guys in action!

Everyone looks good in dominant positions - the real measure of the depth of your skill set is how well you operate in bad positions and navigate back to good positions. Make sure you don’t just practice your favorite scenarios - spend an equal time on your least favorite situations because in a tough match it’s only a matter of time before you end up there.

In this game you’ve got two choices as to how you can beat an opponent - you can either develop your movement potential to a degree where you can consistently out maneuver your opponents in order to get into winning positions. This requires you to create movement that speeds the game up beyond a point that your opponent can follow and generally favors athletes with good mobility and speed. The second option is to stop or restrict an opponent’s movement. The focus is on slowing the game down in situations that favor you and this typically favors athletes with isometric strength and endurance. The first approach is all about creating movement, the second is all about denying movement. I usually find people will tend to naturally gravitate towards one or the other without being prompted or coerced. Interestingly, athletes who start with a movement based game will usually switch as they age to a movement denial game. Who do you think would be good examples of a movement based game among contemporary athletes? Who would be good examples of a movement denial game? Who would be a good example of an athlete who has both elements in their game?

It’s never enough to simply pin an opponent: In jiu jitsu pins are a means to an end - and that end is submission. In most grappling arts pins are an end - you can finish a match with them. For us, it’s a great way to control an opponent’s movement but you have to go further and isolate an opponent’s limb so that submission becomes possible. The single best way to do this for upper body submission is to get his elbows out and away from his torso. Don’t be satisfied with the pin. Always go the extra distance and work his elbows out and away from his torso so that the process of isolating a limb can begin - once you can isolate and restrain a limb - you can attack it with confidence!

New Wave standouts @giancarlobodoni and @jiujitsugiant played a prominent role in the victory - winning both their matches decisively. When your hand fighting - don’t forget your feet! We live in a hand dominant world. Most of the tasks we perform every day, from driving, typing, working, even mundane things like brushing your teeth etc are performed predominantly with the hands. We tend to carry that mindset into Jiu jitsu but his is not good. When you hand fight whether standing or on the ground - DON’T NEGLECT YOUR FEET. Use your feet to off balance and stumble your opponent whenever you can. In the midst of the hand fight - don’t neglect the foot fight! It will double your effectiveness every time!

Pressure over time conquers all: When I first met Nicholas Meregali I was immediately struck by the brilliance and of his attacks and by his general spirit of offense and decisive victory. What was absent however a sense of CONTINUITY OF ATTACK CREATING PRESSURE OVER TIME. Other things being equal Attacks in no gi Jiu jitsu usually have a lower percentage success rate than gi due to decreased friction and therefore there is a greater need for combined/continuous attacks creating a relentless pressure that exhausts opponents until resistance levels fall below a threshold where submissions work with a higher chance of success. Mr Meregali built this approach into his game over the last year to a degree that is truly extraordinary. His last two matches were testimony to this development. He took on two of the most elite grapplers in the world - Kaynan Duarte and Felipe Pena - both ADCC champions and very powerful physical specimens who we consider the best active players in the world outside of our team mates. Mr Meregali defeated both by stranglehold from dominant position after controlling every aspect of the match from feet to floor with unrelenting pressure that made submission look inevitable. I can’t think of a better example of an athlete redefining himself mid career in such impressive fashion. Let that example be your inspiration - I guarantee that focus upon methodical pressure over time will get you decisive submission victories that opponents find very difficult to stop

I discovered tonight that I am extremely short, small and weak 😂😂😂 I hope in my next life I come back looking like one of these monsters 😂😂

Every time you go into a move it’s a chance to learn a little more about it: Most people drill for numbers and think that performing a certain number of repetitions will inevitably create expertise. This is not the case. After a fairly short time you get rapidly diminishing returns on your attempt to improve. Rather, work with an active, experimental mindset that constantly asks what minor changes in angle, positioning, placement and any other relevant factors can have in the quality of your performance of that move. There is always some new nuance that you hadn’t observed before that emerges and over time a feeling of what mechanical efficiency in that position starts to feel like - then it’s all about replication of that feeling in increasingly strenuous tests leading up to full power sparring and competition.

Pressure comes from precision: We tend to think of pressure as the result of the power of our weight and muscles being applied to an opponent. But in truth, precision with positioning of your body on to the appropriate areas of your opponent’s body is far more important. You can be big and heavy but if you don’t know how place and hold your weight, a skilled opponent won’t feel much pressure and easily escape. Conversely, you can be of modest size and strength but if you know exactly how to use your weight and position it, you can feel like a truck parked on an opponents chest. Just as the effect of a boxers punch is mostly determined by its accuracy and timing rather than size and strength, so too with grappling where the exactness of placement, how well you conform to the opponents body and how you apply the weight you have determines pressure rather than your gross weight.

Strangles: There are two major categories of submission holds in Jiu jitsu - strangles and joint locks. It’s very important that you be strong in both but it’s equally important that you understand that strangles have an innate superiority over joint locks. Joint locks attack your opponents body. As such your opponent can always simply choose to sacrifice the joint and continue fighting. Strangles attack your opponents CONSCIOUSNESS by restricting blood flow to the brain. As such it is irrelevant whether they choose to endure the attack - they will simply pass out regardless. For this reason they will always have a substantial advantage over joint locks. In a high stakes match they will never let you down. You could have a great career if your only submission holds were all strangles - but you would struggle if all your submission holds were joint locks since there would always be critical times when opponents simply toughed out the lock and let their arm or leg get injured in order to preserve a lead and win. Be very sure that among your best three submission holds at least one is a strangle - there may be a day you need it!

Giancarlo Bodoni won a tightly contested match against his great rival Lucas Hulk Barbosa - this despite taking the match on just a few days notice. He used a disciplined hand fighting game to push the pace and direction of the match and win the rematch if the last ADCC gold medal match! Luke Griffith won via heel hook against ADCC semi finalist Roosevelt Sousa, showing his versatility with a game that went smoothly from standing to ground with Luke in control throughout. Helena Crevar once again showed her incredible potential defeating an ADCC trials winner and very experienced and highly ranked no gi competitor in a dominant performance - truly remarkable for a sixteen year old against such a high level opponent!
